metadata.title: "A Problem Well Stated"
core_topics: Complete challenging puzzles that make him feel smart
Pay-to-win mechanics that let other players buy advantages
Beating hard puzzles that make him feel smart
Sharing cool discoveries with his friends
I used my own judgement to make decisions
How Large Language Models Make Predictions
ai_focus: Please complete it under strict exam conditions: no use of AI tools or web search is allowed. Your results will be shared with you by your teacher.
Mandatory AI Literacy Diagnostic Test
I used AI in this lesson OR chose not to use AI
🤖  AI Literacy Reflection
Teacher Note: Facilitate the discussion on why AI can be wrong. Why Generative AI Can Be Wrong
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
An AI generates NPC dialogue that sounds natural but contradicts the game’s established story rules.
Code: K1.3 (Generative AI produces probabilistic outputs without understanding)
Code: K1.2 (AI generates outputs in response to inputs without intent or awareness)
learning_objectives: UAE Context Connection: Recognise how real-world UAE challenges connect to meaningful game-design opportunities, and select one brief that motivates you.
Understanding Design Problems: Understand what a design problem is and identify its key elements (problem, audience, purpose, constraints) in UAE-based briefs.
I identified the problem, audience, purpose, and constraints in one UAE-based design brief.
I explained why the problem matters in the UAE or to specific players.
I evaluated an AI summary or suggested ways to improve my idea.
metadata.activity_description:
Activity 1.1 – Problem in My Favourite Game

EA Sports FC 25 addresses the challenge UAE students face during extreme heat (often exceeding 40°C), when playing football outdoors becomes unsafe. The game lets them satisfy their passion for football in a virtual environment, competing with friends online or locally instead of missing out on the sport they love.

It strengthens social bonds through shared gameplay, online tournaments, and discussions about teams and players, while keeping them connected to real football leagues like the Premier League, La Liga, and Saudi Pro League.

Portfolio Entry 1 – Brief Analysis

Brief Title: Guardians of History

| Problem | Audience | Constraints | Purpose | Success Criteria |

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| Students find historical architecture education passive and struggle to understand how UAE forts functionally addressed desert survival challenges through design. | UAE middle and high school students studying local history and cultural heritage. | at least 5 minutes playtime limit Must use specific interactive tools (Rule Blocks, Sequence Rules, Dialogue Nodes) Three mandatory puzzle types with defined mechanics Requires specific environment zones and architectural features | To preserve UAE cultural heritage by teaching students how fort architecture solved real survival problems (defense, water, communication) through hands-on problem-solving. | All three puzzles completed within target time Historical records successfully "restored" (plot flags triggered) Students demonstrate understanding of fort design principles through puzzle completion |

Activity 1.2 – Keyword & Pattern Extraction

Cultural/Culture/Heritage (appears in 5/6 briefs)

Cultural heritage, cultural pride, cultural anchor, cultural event, Emirati culture, high-culture

Strategic/Problem-solving (appears in 6/6 briefs)

Strategic thinking, logic puzzles, resource management, puzzle mechanics, challenges

Architecture/Architectural/Design (appears in 4/6 briefs)

Fort architecture, architectural significance, design principles, spatial design

Insert photo/screenshot of notes (optional)

Preserving UAE heritage (forts, mosques, traditions)

Celebrating Emirati culture and values

Building connection to national identity

Activity 1.4 – Five Questions Framework

| 1.Why | 2. Who | 3.What Tools/ constraints | 4.When/ Success | 5.What if/ Consequences/ Pivot |

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

AI Extension Task (Stretch/ optional)

Summarise a heritage project designed to teach young learners about UAE culture in 100 words or fewer. Compare the AI summary with your own analysis.

Activity 1.4 – Five Questions Framework

| 1.Why | 2. Who | 3.What Tools/ constraints | 4.When/ Success | 5.What if/ Consequences/ Pivot |

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| Connects students to ancestors' strategic thinking Interactive learning creates deeper memory retention Builds pride in Emirati engineering intelligence Demonstrates sustainable design principles still relevant today | Primary users:  UAE youth Secondary stakeholders: Teachers using the game as educational tool Parents wanting children to value heritage Wider community: UAE society preserving cultural knowledge | Technical constraints: Endstar platform capabilities Time constraints: Students building during class time Resource constraints: No complex animation systems Limited NPC dialogue capacity | Success looks like: Students can explain one fort design principle after playing Positive feedback from teachers on learning outcomes Measurable criteria: Completion time tracked Puzzle success rates logged Pre/post knowledge quiz comparison | Possible consequences: Students remain disconnected from heritage Teachers continue relying on passive lectures Recovery/Pivot strategies: If too difficult: Simplify puzzles, add hints system If boring: Increase narrative drama, add time pressure If confusing: Improve tutorial, clearer objectives If culturally inaccurate: Consult historians, revise content |

AI Extension Task (Stretch/ optional)

Summarise a heritage project designed to teach young learners about UAE culture in 100 words or fewer. Compare the AI summary with your own analysis.

"Guardians of History" is an interactive educational game teaching UAE students about fort architecture through puzzle-solving. Players become preservation experts restoring Al Jahili or Al Fahidi Fort exhibits by completing challenges in defense, water management, and communication. The 5-10 minute experience demonstrates how fort design solved desert survival problems through strategic observation, resource sequencing, and historical accuracy. Using Endstar platform tools, students engage with UAE heritage actively rather than passively, building cultural pride while learning practical engineering principles that made life possible in harsh desert conditions.

Imagine growing up in a country that's transformed from desert settlements to gleaming skyscrapers in just 50 years. Your grandparents' generation knew these forts as vital survival structures, but to you, they're just old buildings on school trips. "Guardians of History" changes that. Instead of passively walking through Al Jahili Fort, you become the expert who understands why those narrow slits saved lives, how the water channels meant survival, what made these structures brilliant. In 10 minutes of gameplay, you connect with the ingenious ancestors who thrived in 50°C heat—and realize their strategic thinking runs in your heritage too.

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson

Answer in 1–2 sentences each.

Which setting made the AI most predictable? Why?Low temperature (like 0.1) made the AI most predictable because it always picks the most likely next word.

Which setting made the AI most creative or random? Why?High temperature (like 1.0) made the AI most creative because it gives more chances to pick surprising or unusual words.

Why might a game studio or product team want to control randomness in an AI model? They control randomness so the AI gives the same helpful answers every time, not strange or wrong ones.

How is your work from last lesson (patterns, insights, sequencing) similar to how an AI predicts the next token?

Both look at what came before to guess what comes next – you found patterns in player data, and AI finds patterns in words.

Insert photo/screenshot of notes (optional)

Activity 2.1 – Player Frustrations & Motivations

Frustrations in games I play:

Forced grinding just to progress the story - Like when you're actually invested in what happens next but the game won't let you continue unless you've hit level 25, so you're stuck doing side quests you don't care about for hours just to unlock the next mission

List three specific things that frustrate you:

Pay-to-win mechanics in competitive games - Nothing kills the vibe faster than getting destroyed by someone who just bought better gear or characters with their parents' credit card while you've been grinding for weeks to unlock the same stuff.

Unskippable cutscenes and tutorials that treat you like you've never held a controller - Especially in sequels or games in genres I already know. Don't make me sit through a 5-minute explanation of how to move the camera when I've been gaming for years.

Motivation That Keep Me Playing:

Actual skill progression that feels rewarding - When I can feel myself getting better at the mechanics, pulling off combos or strategies I couldn't do last week. Not just number-go-up, but genuinely improving.

List three specific things that Motivate you:

Playing with friends and the moments we create - The random chaotic stuff that happens in multiplayer, the inside jokes, clutching a win when we were about to lose, or even just the routine of hopping on together after school.

Story hooks that make me genuinely curious - When a game has mysteries or plot threads that I'm actually thinking about between play sessions, wanting to know what happens next.

Activity 2.2 – Persona Creation

Instructions: Complete your player persona by filling in the fields below. Base your answers on your research so far and on what you know about your player’s frustrations, motivations, and goals

Explorer (with Achiever tendencies)

Discover hidden stories and connections in UAE history through gameplay

Complete challenging puzzles that make him feel smart

Share cool discoveries with his friends and compete on leaderboards

Learn things about his heritage that aren't in textbooks

Games that force repetitive grinding just to unlock the next story chapter

Pay-to-win mechanics that let other players buy advantages

Overly simplified "educational" games that feel like school assignments in disguise

Unskippable tutorials that waste his time when he already understands the mechanics

Non-linear exploration where he can discover secrets at his own pace

Skill-based progression that rewards actual improvement, not just time spent

Multiplayer elements where he can share discoveries or compete with friends

Rich environmental storytelling through artifacts, locations, and interactive elements

Optional challenges that unlock deeper lore without blocking main progress

AI Draft Persona (summarise or paste key details):

"Sultan, 15, loves exploring UAE heritage sites in games. Motivated by discovering hidden historical facts and sharing them with friends. Frustrated by oversimplified educational content and pay-to-win systems."

Possible bias, stereotype, or missing detail I noticed:

Initial draft assumed all Emirati students would be primarily motivated by cultural learning alone. Didn't account for universal teenage gaming frustrations like forced grinding or the social aspects of gaming with friends after school.

How I improved my persona after checking AI:

Added realistic teenage gamer behaviors (wanting to feel skilled, playing with friends, disliking time-wasting mechanics). Balanced cultural interest with actual gaming motivations. Included the competitive/social elements that real teens care about, not just educational goals.

Activity 2.3 – Empathy Map

What bias or missing perspective did I notice?

At first, I almost made a mistake. I thought that because Sultan is Emirati, he would only care about learning UAE heritage. This is a stereotype – it makes someone just about where they are from. I forgot that he is also a normal 15-year-old gamer. Like most teens, he plays with friends, hates pay-to-win games, and wants to be treated with respect. I was thinking of him as "an Emirati student" instead of a real teenager who lives in the UAE.

How I improved my empathy map:

I looked at real teenage gamers online to see what they like and dislike. I made sure Sultan cares about heritage, but also about other things like being good at games, exploring, and playing with friends. Heritage is just one part of who he is – not everything. I also added how real teens talk ("Bro," "insane," "unfair") and things they actually do, like checking Discord and watching game videos. This made Sultan feel like a real person, not just a stereotype.

Activity 2.4 – Empathy → Design Connection

My empathy-driven design decision:

Because my player often struggles with tutorials that treat him like he is not smart, my game will test his skill level in the first few minutes and skip easy tutorials if he doesn't need them.

Since my player is motivated by hard challenges and feeling smart, the game will have an "Expert Mode" with harder puzzles that unlock special hidden stories his friends might not find.

This shows empathy because I listened to what frustrates him and designed a game that respects his skills.

Reflect: My Player's Experience(UX)

You've spent this lesson stepping into someone else's shoes. Now reflect on what you've learned about designing for real people rather than imaginary "users".

My player is motivated by:

Finding secret links between old UAE history and new mysteries

Beating hard puzzles that make him feel smart

Sharing cool discoveries with his friends

My player will stay engaged because my game will:

Let him explore freely, with each place teaching new puzzle types (so he keeps learning)

Show what his friends found and let him compete on fair leaderboards (no paying to win)

Unlock deeper stories through hard challenges, not boring repeated tasks

This means he can always find:

A secret to share so he stays the friend who "found it first"

AI Summary (copied or rewritten from your result):

What the AI missed or got wrong:

What the AI emphasized that I did NOT emphasize:

Sultan Al Mansouri is 15 years old and loves finding hidden secrets in games about UAE places.

He likes discovering new things, getting better at games, and telling his friends what he found. But he hates when games make him repeat boring tasks, let rich players win by paying money, and treat him like a little kid.

He wants games that are smart, let him win by being good (not by paying), and teach him about his culture in a fun way - not like school homework.

He plays on his phone with friends after school. He likes both competing and working together.

Sultan is 15 and loves games, but he's tired of games that either ignore his culture or make learning about it boring like school.

He's the type of player who looks everywhere for hidden surprises, then tells his friends.

What makes him angry is when games waste his time with repetitive tasks to unlock the next part, or when people can pay money to win.

He wants to feel smart and skilled, not treated like a child.

When a game makes UAE history actually fun with puzzles and exploration, he loves it—but it has to be really fun, not just a lesson pretending to be a game.

Missing feelings: The AI shared facts but missed his strong emotions—Sultan doesn't just feel "frustrated," he gets really annoyed and will delete games fast.

Missing personality: My version sounds like a real teenager who says "Bro" and "insane," not just a list of facts.

Missing the main problem: The AI didn't explain that Sultan wants to learn about his heritage but hates how games usually teach it.

Missing social details: The AI mentioned friends but didn't show how important it is for Sultan to be "the first one to find something."

Called him an "Explorer-type" gamer (using game theory words)

Said he plays on "mobile" phones

Made clear lists of what he likes vs. what he hates

Said he plays "after school"

"My AI summary got the facts right, but it didn't sound like a real teenager and missed the strong feelings and cultural details that make Sultan feel like a real person instead of just a description."

Starter: Fall in Love with the Problem

What do Kettering's and Einstein's quotes mean for game designers? Why is it more important to understand the problem than jump straight to solutions?

Think about your favourite game.

What problem does it solve for players?

Is it boredom, loneliness, lack of challenge, or something else?

Write down one problem your favourite game addresses.

Activity 1.1 – Problem in My Favourite Game

Describe the problem your favourite game solves.

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Design | 🟪 Art | 🟩 Engineering | 🟦 Go-To-Market | 🟥 Management & Production

You'll receive several Term 2 design briefs focused on UAE heritage, education, community, and innovation. Your task is to become a brief detective, hunting for five key elements.

"What's the real need behind this brief?"

"Who benefits most from solving this problem?"

"What would happen if this problem wasn't solved?"

Portfolio Entry 1 – Brief Analysis

Brief Title:____________________

| Problem | Audience | Purpose | Constraints | Success Criteria |

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Design | 🟪 Art | 🟩 Engineering | 🟦 Go-To-Market | 🟥 Management & Production

Activity 1.2 – Keyword & Pattern Extraction

Insert photo/screenshot of notes (optional)

Activity 1.3 – Five Questions Framework

| 1.Why | 2. Who | 3.What Tools/ constraints | 4.When/ Success | 5.What if/ Consequences/ Pivot |

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

Summarise a heritage project designed to teach young learners about UAE culture in 100 words or fewer. Compare the AI summary with your own analysis.

Reflect: My Design Purpose

Reflection is where learning becomes personal. Take a moment to connect emotionally with the work you've done today.

"The problem I'm most motivated to solve is creating a unique puzzle because am curious about using new tools/ rule blocks."

Be honest and specific. Your motivation might be personal experience, curiosity about a topic, or a desire to help a particular group of people. There are no wrong answers.

We'll hear 1-2 examples shared aloud if anyone would like to volunteer.

I used AI in this lesson OR chose not to use AI

I checked AI output instead of trusting it straight away

I used my own judgement to make decisions

✍️ AI Reflection (2–3 sentences) Complete the sentence starters:

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson

In this lesson, you analysed design briefs by:

sequencing information into a clear structure.

Now let’s connect this to how AI models (like ChatGPT) work.

How Large Language Models Make Predictions

LLMs don’t “think” like humans—they predict the next word (or token) in a sentence based on patterns they learned from massive datasets.

A token is a small piece of text.  It might be a full word (“spaceship”), part of a word (“approach”), or a symbol (“!”).

During every response, the AI repeatedly asks itself:

“Based on the patterns I’ve seen, what token is most likely to come next?”

🎥LLM Transformer Model Visually Explained

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson

Use this tool to see how AI predicts the next token. But before you do take a moment to understand some key LLM terminology.

Temperature (Creativity / randomness level)

Low temperature (0–0.4): AI is more predictable and sticks to safe, common words.

High temperature (0.7–1.0):AI becomes more creative, surprising, or even chaotic.

Top-K (Limits how many tokens AI can choose from)

Low top-k (e.g., k=5): AI chooses from only the top 5 most likely tokens → safer, focused.

High top-k (e.g., k=50+): AI can choose from many tokens → more variety, less predictable.

Top-P (Probability-based filtering)

AI picks from a group of tokens whose combined probability reaches “p.”

Low p (0.2–0.5): Only very likely words are allowed → stable output.

Higher p (0.8–1.0): AI can choose from a broader range → more creative, riskier output.

🎥LLM Transformer Model Visually Explained

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson

Answer in 1–2 sentences each.

Which setting made the AI most predictable? Why?

Which setting made the AI most creative or random? Why?

Why might a game studio or product team want to control randomness in an AI model?

How is your work from last lesson (patterns, insights, sequencing) similar to how an AI predicts the next token?

Insert photo/screenshot of notes (optional)

Extension / Adaptive Task – Endstar Warm-Up

If time allows — or if the class would benefit from a more hands-on, active task — this Endstar Warm-Up can be used to wrap up or replace part of the discussion. It helps maintain engagement and supports learners who respond best to practical or collaborative activities.

Purpose: Refresh Endstar skills, practise computational logic, and show how every design problem can become a prototype solution.

Choose one based on time and readiness:

Rebuild a simple trigger → door opens sequence.

Create a mini scene linked to a brief’s problem (6 props max).

The teacher should allocate a brief to each group so that all briefs get analyzed during this lesson. NOTE** The students can analyze more than their allocated brief outside of the teaching time. Each student must have analyzed at least one brief in this lesson.

Teacher Note: Model completing one quadrant as a class. Emphasise that empathy maps should be based on research or genuine insight, not stereotypes. The AI prompt (from Activity 2.2) encourages students to question their own assumptions and consider accessibility.

Teacher Note: This extension develops prompt engineering skills and critical AI literacy. Students learn that HOW you ask the question determines the quality of AI responses. Emphasise that even improved prompts require human verification and cultural sensitivity checking.Optional 5-minute extension. Use only if time allows or to support kinaesthetic learners.

Teaching note: Before starting any work please have the students complete the diagnostic here. They will repeat the same set of questions in lesson 24.

Ask students to briefly brainstorm some real-world problems they encounter and how these might be turned into game challenges. Encourage them to think about player motivation.Context: Discuss how this concept applies within the UAE's specific cultural or social contexts. (Refer to relevant UAE context link, if applicable).In game design, the most compelling experiences often stem from exploring real-world problems and designing for real players, creating challenges that resonate and deeply engage.

Display this slide immediately after the Learning Objectives. Revisit it mid-lesson to check which criteria students feel confident about. Inspectors should see learners able to recall one or more criteria in their own words. This links directly to the Product Development Cycle and IB Criterion A – Inquiring & Analysing.Links to IB Criterion A – Inquiring & Analysing.

Teacher Note: Facilitate a 3-minute class discussion before the mini-task. Collect 3-4 examples to share aloud. Emphasise that problems don't have to be serious — entertainment and fun are valid problems to solve.

Teacher should split the class into groups so that each group covers a separate brief. Jigsaw method of teaching can be used here to research and then regroup as an entire class to share their research and insights.

Teacher Note: Provide printed/digital versions of the briefs with wide margins for annotation. Model colour-coding/ numbering with one brief on the board first. Circulate to check students are identifying elements correctly.

The teacher should allocate a brief to each group so that all briefs get analyzed during this lesson. NOTE** The students can analyze more than their allocated brief outside of the teaching time. Each student must have analyzed at least one brief in this lesson.

Teacher can led the activity here by explaining pattern recognition and detection as a wider concept in the field of CS. Teacher can then assign the activity to the students to see if they can identify patterns, sequences and general insights from the briefs provided. Students can then answer in the provided section Activity

Teacher Note: Provide printed/digital briefs with wide margins for annotation. Model colour-coding with one brief on the board first. Circulate to check students are identifying elements correctly.

Teacher Note: Model completing one row of the framework as a class using a sample brief. Students then complete their own table independently. Peer-check in pairs before moving to reflection.

Use this slide for end-of-lesson self-assessment. Ask students to identify whether they achieved All, Most, or Some of today’s criteria and to record one improvement target. Inspectors should see students articulating their progress and next steps. Ask: 'What will you do differently next time?' and record responses.

Teacher Note: Explain the system settings temperature, TOP-K and Top-P allow the students to get active and change these using their own devices and user prompts.

Endless studios session, if you have three lessons per week this extra lesson can be dedicated studio lessons where students design mock up and build in Endstar

Games that force repetitive grinding just to unlock the next story chapter

Pay-to-win mechanics that let other players buy advantages

Overly simplified "educational" games that feel like school assignments in disguise

Unskippable tutorials that waste his time when he already understands the mechanics

Non-linear exploration where he can discover secrets at his own pace

Skill-based progression that rewards actual improvement, not just time spent

Multiplayer elements where he can share discoveries or compete with friends

Rich environmental storytelling through artifacts, locations, and interactive elements

Optional challenges that unlock deeper lore without blocking main progress

AI Draft Persona (summarise or paste key details):

"Sultan, 15, loves exploring UAE heritage sites in games. Motivated by discovering hidden historical facts and sharing them with friends. Frustrated by oversimplified educational content and pay-to-win systems."

Possible bias, stereotype, or missing detail I noticed:

Initial draft assumed all Emirati students would be primarily motivated by cultural learning alone. Didn't account for universal teenage gaming frustrations like forced grinding or the social aspects of gaming with friends after school.

How I improved my persona after checking AI:

Added realistic teenage gamer behaviors (wanting to feel skilled, playing with friends, disliking time-wasting mechanics). Balanced cultural interest with actual gaming motivations. Included the competitive/social elements that real teens care about, not just educational goals.

Activity 2.3 – Empathy Map

What bias or missing perspective did I notice?

At first, I almost made a mistake. I thought that because Sultan is Emirati, he would only care about learning UAE heritage. This is a stereotype – it makes someone just about where they are from. I forgot that he is also a normal 15-year-old gamer. Like most teens, he plays with friends, hates pay-to-win games, and wants to be treated with respect. I was thinking of him as "an Emirati student" instead of a real teenager who lives in the UAE.

How I improved my empathy map:

I looked at real teenage gamers online to see what they like and dislike. I made sure Sultan cares about heritage, but also about other things like being good at games, exploring, and playing with friends. Heritage is just one part of who he is – not everything. I also added how real teens talk ("Bro," "insane," "unfair") and things they actually do, like checking Discord and watching game videos. This made Sultan feel like a real person, not just a stereotype.

Activity 2.4 – Empathy → Design Connection

My empathy-driven design decision:

Because my player often struggles with tutorials that treat him like he is not smart, my game will test his skill level in the first few minutes and skip easy tutorials if he doesn't need them.

Since my player is motivated by hard challenges and feeling smart, the game will have an "Expert Mode" with harder puzzles that unlock special hidden stories his friends might not find.

This shows empathy because I listened to what frustrates him and designed a game that respects his skills.

Reflect: My Player's Experience(UX)

You've spent this lesson stepping into someone else's shoes. Now reflect on what you've learned about designing for real people rather than imaginary "users".

My player is motivated by:

Finding secret links between old UAE history and new mysteries

Beating hard puzzles that make him feel smart

Sharing cool discoveries with his friends

My player will stay engaged because my game will:

Let him explore freely, with each place teaching new puzzle types (so he keeps learning)

Show what his friends found and let him compete on fair leaderboards (no paying to win)

Unlock deeper stories through hard challenges, not boring repeated tasks

This means he can always find:

A secret to share so he stays the friend who "found it first"

AI Summary (copied or rewritten from your result):

What the AI missed or got wrong:

What the AI emphasized that I did NOT emphasize:

Sultan Al Mansouri is 15 years old and loves finding hidden secrets in games about UAE places.

He likes discovering new things, getting better at games, and telling his friends what he found. But he hates when games make him repeat boring tasks, let rich players win by paying money, and treat him like a little kid.

He wants games that are smart, let him win by being good (not by paying), and teach him about his culture in a fun way - not like school homework.

He plays on his phone with friends after school. He likes both competing and working together.

Sultan is 15 and loves games, but he's tired of games that either ignore his culture or make learning about it boring like school.

He's the type of player who looks everywhere for hidden surprises, then tells his friends.

What makes him angry is when games waste his time with repetitive tasks to unlock the next part, or when people can pay money to win.

He wants to feel smart and skilled, not treated like a child.

When a game makes UAE history actually fun with puzzles and exploration, he loves it—but it has to be really fun, not just a lesson pretending to be a game.

Missing feelings: The AI shared facts but missed his strong emotions—Sultan doesn't just feel "frustrated," he gets really annoyed and will delete games fast.

Missing personality: My version sounds like a real teenager who says "Bro" and "insane," not just a list of facts.

Missing the main problem: The AI didn't explain that Sultan wants to learn about his heritage but hates how games usually teach it.

Missing social details: The AI mentioned friends but didn't show how important it is for Sultan to be "the first one to find something."

Called him an "Explorer-type" gamer (using game theory words)

Said he plays on "mobile" phones

Made clear lists of what he likes vs. what he hates

Said he plays "after school"

"My AI summary got the facts right, but it didn't sound like a real teenager and missed the strong feelings and cultural details that make Sultan feel like a real person instead of just a description."

Think about your favourite game.

What problem does it solve for players?

✍️ AI Reflection (2–3 sentences) Complete the sentence starters:

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson

In this lesson, you analysed design briefs by:

sequencing information into a clear structure.

Now let’s connect this to how AI models (like ChatGPT) work.

How Large Language Models Make Predictions

LLMs don’t “think” like humans—they predict the next word (or token) in a sentence based on patterns they learned from massive datasets.

A token is a small piece of text.  It might be a full word (“spaceship”), part of a word (“approach”), or a symbol (“!”).

During every response, the AI repeatedly asks itself:

“Based on the patterns I’ve seen, what token is most likely to come next?”

🎥LLM Transformer Model Visually Explained

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson

Use this tool to see how AI predicts the next token. But before you do take a moment to understand some key LLM terminology.

Temperature (Creativity / randomness level)

Low temperature (0–0.4): AI is more predictable and sticks to safe, common words.

High temperature (0.7–1.0):AI becomes more creative, surprising, or even chaotic.

Top-K (Limits how many tokens AI can choose from)

Low top-k (e.g., k=5): AI chooses from only the top 5 most likely tokens → safer, focused.

High top-k (e.g., k=50+): AI can choose from many tokens → more variety, less predictable.

Top-P (Probability-based filtering)

AI picks from a group of tokens whose combined probability reaches “p.”

Low p (0.2–0.5): Only very likely words are allowed → stable output.

Higher p (0.8–1.0): AI can choose from a broader range → more creative, riskier output.

🎥LLM Transformer Model Visually Explained

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson

Answer in 1–2 sentences each.

Which setting made the AI most predictable? Why?

Which setting made the AI most creative or random? Why?

Why might a game studio or product team want to control randomness in an AI model?

How is your work from last lesson (patterns, insights, sequencing) similar to how an AI predicts the next token?

Insert photo/screenshot of notes (optional)

lessons.uae_link: This term project is grounded in the UAE’s cultural, historical, and environmental context, with design briefs inspired by local landmarks and heritage sites. Reflection Question: How can digital experiences represent UAE culture or environments in a respectful and engaging way?
success_criteria: Explain why the problem matters in the UAE context or to specific players, noting shared design values such as heritage, sustainability, or innovation.
Identify and describe a design brief's problem, audience, purpose, and constraints clearly and accurately.
How do we know it works?

CSTA Computer Science Standards (US)
3A-AP-17 – Decompose problems and define requirements prior to developing solutions.
Students break down design briefs into clear requirements and constraints before proposing any digital solutions.

UK Computer Science
Problem decomposition and logical reasoning.
Students apply computational thinking by separating complex design briefs into manageable elements such as problem, audience, and constraints.

UK Design & Technology
KS3 1d – Investigating and analysing design contexts.
Students investigate UAE-inspired design contexts and analyse user needs, design constraints, and opportunities before ideation.

IB Design
Criterion A – Inquiring and Analysing.
Students analyse a real-world design problem, identify key requirements, and justify the relevance of the problem within its context.

lessons.detailed_activities:
Activity 1.1 – Problem in My Favourite Game

EA Sports FC 25 addresses the challenge UAE students face during extreme heat (often exceeding 40°C), when playing football outdoors becomes unsafe. The game lets them satisfy their passion for football in a virtual environment, competing with friends online or locally instead of missing out on the sport they love.

It strengthens social bonds through shared gameplay, online tournaments, and discussions about teams and players, while keeping them connected to real football leagues like the Premier League, La Liga, and Saudi Pro League.

Portfolio Entry 1 – Brief Analysis

Brief Title: Guardians of History

| Problem | Audience | Constraints | Purpose | Success Criteria |

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| Students find historical architecture education passive and struggle to understand how UAE forts functionally addressed desert survival challenges through design. | UAE middle and high school students studying local history and cultural heritage. | at least 5 minutes playtime limit Must use specific interactive tools (Rule Blocks, Sequence Rules, Dialogue Nodes) Three mandatory puzzle types with defined mechanics Requires specific environment zones and architectural features | To preserve UAE cultural heritage by teaching students how fort architecture solved real survival problems (defense, water, communication) through hands-on problem-solving. | All three puzzles completed within target time Historical records successfully "restored" (plot flags triggered) Students demonstrate understanding of fort design principles through puzzle completion |

Activity 1.2 – Keyword & Pattern Extraction

Cultural/Culture/Heritage (appears in 5/6 briefs)

Cultural heritage, cultural pride, cultural anchor, cultural event, Emirati culture, high-culture

Strategic/Problem-solving (appears in 6/6 briefs)

Strategic thinking, logic puzzles, resource management, puzzle mechanics, challenges

Architecture/Architectural/Design (appears in 4/6 briefs)

Fort architecture, architectural significance, design principles, spatial design

Preserving UAE heritage (forts, mosques, traditions)

Celebrating Emirati culture and values

Building connection to national identity

| 1.Why | 2. Who | 3.What Tools/ constraints | 4.When/ Success | 5.What if/ Consequences/ Pivot |

| Connects students to ancestors' strategic thinking Interactive learning creates deeper memory retention Builds pride in Emirati engineering intelligence Demonstrates sustainable design principles still relevant today | Primary users:  UAE youth Secondary stakeholders: Teachers using the game as educational tool Parents wanting children to value heritage Wider community: UAE society preserving cultural knowledge | Technical constraints: Endstar platform capabilities Time constraints: Students building during class time Resource constraints: No complex animation systems Limited NPC dialogue capacity | Success looks like: Students can explain one fort design principle after playing Positive feedback from teachers on learning outcomes Measurable criteria: Completion time tracked Puzzle success rates logged Pre/post knowledge quiz comparison | Possible consequences: Students remain disconnected from heritage Teachers continue relying on passive lectures Recovery/Pivot strategies: If too difficult: Simplify puzzles, add hints system If boring: Increase narrative drama, add time pressure If confusing: Improve tutorial, clearer objectives If culturally inaccurate: Consult historians, revise content |

AI Extension Task (Stretch/ optional)

Summarise a heritage project designed to teach young learners about UAE culture in 100 words or fewer. Compare the AI summary with your own analysis.

"Guardians of History" is an interactive educational game teaching UAE students about fort architecture through puzzle-solving. Players become preservation experts restoring Al Jahili or Al Fahidi Fort exhibits by completing challenges in defense, water management, and communication. The 5-10 minute experience demonstrates how fort design solved desert survival problems through strategic observation, resource sequencing, and historical accuracy. Using Endstar platform tools, students engage with UAE heritage actively rather than passively, building cultural pride while learning practical engineering principles that made life possible in harsh desert conditions.

Imagine growing up in a country that's transformed from desert settlements to gleaming skyscrapers in just 50 years. Your grandparents' generation knew these forts as vital survival structures, but to you, they're just old buildings on school trips. "Guardians of History" changes that. Instead of passively walking through Al Jahili Fort, you become the expert who understands why those narrow slits saved lives, how the water channels meant survival, what made these structures brilliant. In 10 minutes of gameplay, you connect with the ingenious ancestors who thrived in 50°C heat—and realize their strategic thinking runs in your heritage too.

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson

Answer in 1–2 sentences each.

Which setting made the AI most predictable? Why?Low temperature (like 0.1) made the AI most predictable because it always picks the most likely next word.

Which setting made the AI most creative or random? Why?High temperature (like 1.0) made the AI most creative because it gives more chances to pick surprising or unusual words.

Why might a game studio or product team want to control randomness in an AI model? They control randomness so the AI gives the same helpful answers every time, not strange or wrong ones.

How is your work from last lesson (patterns, insights, sequencing) similar to how an AI predicts the next token?

Both look at what came before to guess what comes next – you found patterns in player data, and AI finds patterns in words.

Insert photo/screenshot of notes (optional)

Activity 2.1 – Player Frustrations & Motivations

Frustrations in games I play:

Forced grinding just to progress the story - Like when you're actually invested in what happens next but the game won't let you continue unless you've hit level 25, so you're stuck doing side quests you don't care about for hours just to unlock the next mission

List three specific things that frustrate you:

Pay-to-win mechanics in competitive games - Nothing kills the vibe faster than getting destroyed by someone who just bought better gear or characters with their parents' credit card while you've been grinding for weeks to unlock the same stuff.

Unskippable cutscenes and tutorials that treat you like you've never held a controller - Especially in sequels or games in genres I already know. Don't make me sit through a 5-minute explanation of how to move the camera when I've been gaming for years.

Motivation That Keep Me Playing:

Actual skill progression that feels rewarding - When I can feel myself getting better at the mechanics, pulling off combos or strategies I couldn't do last week. Not just number-go-up, but genuinely improving.

List three specific things that Motivate you:

Playing with friends and the moments we create - The random chaotic stuff that happens in multiplayer, the inside jokes, clutching a win when we were about to lose, or even just the routine of hopping on together after school.

Story hooks that make me genuinely curious - When a game has mysteries or plot threads that I'm actually thinking about between play sessions, wanting to know what happens next.

🎥LLM Transformer Model Visually Explained

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson

Use this tool to see how AI predicts the next token. But before you do take a moment to understand some key LLM terminology.

Temperature (Creativity / randomness level)

Low temperature (0–0.4): AI is more predictable and sticks to safe, common words.

High temperature (0.7–1.0):AI becomes more creative, surprising, or even chaotic.

Top-K (Limits how many tokens AI can choose from)

Low top-k (e.g., k=5): AI chooses from only the top 5 most likely tokens → safer, focused.

High top-k (e.g., k=50+): AI can choose from many tokens → more variety, less predictable.

Top-P (Probability-based filtering)

AI picks from a group of tokens whose combined probability reaches “p.”

Low p (0.2–0.5): Only very likely words are allowed → stable output.

Higher p (0.8–1.0): AI can choose from a broader range → more creative, riskier output.

🎥LLM Transformer Model Visually Explained

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson

Answer in 1–2 sentences each.

Which setting made the AI most predictable? Why?

Which setting made the AI most creative or random? Why?

Why might a game studio or product team want to control randomness in an AI model?

How is your work from last lesson (patterns, insights, sequencing) similar to how an AI predicts the next token?

“Just like we looked for patterns and structured information in the design brief, the AI also looks for patterns in the text to decide what comes next. Both processes use clues from earlier information to make the best prediction.”

Teacher Note: Facilitate the discussion on why AI can be wrong. Why Generative AI Can Be Wrong
Because AI:
predicts what sounds correct, not what is correct
fills gaps using probability, not real understanding
can misread context or skip important details
may produce confident answers that are inaccurate
This is why your human skills—pattern-spotting, insight-finding, and structuring information—are essential.
They help you decide when AI is trustworthy and when it isn’t.

Term 2

Lesson 1

‹#›

Activity 1.1 – Problem in My Favourite Game

‹#›

EA Sports FC 25 addresses the challenge UAE students face during extreme heat (often exceeding 40°C), when playing football outdoors becomes unsafe. The game lets them satisfy their passion for football in a virtual environment, competing with friends online or locally instead of missing out on the sport they love. 
It strengthens social bonds through shared gameplay, online tournaments, and discussions about teams and players, while keeping them connected to real football leagues like the Premier League, La Liga, and Saudi Pro League.

Portfolio Entry 1 – Brief Analysis

‹#›

Brief Title: Guardians of History


| Problem | Audience | Constraints | Purpose | Success Criteria |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Students find historical architecture education passive and struggle to understand how UAE forts functionally addressed desert survival challenges through design. | UAE middle and high school students studying local history and cultural heritage. | at least 5 minutes playtime limit Must use specific interactive tools (Rule Blocks, Sequence Rules, Dialogue Nodes) Three mandatory puzzle types with defined mechanics Requires specific environment zones and architectural features | To preserve UAE cultural heritage by teaching students how fort architecture solved real survival problems (defense, water, communication) through hands-on problem-solving. | All three puzzles completed within target time Historical records successfully "restored" (plot flags triggered) Students demonstrate understanding of fort design principles through puzzle completion |

Activity 1.2 – Keyword & Pattern Extraction

My keywords:

‹#›

Cultural/Culture/Heritage (appears in 5/6 briefs)
Cultural heritage, cultural pride, cultural anchor, cultural event, Emirati culture, high-culture
Strategic/Problem-solving (appears in 6/6 briefs)
Strategic thinking, logic puzzles, resource management, puzzle mechanics, challenges
Architecture/Architectural/Design (appears in 4/6 briefs)
Fort architecture, architectural significance, design principles, spatial design

Insert photo/screenshot of notes (optional)

Patterns I Identified:

Cultural Identity & Pride
Preserving UAE heritage (forts, mosques, traditions)
Celebrating Emirati culture and values
Building connection to national identity

Insert Link(optional)

‹#›


| 1.Why | 2. Who | 3.What Tools/ constraints | 4.When/ Success | 5.What if/ Consequences/ Pivot |

| Connects students to ancestors' strategic thinking Interactive learning creates deeper memory retention Builds pride in Emirati engineering intelligence Demonstrates sustainable design principles still relevant today | Primary users:  UAE youth Secondary stakeholders: Teachers using the game as educational tool Parents wanting children to value heritage Wider community: UAE society preserving cultural knowledge | Technical constraints: Endstar platform capabilities Time constraints: Students building during class time Resource constraints: No complex animation systems Limited NPC dialogue capacity | Success looks like: Students can explain one fort design principle after playing Positive feedback from teachers on learning outcomes Measurable criteria: Completion time tracked Puzzle success rates logged Pre/post knowledge quiz comparison | Possible consequences: Students remain disconnected from heritage Teachers continue relying on passive lectures Recovery/Pivot strategies: If too difficult: Simplify puzzles, add hints system If boring: Increase narrative drama, add time pressure If confusing: Improve tutorial, clearer objectives If culturally inaccurate: Consult historians, revise content |

AI Extension Task (Stretch/ optional)

Summarise a heritage project designed to teach young learners about UAE culture in 100 words or fewer. Compare the AI summary with your own analysis.

"Guardians of History" is an interactive educational game teaching UAE students about fort architecture through puzzle-solving. Players become preservation experts restoring Al Jahili or Al Fahidi Fort exhibits by completing challenges in defense, water management, and communication. The 5-10 minute experience demonstrates how fort design solved desert survival problems through strategic observation, resource sequencing, and historical accuracy. Using Endstar platform tools, students engage with UAE heritage actively rather than passively, building cultural pride while learning practical engineering principles that made life possible in harsh desert conditions.

Imagine growing up in a country that's transformed from desert settlements to gleaming skyscrapers in just 50 years. Your grandparents' generation knew these forts as vital survival structures, but to you, they're just old buildings on school trips. "Guardians of History" changes that. Instead of passively walking through Al Jahili Fort, you become the expert who understands why those narrow slits saved lives, how the water channels meant survival, what made these structures brilliant. In 10 minutes of gameplay, you connect with the ingenious ancestors who thrived in 50°C heat—and realize their strategic thinking runs in your heritage too.

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson

10 minutes (Optional)

‹#›

Compare & Reflect
Answer in 1–2 sentences each.
Which setting made the AI most predictable? Why?Low temperature (like 0.1) made the AI most predictable because it always picks the most likely next word.

Which setting made the AI most creative or random? Why?High temperature (like 1.0) made the AI most creative because it gives more chances to pick surprising or unusual words.
Why might a game studio or product team want to control randomness in an AI model? They control randomness so the AI gives the same helpful answers every time, not strange or wrong ones.
How is your work from last lesson (patterns, insights, sequencing) similar to how an AI predicts the next token?
 Both look at what came before to guess what comes next – you found patterns in player data, and AI finds patterns in words.

Insert photo/screenshot of notes (optional)

‹#›

Term 2

Lesson 2

Activity 2.1 – Player Frustrations & Motivations

‹#›

Frustrations in games I play:

Forced grinding just to progress the story - Like when you're actually invested in what happens next but the game won't let you continue unless you've hit level 25, so you're stuck doing side quests you don't care about for hours just to unlock the next mission

List three specific things that frustrate you:

Pay-to-win mechanics in competitive games - Nothing kills the vibe faster than getting destroyed by someone who just bought better gear or characters with their parents' credit card while you've been grinding for weeks to unlock the same stuff.

Unskippable cutscenes and tutorials that treat you like you've never held a controller - Especially in sequels or games in genres I already know. Don't make me sit through a 5-minute explanation of how to move the camera when I've been gaming for years.

Motivation That Keep Me Playing:

Actual skill progression that feels rewarding - When I can feel myself getting better at the mechanics, pulling off combos or strategies I couldn't do last week. Not just number-go-up, but genuinely improving.

List three specific things that Motivate you:

Playing with friends and the moments we create - The random chaotic stuff that happens in multiplayer, the inside jokes, clutching a win when we were about to lose, or even just the routine of hopping on together after school.

Story hooks that make me genuinely curious - When a game has mysteries or plot threads that I'm actually thinking about between play sessions, wanting to know what happens next.

Activity 2.2 – Persona Creation

‹#›

Name:

Sultan Al Mansouri

Instructions: Complete your player persona by filling in the fields below. Base your answers on your research so far and on what you know about your player’s frustrations, motivations, and goals

Age:

15

Player Type:

Explorer (with Achiever tendencies)

Goals:

Discover hidden stories and connections in UAE history through gameplay
Complete challenging puzzles that make him feel smart
Share cool discoveries with his friends and compete on leaderboards
Learn things about his heritage that aren't in textbooks

Frustrations:

Games that force repetitive grinding just to unlock the next story chapter
Pay-to-win mechanics that let other players buy advantages
Overly simplified "educational" games that feel like school assignments in disguise
Unskippable tutorials that waste his time when he already understands the mechanics

Desired Mechanics:

Non-linear exploration where he can discover secrets at his own pace
Skill-based progression that rewards actual improvement, not just time spent
Multiplayer elements where he can share discoveries or compete with friends
Rich environmental storytelling through artifacts, locations, and interactive elements
Optional challenges that unlock deeper lore without blocking main progress

AI Draft & Bias Check
AI Draft Persona (summarise or paste key details):
"Sultan, 15, loves exploring UAE heritage sites in games. Motivated by discovering hidden historical facts and sharing them with friends. Frustrated by oversimplified educational content and pay-to-win systems."
Possible bias, stereotype, or missing detail I noticed:
Initial draft assumed all Emirati students would be primarily motivated by cultural learning alone. Didn't account for universal teenage gaming frustrations like forced grinding or the social aspects of gaming with friends after school.
How I improved my persona after checking AI:
Added realistic teenage gamer behaviors (wanting to feel skilled, playing with friends, disliking time-wasting mechanics). Balanced cultural interest with actual gaming motivations. Included the competitive/social elements that real teens care about, not just educational goals.

Activity 2.3 – Empathy Map

‹#›

Reflection
What bias or missing perspective did I notice?
At first, I almost made a mistake. I thought that because Sultan is Emirati, he would only care about learning UAE heritage. This is a stereotype – it makes someone just about where they are from. I forgot that he is also a normal 15-year-old gamer. Like most teens, he plays with friends, hates pay-to-win games, and wants to be treated with respect. I was thinking of him as "an Emirati student" instead of a real teenager who lives in the UAE.
How I improved my empathy map:
I looked at real teenage gamers online to see what they like and dislike. I made sure Sultan cares about heritage, but also about other things like being good at games, exploring, and playing with friends. Heritage is just one part of who he is – not everything. I also added how real teens talk ("Bro," "insane," "unfair") and things they actually do, like checking Discord and watching game videos. This made Sultan feel like a real person, not just a stereotype.

Activity 2.4 – Empathy → Design Connection

‹#›

My empathy-driven design decision:

Because my player often struggles with tutorials that treat him like he is not smart, my game will test his skill level in the first few minutes and skip easy tutorials if he doesn't need them. 

Since my player is motivated by hard challenges and feeling smart, the game will have an "Expert Mode" with harder puzzles that unlock special hidden stories his friends might not find. 
This shows empathy because I listened to what frustrates him and designed a game that respects his skills.

Reflect: My Player's Experience(UX)

5 minutes

‹#›

You've spent this lesson stepping into someone else's shoes. Now reflect on what you've learned about designing for real people rather than imaginary "users".

My Reflection:

My player is motivated by:
  Finding secret links between old UAE history and new mysteries
  Beating hard puzzles that make him feel smart
  Sharing cool discoveries with his friends
My player will stay engaged because my game will:
  Let him explore freely, with each place teaching new puzzle types (so he keeps learning)
  Show what his friends found and let him compete on fair leaderboards (no paying to win)
  Unlock deeper stories through hard challenges, not boring repeated tasks
This means he can always find:
  Something new to explore
  A harder puzzle to solve
  A secret to share so he stays the friend who "found it first"

Activity– AI Mini Lesson

‹#›

Write in the boxes below:

AI Summary (copied or rewritten from your result):

My Human Summary:

What the AI missed or got wrong:

What the AI emphasized that I did NOT emphasize:

One-sentence evaluation:

Sultan Al Mansouri is 15 years old and loves finding hidden secrets in games about UAE places.
He likes discovering new things, getting better at games, and telling his friends what he found. But he hates when games make him repeat boring tasks, let rich players win by paying money, and treat him like a little kid.
He wants games that are smart, let him win by being good (not by paying), and teach him about his culture in a fun way - not like school homework.
He plays on his phone with friends after school. He likes both competing and working together.

Sultan is 15 and loves games, but he's tired of games that either ignore his culture or make learning about it boring like school.
He's the type of player who looks everywhere for hidden surprises, then tells his friends.
What makes him angry is when games waste his time with repetitive tasks to unlock the next part, or when people can pay money to win.
He wants to feel smart and skilled, not treated like a child.
When a game makes UAE history actually fun with puzzles and exploration, he loves it—but it has to be really fun, not just a lesson pretending to be a game.

Missing feelings: The AI shared facts but missed his strong emotions—Sultan doesn't just feel "frustrated," he gets really annoyed and will delete games fast.
Missing personality: My version sounds like a real teenager who says "Bro" and "insane," not just a list of facts.
Missing the main problem: The AI didn't explain that Sultan wants to learn about his heritage but hates how games usually teach it.
Missing social details: The AI mentioned friends but didn't show how important it is for Sultan to be "the first one to find something."

Called him an "Explorer-type" gamer (using game theory words)
Said he plays on "mobile" phones
Made clear lists of what he likes vs. what he hates
Said he plays "after school"

"My AI summary got the facts right, but it didn't sound like a real teenager and missed the strong feelings and cultural details that make Sultan feel like a real person instead of just a description."

Term 2

Lesson 1

‹#›

Lesson 1: "A Problem Well Stated"

"A problem well stated is a problem half solved." — Charles Kettering

Analysing Design Briefs

‹#›

"If I had an hour to solve a problem, I'd spend 55 minutes thinking about the problem and five minutes thinking about solutions." 
— Albert Einstein

Today's lesson focuses on the Define phase of the Product Development Cycle. Before we can design anything, we must truly understand the problem we're solving.

UAE Link: This term project is grounded in the UAE’s cultural, historical, and environmental context, with design briefs inspired by local landmarks and heritage sites.
Reflection Question: How can digital experiences represent UAE culture or environments in a respectful and engaging way?

Big Question / Big Picture

Game Design

‹#›

Why Does Every Great Game Start with a Problem Worth Solving?

Lesson 1: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

‹#›

UAE Context Connection

Recognise how real-world UAE challenges connect to meaningful game-design opportunities, and select one brief that motivates you.

Understanding Design Problems
Understand what a design problem is and identify its key elements (problem, audience, purpose, constraints) in UAE-based briefs.

Lesson 1: Success Criteria – A Problem Well Stated

We will know we are successful when we can …

‹#›

UAE context
Explain why the problem matters in the UAE context or to specific players, noting shared design values such as heritage, sustainability, or innovation.

Brief analysis
Identify and describe a design brief's problem, audience, purpose, and constraints clearly and accurately.

Starter: Fall in Love with the Problem

‹#›

Discussion Questions

What do Kettering's and Einstein's quotes mean for game designers? Why is it more important to understand the problem than jump straight to solutions?

Mini-Task

Think about your favourite game. 
What problem does it solve for players? 
Is it boredom, loneliness, lack of challenge, or something else? 
Write down one problem your favourite game addresses.

Activity 1.1 – Problem in My Favourite Game

‹#›

Describe the problem your favourite game solves.

🟧

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Design | 🟪 Art | 🟩 Engineering | 🟦 Go-To-Market | 🟥 Management & Production

Explore: Brief Immersion

‹#›

You'll receive several Term 2 design briefs focused on UAE heritage, education, community, and innovation. Your task is to become a brief detective, hunting for five key elements.

Prompt Questions: 
"What's the real need behind this brief?" 
"Who benefits most from solving this problem?" 
"What would happen if this problem wasn't solved?"

🟨 1. Problem

What needs solving?

🟦 2. Audience

Who is this for?

🟩 4. Purpose

Why does it matter?

3. Constraints

What limits us?

🟧 5. Success Criteria

How do we know it works?

Portfolio Entry 1 – Brief Analysis

‹#›

Brief Title:____________________


| Problem | Audience | Purpose | Constraints | Success Criteria |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  |  |  |  |  |

🟧

Skill Boxes Key: 🟧 Design | 🟪 Art | 🟩 Engineering | 🟦 Go-To-Market | 🟥 Management & Production

Explore: Brief Immersion

‹#›

Analyse: Keyword & Pattern Extraction

‹#›

Finding Common Themes
Now that you've analysed individual briefs, let's look across all of them. 
What words appear repeatedly? Heritage, community, innovation, engagement, culture, learning?

Work with your table group to identify the top five recurring keywords. Then, group them into patterns. 
For example, do several briefs focus on "preserving culture" or "connecting generations"?

Class Discussion
"What values are common across all these briefs?" 
"Why might these themes be important to the UAE?" 
"How do these patterns help us as designers?"

Activity 1.2 – Keyword & Pattern Extraction

My keywords:

‹#›

Insert photo/screenshot of notes (optional)

Patterns I Identified:

Insert Link(optional)

🟧

To design anything meaningful—whether a game, app, product, or digital experience—professionals start by interrogating the problem from multiple angles.
This framework is adapted from real industry practice and will become your “go-to toolkit” for analysing any brief you receive in this course and beyond.

01

Why is it important?

What impact will solving this have? Who cares about this problem?

02

Who does it affect?

Primary users? Secondary stakeholders? Wider community?

03

What tools limit us?

Technical constraints? Time? Resources? Platform restrictions?

04

When is it finished?

What does success look like? How will we measure it?

05

What if it fails?

What are the consequences? How might we recover or pivot?

AI Extension Prompt: "Summarise a heritage project designed to teach young learners about UAE culture in 100 words or fewer." Compare the AI summary with your own analysis.

Deepen: Five Key Questions Framework

What are the consequences? How might we recover or pivot?

Activity 1.3 – Five Questions Framework

‹#›


| 1.Why | 2. Who | 3.What Tools/ constraints | 4.When/ Success | 5.What if/ Consequences/ Pivot |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  |  |  |  |  |

AI Extension Task

Summarise a heritage project designed to teach young learners about UAE culture in 100 words or fewer. Compare the AI summary with your own analysis.

Insert AI Summary Here

Insert Your Summary Here

🟧

Reflect: My Design Purpose

‹#›

Reflection is where learning becomes personal. Take a moment to connect emotionally with the work you've done today.

Portfolio Entry Prompt
"The problem I'm most motivated to solve is creating a unique puzzle because am curious about using new tools/ rule blocks."

Be honest and specific. Your motivation might be personal experience, curiosity about a topic, or a desire to help a particular group of people. There are no wrong answers.

We'll hear 1-2 examples shared aloud if anyone would like to volunteer.

I used AI in this lesson OR chose not to use AI
I checked AI output instead of trusting it straight away
I used my own judgement to make decisions

✍️ AI Reflection (2–3 sentences) Complete the sentence starters:

Today AI helped me by…

I still needed to decide…

🤖  AI Literacy Reflection

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment

‹#›

Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

All

I identified the problem, audience, purpose, and constraints in one UAE-based design brief.

Most

I explained why the problem matters in the UAE or to specific players.

Some

I evaluated an AI summary or suggested ways to improve my idea.

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson

‹#›

In this lesson, you analysed design briefs by:
spotting patterns,
identifying insights, and
sequencing information into a clear structure.
Now let’s connect this to how AI models (like ChatGPT) work.
How Large Language Models Make Predictions
LLMs don’t “think” like humans—they predict the next word (or token) in a sentence based on patterns they learned from massive datasets.
A token is a small piece of text.  It might be a full word (“spaceship”), part of a word (“approach”), or a symbol (“!”).
During every response, the AI repeatedly asks itself:
“Based on the patterns I’ve seen, what token is most likely to come next?”
Why AI Can Be Wrong?

🎥LLM Transformer Model Visually Explained

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson

‹#›

Try the Transformer Demo
Use this tool to see how AI predicts the next token. But before you do take a moment to understand some key LLM terminology. 
Controls You Will Explore
Temperature (Creativity / randomness level)
Low temperature (0–0.4): AI is more predictable and sticks to safe, common words.
High temperature (0.7–1.0):AI becomes more creative, surprising, or even chaotic.
Top-K (Limits how many tokens AI can choose from)
Low top-k (e.g., k=5): AI chooses from only the top 5 most likely tokens → safer, focused.
High top-k (e.g., k=50+): AI can choose from many tokens → more variety, less predictable.
Top-P (Probability-based filtering)
AI picks from a group of tokens whose combined probability reaches “p.”
Low p (0.2–0.5): Only very likely words are allowed → stable output.
Higher p (0.8–1.0): AI can choose from a broader range → more creative, riskier output.

🎥LLM Transformer Model Visually Explained

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson

‹#›

Compare & Reflect
Answer in 1–2 sentences each.
Which setting made the AI most predictable? Why?
Which setting made the AI most creative or random? Why?
Why might a game studio or product team want to control randomness in an AI model?
How is your work from last lesson (patterns, insights, sequencing) similar to how an AI predicts the next token?

Insert photo/screenshot of notes (optional)

Extension / Adaptive Task – Endstar Warm-Up

‹#›

If time allows — or if the class would benefit from a more hands-on, active task — this Endstar Warm-Up can be used to wrap up or replace part of the discussion. It helps maintain engagement and supports learners who respond best to practical or collaborative activities.

Purpose: Refresh Endstar skills, practise computational logic, and show how every design problem can become a prototype solution.

Choose one based on time and readiness:

Logic Loop Challenge

Rebuild a simple trigger → door opens sequence.

Environment Sprint

Create a mini scene linked to a brief’s problem (6 props max).

Lesson 1 – Learning Check

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 1.1

An AI generates NPC dialogue that sounds natural but contradicts the game’s established story rules.

What is the most accurate explanation?

A. The AI misunderstood the theme

B. The AI prioritised linguistic probability over factual consistency

C. The AI lacked access to the design document

D. The AI chose creativity over accuracy

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: Nature of AI
Code: K1.3 (Generative AI produces probabilistic outputs without understanding)

An AI response feels convincing, but the design team cannot verify where the information came from.

Why does this require human review?

A. AI outputs are always incomplete

B. AI may fabricate plausible but incorrect details

C. AI ignores instructions

D. AI cannot summarise information

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 1.2

Lesson 1 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: Capabilities & Limits
Code: K4.1 (AI lacks context, judgment, and originality)

An AI produces an answer that is statistically likely but contextually inappropriate for the game.

This most clearly shows that AI:

A. Failed to generalise

B. Lacks situational awareness and intent

C. Has insufficient training data

D. Is biased toward creativity

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

MCQ 1.3

Lesson 1 – Learning Check

OECD Mapping
Knowledge Area: Nature of AI
Code: K1.2 (AI generates outputs in response to inputs without intent or awareness)
