Think about how your research from earlier activities could inform the dialogue or interactions you create. What did you learn about player preferences that you could apply here?
Make it relevant: Base your interaction or scene on your game’s theme or story from your brief. Use insights from your research to make the interactions feel authentic and engaging for your target audience.

headers: One research finding about your game’s setting, experience, or puzzles (what you discovered and how it could affect gameplay)
rows: Primary (Human)
Interviews, observations, user testing

My brother told me that the light in Sheikh Zayed Mosque changes from golden in the morning to blue and purple at night. I can use this idea to make a puzzle where the player fixes lights to match different times of day.
You get real emotions and personal details that websites don't have.
My brother couldn't remember exact facts like how many columns there are.
Secondary
Books, articles, videos, credible web sources, google search
I found out the mosque has 82 domes and reflecting pools in the courtyard. I could make a puzzle where the player uses the pools to reflect light into the main hall.
You get accurate facts and numbers from official sources.
It took a long time to find what I needed and some websites had different information.
AI-Based
Gemini, ChatGPT – AI summaries, pattern recognition
ChatGPT suggested puzzles using Islamic geometric patterns and gave me the idea to add an NPC guide who helps players and reminds them to be respectful.
It's really fast and gives you lots of ideas for brainstorming.
AI gives general mosque info, not specifically about Sheikh Zayed Mosque, and sometimes the facts are wrong.
Human Research
My classmate Ahmad said when he visited the mosque, the coolest part was walking through the huge courtyard and seeing the main dome appear slowly as you get closer.
Ahmad only visited once and he was more focused on taking photos than noticing small architectural details.
I can design my courtyard zone so the player slowly discovers the main hall, making the first view of the big dome feel like a special moment.
AI Research
I asked Gemini about mosque architecture and it explained that light and shadow are used on purpose in Islamic design to create a spiritual feeling.
Gemini gave general info about mosques but didn't know specific things about Sheikh Zayed Mosque like where the chandeliers are located.
My light-restoring puzzle makes more sense now because light isn't just decoration - it's actually an important part of the mosque's design and meaning.
🟨 Problem
Kind of
I know players need to restore the light, but I need to be more specific about WHY the light went out in the first place.
🟦 Audience
Yes
Players aged 13-16 who like calm puzzle games and learning about culture.
🟩 Purpose
Yes
To show how beautiful mosque architecture is and make players feel peaceful and amazed, like visiting the real place.
🩷 Constraints
Need to check
I know it has to be 5 minutes and use Endstar, but I need to remember: prayer area must stay private and blocked off.
🟧 Success Criteria
Not really

I wrote "restore the light" but I need to be clearer - like how many puzzles? What happens at the end exactly?  I need to make my Success Criteria more specific. Like "player completes 2 light puzzles and sees the final chandelier turn on" instead of just "restore the light."

headers: Player enters the courtyard and walks into a Trigger Volume near an NPC caretaker, which activates a Dialogue Node explaining that the mosque's main lights have gone out and need to be restored.
rows: 2 – Core Challenge / Linked Mechanics

Player must find and collect 3 hidden light crystals (Treasure props) scattered across two zones. A Counter Rule tracks the pickups. When all 3 are collected, a Mechanical Door opens to the main hall. Inside, player pulls a Lever to activate the final light system.
3 – Completion / Reward Event

Completion / Reward Event | When the lever is pulled, the Ambient Settings change the skybox from dark to golden sunset, and a Level Transition Rule takes the player to a "You Win" completion
screen.

teacher_notes.notes: Discussion: Ask students to think about the difference between having lots of information and having a clear plan. How do successful projects transform ideas into action? Context: Connect this to how UAE visionary projects like Dubai 2040 or UAE Vision 2071 transform research and insights into specific, measurable goals. The strongest game designs emerge when we can clearly articulate what we're building, for whom, and why it matters.
Keep the full plan version in documentation. Use these 2 grouped objectives for delivery and pacing.
Display this slide immediately after the Learning Objectives. Revisit it mid-lesson to check which criteria students feel confident about. Links to IB Criterion A – Inquiring & Analysing.

Teacher Note: Give students 2-3 minutes to review their Lesson 3 portfolio entries before the connection task. The visual should emphasize the transformation from research to action. Collect key insights to reference during brief-writing activity. *Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1a-1b | AQA 3.1.2 | IB Criterion B Developing Ideas | CSTA 3A-AP-16 (Requirements & Solutions)*
*Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1a-1b | AQA 3.1.3 | IB Criterion B | CSTA 3A-AP-16*

*Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1a-1c | AQA 3.1.2-3.1.3 | IB Criterion B Developing Ideas | CSTA 3A-AP-16 (Requirements & Solutions)* Teacher Note: Circulate to provide individual guidance on brief clarity. Emphasize that AI should assist, not replace, their own thinking. Students should critically evaluate AI suggestions rather than accepting them blindly.
*Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1a-1c | AQA 3.1.2-3.1.3 | IB Criterion B Developing Ideas | CSTA 3A-AP-16 (Requirements & Solutions)*

Teacher Note: Encourage students to provide clear, labelled images or sketches. Remind them to connect their concept board to the "Define" stage of the Product Development Cycle and explain how it shapes their game brief.

Provide Team Charter templates. Monitor to ensure all students have meaningful roles that match their interests and abilities.

*Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1d | AQA 3.1.4 | IB Criterion B Reflection* Teacher Note: Encourage students to think beyond just features to the impact their game will have. slides.content:
Term 2
Lesson 4

Lesson 4: "Rewriting the Brief: From Insights to Action" — Define Phase
Today's lesson focuses on the Define phase of the Product Development Cycle. We'll transform the research insights from Lesson 3 into clear, actionable project goals that will guide our game development.
UAE Link: This lesson reflects the importance of clear problem definition by refining ideas into focused, actionable design goals within a UAE context. Reflection Question: Why is clearly defining a problem important before developing digital solutions?

Big Question / Big Picture
Game Design
How do designers turn research into clear project goals?

Lesson 4: Learning Objectives
By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:
Form Teams with Clear Roles Form balanced teams with clear roles and timelines to support the design process.
Combine Research into a Brief Use human and AI research to personalise a Term 2 brief (Problem, Audience, Purpose, Constraints, Success Criteria).

Lesson 4: Success Criteria – From Insights to Action
We will know we are successful when we can …
Evidence & roles Team roles clearly defined in a Team Charter.
Rewrite the brief All five elements stated clearly and realistically.
Aligned to Product Development Cycle – Define Phase.

Starter: From Information to Insight
Visual Comparison Look at the contrast between messy research data and a concise, clear brief. What transforms scattered information into actionable goals? Connection Task From your Lesson 3 research, highlight one finding you discovered that should influence your game design. How might this finding change what you build or how you build it? Share your finding with a partner and explain why it's significant for your project direction.

Explore: Quick Recap – Brief Elements Check-In
Before rewriting your brief, confirm you've clearly defined every key element.
Tip : If any box is unclear, revisit your Lesson 1 notes before rewriting. These five elements must appear in your final brief.
Activity 4.1

How Game Objectives Work
Task: "Choose your chosen brief. Write one short sentence for each objective describing how your player will progress through the game."
📜 Objective 1 – Setup / Quest Giver
Player meets the museum guide who explains the lost artefact.
🧩 Objective 2 – Core Challenge / Linked Mechanics
Solve three logic puzzles to unlock the gallery door.
💎 Objective 3 – Completion / Reward Event
Return artefact to display; light animation and music play.
Activity 4.2

Analyse: Rewrite the Brief as a Design Specification (with AI Support)
Brief Rewriting Process Using your chosen brief from Lesson 1 and your research insights from Lesson 3, “Rewrite your brief into a clear design specification with all five elements defined” “This rewrite does not replace the original brief. It clarifies it for you as a designer.” Problem: Based on your research, what specific issue will your game address? Audience: Who exactly will benefit from your solution? Purpose: Why does this matter in the UAE context? Constraints: What realistic limits do you face? Success Criteria: How will you measure success?
AI Assist Prompt: "Rewrite this brief clearly for a 13-year-old in the UAE — keep purpose same, make goals measurable."
This rewrite defines what success looks like, not how the game must be designed. Your narrative, mechanics, and presentation choices remain creative and open.

Prompt Engineer Like a Pro
Use the R-E-X structure — Role , Example , Expectation — to make your AI instructions clear and creative. You can use this structure to prompt AI when refining your rewritten design specification, mechanics, and narrative ideas.
How can clear, structured prompts make your AI research and design work more accurate and creative?

Portfolio Entry 5 – Rewritten Brief
Rewrite your brief using clear, complete sentences. Include: Problem, Audience, Purpose, Constraints, and Success Criteria. Note: This rewritten brief clarifies what success looks like, not how your game must be designed. Your narrative, mechanics, and presentation choices remain creative and open.
What changed from your Lesson 1 brief?
Example Rewritten Brief (Defender of the Desert) Problem: Players need an engaging way to understand how UAE desert forts were designed for protection and survival in harsh environments. Audience: Casual puzzle and exploration game players aged 13–16 who enjoy historical or cultural settings. Purpose: Design a survival-strategy mini-game where players take the role of a fort recruit preparing a UAE desert fort for an upcoming challenge (such as a sandstorm or water shortage) using architecture, vantage points, and stored resources. Constraints: The game must be built in Endstar, include at least two zones, use one main logic challenge, include simple item collection or interaction, and provide at least 5 minutes of playable gameplay for a first-time player. Success Criteria: Players successfully prepare the fort for the challenge, demonstrate understanding of the fort’s defensive and survival features, and experience a clear sense of purpose, urgency, and achievement.
🟧🟩

What Is a Game Design Concept Board ?
A Game Design Concept Board turns your rewritten brief into early visual gameplay ideas. It helps you explore: The setting or environment your game will take place in Key objects or interactable items The main mechanic or action players will perform Early sketches, diagrams, or core loop notes Reference images that match the gameplay style, not just the mood Game designers use concept boards to communicate how the game might work and feel before building the first prototype. You created a similar visual inspiration board in Term 1 — this Concept Board repeats that skill but now focuses on gameplay ideas, mechanics, and key objects instead of just mood or themes.
Your task: Gather 3–6 visuals that represent your game idea — sketches, objects, environment references, mechanic diagrams, or simple inspiration images. These will be uploaded in Portfolio Entry 6.

Portfolio Entry 6 – Game Design Concept Board
🟧🟥
Upload Area: Upload your concept sketch, storyboard, or flowchart image here. (Optional: insert video clip or digital drawing link.)
insert video clip or digital drawing link here.

Portfolio Entry 6 Additional Upload Space (Optional)
🟧🟥
Use this slide if you need more room to upload extra sketches, references, or concept board images.
insert video clip or digital drawing link here.

Deepen: Peer Review & Team Formation
Peer Review Checklist Exchange briefs with another student and use this checklist to provide feedback: Problem is specific and solvable Audience is clearly defined Purpose connects to UAE context Constraints are realistic Success criteria are measurable
Team Charter Creation Form balanced teams of 3-4 members with defined roles: Designer: User experience and gameplay Programmer: Technical implementation Art Lead: Visual design and assets Story/Marketing: Content and communication
Create a Team Charter documenting each member's role, responsibilities, and timeline commitments for the project.

Activity 4.3 – Team Charter
Team / Game Name:
Complete this Team Charter in 5 minutes. Depending on your team size, each member may choose one role or a combination of roles to support the project.
Team Agreements (choose 1–2): Examples: communicate clearly, be kind, share work fairly, support each other, test often.
Team / Game Name (Working Title):
🟥

Reflect: My Project vision
With your brief complete and team formed, articulate your vision clearly. This reflection will become your north star throughout the project. Please avoid using language that is technical or subject specific, keep the language general so that any person investor or CEO could read this document and understand it.
"My game aims to ___________________ because ___________________; we'll know it's successful when ___________________."
This statement should be specific and inspiring. It should capture not just what you're building, but why it matters and how you'll recognize success. Your team should be able to rally around this vision.
My Reflection:
I used AI in this lesson OR chose not to use AI I checked AI output instead of trusting it straight away I used my own judgement to make decisions
✍️ AI Reflection (2–3 sentences) Complete the sentence starters:
Today AI helped me by…
I still needed to decide…
🤖 AI Literacy Reflection

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment
Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson
Specify the Output Format AI can provide more useful responses when you specify exactly how you want the information formatted. This makes the output more actionable and easier to use in your work. Instead of asking general questions, try specifying the exact format, length, or structure you need for your response.
Your Task: Try both prompts with your game concept. Which format is more helpful for your team planning? Why does structure matter?
Comparison Activity General Prompt: "Explain purpose of my game." Specific Format Prompt: "Explain purpose in exactly three bullet points (≤ 15 words each)."

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini - Lesson
Add Context to Avoid Hallucination AI "hallucination" occurs when AI generates confident-sounding but incorrect information. One way to reduce this is by providing specific context in your prompts. AI works better when you give it a clear role, specific constraints, and relevant background information. This helps it generate more accurate and useful responses.
Your Task: Try both prompts and compare the outputs. Which response is more useful for your actual project? Why?
Comparison Activity: Generic Prompt: "What are good game mechanics?" Specific Prompt: "I'm a Grade 8 student designing a puzzle game about UAE heritage in Endstar — suggest three simple mechanics."

Extension / Adaptive Task – Endstar Warm-Up
If time allows — or if the class would benefit from a more hands-on, active task — this Endstar Warm-Up connects brief-writing to practical implementation. Choose one based on time and readiness:
Purpose: Translate written goals into practical game mechanics. Test whether the brief's vision can be implemented in Endstar environment.
Logic Blueprint Create scene that represents the brief's core goal + simple trigger.

Which change would MOST directly improve output relevance?
A. Increasing response length
B. Adding task-aligned examples and constraints
C. Using more technical language
D. Increasing randomness
Lesson 4 – Learning Check
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
MCQ 4 . 1
A prompt produces vague and unfocused AI responses.
OECD Mapping Knowledge Area: Nature of AI (K1) Code: K1. 4 AI behaviour varies by task framing and purpose

What does this demonstrate?
A. AI performance depends on user skill, not intelligence
B. AI adapts based on preference
C. AI improves through repeated exposure
D. AI evaluates task difficulty
Lesson 4 – Learning Check
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
MCQ 4 . 2
Two teams use the same AI tool but receive different quality outputs because one team provides clearer constraints.
OECD Mapping Knowledge Area: Human Choices & Bias Code: K 2 . 4 Humans structure inputs that shape outputs

Why does adding constraints generally improve reproducibility in AI outputs?
A. Constraints reduce the number of possible interpretations
B. Constraints eliminate randomness
C. Constraints increase creativity
D. Constraints train the model
Lesson 4 – Learning Check
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
MCQ 4 . 3
OECD Mapping Knowledge Area: Capabilities & Limits (K4) Code: K 4 . 1 AI outputs vary due to probabilistic inference

Portfolio Entry / Next Steps
Upload your artefact evidence and write one sentence: 'Today I demonstrated…'
1
Research Comparison Table
Evaluates how different research sources influence game settings and mechanics.
2
Rewritten Brief
Refines and formalizes the project’s scope, constraints, and success criteria.
3
Endstar Prototype Evidence
Showcases a functional prototype interaction based on your research findings.
4
Game Design Concept Board
Visualizes the game's core concept through sketches, storyboards, or flowcharts.
Next Steps: These portfolio entries establish your foundation for the Ideate and Prototype phases. You'll reference your brief  throughout the design process to ensure you stay focused on your goals.
🟦🟧🟩🟪🟥

Endless Studios Session
Let’s Build. Create. Experiment Make You Choose.
Jump into Endstar Build and experiment Test with your team Fix bugs together Try new ideas Explore AI tools Choose how you want to work
Schools with 2 lessons/week: this can be self-directed learning from home. Schools with 3 lessons/week: teachers may run this as an open studio build sessions.

Purpose: Why does this matter in the UAE context? Constraints: What realistic limits do you face? Success Criteria: How will you measure success?
AI Assist Prompt: "Rewrite this brief clearly for a 13-year-old in the UAE — keep purpose same, make goals measurable."
This rewrite defines what success looks like, not how the game must be designed. Your narrative, mechanics, and presentation choices remain creative and open.

Prompt Engineer Like a Pro
Use the R-E-X structure — Role , Example , Expectation — to make your AI instructions clear and creative. You can use this structure to prompt AI when refining your rewritten design specification, mechanics, and narrative ideas.
How can clear, structured prompts make your AI research and design work more accurate and creative?

Portfolio Entry 5 – Rewritten Brief
Rewrite your brief using clear, complete sentences. Include: Problem, Audience, Purpose, Constraints, and Success Criteria. Note: This rewritten brief clarifies what success looks like, not how your game must be designed. Your narrative, mechanics, and presentation choices remain creative and open.
What changed from your Lesson 1 brief?
Example Rewritten Brief (Defender of the Desert) Problem: Players need an engaging way to understand how UAE desert forts were designed for protection and survival in harsh environments. Audience: Casual puzzle and exploration game players aged 13–16 who enjoy historical or cultural settings. Purpose: Design a survival-strategy mini-game where players take the role of a fort recruit preparing a UAE desert fort for an upcoming challenge (such as a sandstorm or water shortage) using architecture, vantage points, and stored resources. Constraints: The game must be built in Endstar, include at least two zones, use one main logic challenge, include simple item collection or interaction, and provide at least 5 minutes of playable gameplay for a first-time player. Success Criteria: Players successfully prepare the fort for the challenge, demonstrate understanding of the fort’s defensive and survival features, and experience a clear sense of purpose, urgency, and achievement.
🟧🟩

What Is a Game Design Concept Board ?
A Game Design Concept Board turns your rewritten brief into early visual gameplay ideas. It helps you explore: The setting or environment your game will take place in Key objects or interactable items The main mechanic or action players will perform Early sketches, diagrams, or core loop notes Reference images that match the gameplay style, not just the mood Game designers use concept boards to communicate how the game might work and feel before building the first prototype. You created a similar visual inspiration board in Term 1 — this Concept Board repeats that skill but now focuses on gameplay ideas, mechanics, and key objects instead of just mood or themes.
Your task: Gather 3–6 visuals that represent your game idea — sketches, objects, environment references, mechanic diagrams, or simple inspiration images. These will be uploaded in Portfolio Entry 6.

Team / Game Name:

Team / Game Name (Working Title):
🟥

Reflect: My Project vision
With your brief complete and team formed, articulate your vision clearly. This reflection will become your north star throughout the project. Please avoid using language that is technical or subject specific, keep the language general so that any person investor or CEO could read this document and understand it.
"My game aims to ___________________ because ___________________; we'll know it's successful when ___________________."
This statement should be specific and inspiring. It should capture not just what you're building, but why it matters and how you'll recognize success. Your team should be able to rally around this vision.
My Reflection:
I used AI in this lesson OR chose not to use AI I checked AI output instead of trusting it straight away I used my own judgement to make decisions

Today AI helped me by…
I still needed to decide…

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson
Specify the Output Format AI can provide more useful responses when you specify exactly how you want the information formatted. This makes the output more actionable and easier to use in your work. Instead of asking general questions, try specifying the exact format, length, or structure you need for your response.
Your Task: Try both prompts with your game concept. Which format is more helpful for your team planning? Why does structure matter?
Comparison Activity General Prompt: "Explain purpose of my game." Specific Format Prompt: "Explain purpose in exactly three bullet points (≤ 15 words each)."

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini - Lesson
Add Context to Avoid Hallucination AI "hallucination" occurs when AI generates confident-sounding but incorrect information. One way to reduce this is by providing specific context in your prompts. AI works better when you give it a clear role, specific constraints, and relevant background information. This helps it generate more accurate and useful responses.
Your Task: Try both prompts and compare the outputs. Which response is more useful for your actual project? Why?
Comparison Activity: Generic Prompt: "What are good game mechanics?" Specific Prompt: "I'm a Grade 8 student designing a puzzle game about UAE heritage in Endstar — suggest three simple mechanics."

Extension / Adaptive Task – Endstar Warm-Up
If time allows — or if the class would benefit from a more hands-on, active task — this Endstar Warm-Up connects brief-writing to practical implementation. Choose one based on time and readiness:
Purpose: Translate written goals into practical game mechanics. Test whether the brief's vision can be implemented in Endstar environment.
Logic Blueprint Create scene that represents the brief's core goal + simple trigger.

Term 2
Lesson 4

Lesson 4: "Rewriting the Brief: From Insights to Action" — Define Phase
Today's lesson focuses on the Define phase of the Product Development Cycle. We'll transform the research insights from Lesson 3 into clear, actionable project goals that will guide our game development.
UAE Link: This lesson reflects the importance of clear problem definition by refining ideas into focused, actionable design goals within a UAE context. Reflection Question: Why is clearly defining a problem important before developing digital solutions?

Big Question / Big Picture
Game Design
How do designers turn research into clear project goals?
native_slides.speaker_notes: Discussion: Ask students to think about the difference between having lots of information and having a clear plan. How do successful projects transform ideas into action? Context: Connect this to how UAE visionary projects like Dubai 2040 or UAE Vision 2071 transform research and insights into specific, measurable goals. The strongest game designs emerge when we can clearly articulate what we're building, for whom, and why it matters.

native_slides.content:

Form Teams with Clear Roles Form balanced teams with clear roles and timelines to support the design process.

metadata.activity_description:
Students identify one simple game mechanic they enjoy and explain why it feels engaging.

Activity 5.1 – Concept Generation (Assessment for Learning) Students generate three game concepts based on their brief, identifying a core mechanic, player goal, and reward loop for each.

Activity 5.2 – Select & Plan (Assessment for Learning) Students select one concept to take forward and identify the Endstar tools needed to build it.

Portfolio Entry 7 – Micro-Prototype Evidence (Assessment of Learning) Students build a simple micro-prototype in Endstar showing one core interaction and upload screenshots or a short clip with a brief reflection on what worked and what to improve next.

lessons.description_of_activities:
Students identify one simple game mechanic they enjoy and explain why it feels engaging.

Activity 5.1 – Concept Generation (Assessment for Learning) Students generate three game concepts based on their brief, identifying a core mechanic, player goal, and reward loop for each.

Activity 5.2 – Select & Plan (Assessment for Learning) Students select one concept to take forward and identify the Endstar tools needed to build it.

Portfolio Entry 7 – Micro-Prototype Evidence (Assessment of Learning) Students build a simple micro-prototype in Endstar showing one core interaction and upload screenshots or a short clip with a brief reflection on what worked and what to improve next.
lessons.big_question: How do we turn an idea into something we can actually play?
lessons.uae_link: This lesson supports innovation in the UAE by encouraging creative exploration of multiple ideas before selecting and testing a viable concept.
success_criteria: We will know we are successful when we can …
Generate concepts Produce three distinct concepts aligned to your brief.
Build micro-prototype Create a micro-prototype showing at least one core mechanic in Endstar and document peer feedback to guide your next iteration.

curriculum_alignment.text: CSTA Computer Science Standards (US) 3A-AP-17 – Decompose problems and define requirements prior to developing solutions. Students break down their brief into mechanics and goals, generating and evaluating multiple ideas before prototyping.
UK Computer Science Problem decomposition and structured planning. Students plan and structure digital solutions by selecting a viable concept to develop.
UK Design & Technology KS3 1d – Generating, developing, and communicating design ideas. Students generate, refine, and communicate design ideas through sketches and prototypes.
IB Design Criterion B – Developing Ideas. Students develop multiple ideas and justify their selected concept through early prototyping.

lessons.detailed_activities:
Students identify one simple game mechanic they enjoy and explain why it feels engaging.

Activity 5.1 – Concept Generation (Assessment for Learning) Students generate three game concepts based on their brief, identifying a core mechanic, player goal, and reward loop for each.

Activity 5.2 – Select & Plan (Assessment for Learning) Students select one concept to take forward and identify the Endstar tools needed to build it.

Portfolio Entry 7 – Micro-Prototype Evidence (Assessment of Learning) Students build a simple micro-prototype in Endstar showing one core interaction and upload screenshots or a short clip with a brief reflection on what worked and what to improve next.

Treasure (x3 crystals), Door - Mechanical, Lever
Rule Blocks
Counter Rule, Trigger Volume, Dialogue Node
NPCs
Medium NPC (caretaker character)
Display

Environment

Logic

headers: Why it Worked / What I learned
rows: Player spawned facing away from the NPC caretaker, so they walked the wrong direction and missed the dialogue completely
Moved the Trigger Volume to cover the entire spawn area and rotated the spawn point to face the NPC
Now players trigger the dialogue automatically when they spawn - I learned spawn orientation matters a lot for first impressions
Only 2 of the 3 crystals were counting. Counter showed 2/3 even after collecting all crystals
Found that Crystal #3 wasn't wired to the Counter Rule - I forgot to click "Confirm" on the wire
Always check every wire individually and click Confirm! One missing connection breaks the whole system
Door opened but there was no feedback - player didn't know it happened because they were far away collecting the last crystal
Players need feedback at the moment something happens, not just the result. Sound or text helps a lot
Added wire from Counter Rule OnCountReached → Billboard SetRawText to display "Door Unlocked!" message

Term 2

Lesson 5

Explore — Activity 5.1: Concept 1

‹#›

🟧🟥

insert video clip or digital drawing link here.

Core Mechanic: 
Player Goal: 
Reward Loop:

Collect and Count

Find All Crystals to Open the Door

Action: Player explores and finds a crystal → Feedback: Pickup sound plays, counter updates on billboard → Motivation: Player wants to find the next crystal to see what's behind the locked door

Explore: Activity 5.2: Select & Plan

5 minutes

‹#›

Review your three concepts and choose ONE to take forward into your micro-prototype. Discuss your ideas with a partner, then identify the Endstar tools you will need.

🟧🟩

Concept Selection

Peer Feedback

Concept 1 - Collect and Count

I'm choosing Concept 1 because it's the simplest to build in Endstar and shows the core gameplay clearly. Collecting crystals and unlocking a door is easy for players to understand and connects directly to my brief about restoring light to the mosque.


| Tool Type | Specific Items |
| --- | --- |
| Props | Treasure (x3 crystals), Door - Mechanical, Lever |
| Rule Blocks | Counter Rule, Trigger Volume, Dialogue Node |
| NPCs | Medium NPC (caretaker character) |
| Display | Anachronist Billboard (show crystal count) |
| Environment | Ambient Settings (skybox change for ending) |
| Logic | Level Transition Rule (win screen) |

Partner: Ahmed

  Which concept feels most engaging for players?
  Ahmed said Concept 1 feels most engaging because players get instant feedback every time they find a crystal, and the locked door makes them curious about what's behind it.

  What might be challenging to build in Endstar?
  Making sure all 3 crystal pickups are wired correctly to the same Counter Rule. Ahmed reminded me to test each wire separately.

  How does this concept connect to your brief's purpose?
  It connects because players explore the mosque zones to find the crystals, so they naturally see the courtyard and architecture while playing. The "restore the light" story gives meaning to collecting the items.

‹#›

Term 2

Lesson 6

Explore: Prototype V1 Plan

‹#›

Review Micro-PrototypeWhat worked? What broke? What felt incomplete?

Plan Implementation
Which Endstar tools will you need? What’s the build order?

Identify Core Features
List 2-3 essential features for a complete experience.

Predict Issues
What problems might players encounter?

"A moment where the player might get confused is…" when they first spawn in the courtyard and don't know they need to find crystals. There's no NPC or sign telling them the goal yet.
  "An interaction that felt unfinished or missing was…" the ending. After collecting all 3 crystals, the door opens but there's nothing in the second zone - no lever, no win screen, no skybox change.

"To make the mechanic work, I need to use the Dialogue Node tool because… players need the caretaker NPC to explain the quest at the start so they know what to do."
"My build order will be:"
  1. Add Trigger Volume + Dialogue Node near spawn for NPC introduction
  2. Build Zone 2 (main hall) with lever and wire to Ambient Settings
  3. Add Level Transition Rule for win screen when lever is pulled

"My game won't make sense unless players can…" talk to the caretaker NPC at the start and understand they need to find 3 light crystals to restore the mosque's light..
 "Players should always be able to…" see their progress (crystal count on billboard) and know where to go next (door opens when ready, lever is visible in Zone 2).

1. Players might miss the NPC - They could walk right past the caretaker without triggering the dialogue. Fix: Make the Trigger Volume bigger or place it directly in the player's path.
  2. Players might not find all 3 crystals - Some crystals might be too hidden. Fix: Add visual hints or make crystals glow/stand out more.
  3. Players might not know the door opened - If they're far away when the counter reaches 3, they won't see it. Fix: Add a sound effect or billboard message saying "Door Unlocked!"

Activity 6.2: Playtest & Debug Log

‹#›


| Screenshot (s) (Before / After if needed) | Issue Found OR “No issues found” | Fix Applied/ Improvement | Why it Worked / What I learned |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Player spawned facing away from the NPC caretaker, so they walked the wrong direction and missed the dialogue completely | Moved the Trigger Volume to cover the entire spawn area and rotated the spawn point to face the NPC | Now players trigger the dialogue automatically when they spawn - I learned spawn orientation matters a lot for first impressions |
|  | Only 2 of the 3 crystals were counting. Counter showed 2/3 even after collecting all crystals | Found that Crystal #3 wasn't wired to the Counter Rule - I forgot to click "Confirm" on the wire | Always check every wire individually and click Confirm! One missing connection breaks the whole system |
|  | Door opened but there was no feedback - player didn't know it happened because they were far away collecting the last crystal | Players need feedback at the moment something happens, not just the result. Sound or text helps a lot | Added wire from Counter Rule OnCountReached → Billboard SetRawText to display "Door Unlocked!" message |

Playtest your Prototype v1
Play through your prototype from beginning to end. Identify anything confusing, broken, or incomplete.
If everything works, record what worked well and one improvement you plan for v2.
Debug (Fix)
Fix one issue at a time. After each fix, test again to confirm it works.
Document only the key change and why it worked.

Portfolio Entry 8 – Prototype V1 Evidence

‹#›

🟧🟩

What worked well in my first build was the crystal collection and counter system - every pickup registered correctly and the door opened exactly when it should after I fixed the wiring bug; what I'll improve next is adding more visual polish to Zone 2 and including sound effects so players get better feedback when they pick up crystals.

Deepen: Micro-Prototype Build
Build a tiny scene showing the main mechanic or interaction. Focus on one core element that players can test.
1- Set Up Scene Create basic environment with essential props
3- Test & Debug Play through your interaction and fix any issues
2- Add Interaction Implement one key mechanic (button, dialogue, trigger)
4- Document Progress Take screenshots and note ideas for improvement
Remember: This is a micro-prototype. Keep it simple but functional. Need a refresher from Term 1? Watch this quick recap: 🎥 👉 Video Link: “ How to Build a Basic Puzzle in Endstar ”

Stretch Activity - AI Workflow: 3D Iteration with Nano Banana & Meshy AI
Explore an advanced workflow for rapid 3D concept iteration, combining human blocking with AI enhancement. This process helps you understand and apply a professional workflow for evolving 3D concepts, leveraging AI as a powerful creative co-pilot without replacing foundational human creativity
Step-by-Step Workflow Initial 3D Blocking in Nano Banana: Create a basic prop or environment using simple geometric shapes. Focus on overall layout and form. Screenshot & Upload: Take a clear screenshot of your Nano Banana blockout and upload it to Meshy AI. AI Enhancement with Meshy AI: Use Meshy AI's "Image-to-3D" or "Text + Image" features to enhance your concept. Experiment with descriptive text prompts to guide the AI. Compare & Caption Results: Generate a new screenshot of the AI-refined concept. Compare it with your original and add a caption: "This shows my design evolving from a human 3D sketch to an AI-refined concept."
Evidence Submit two side-by-side screenshots: Your initial Nano Banana blockout. The AI-refined concept from Meshy AI. Include a brief reflection: "AI helped me refine my 3D idea and visualize a detailed prop or environment, bridging my initial concept with a polished visual prototype."
🟩🟪

Concept Ideation: Sketching with AI
Rapid Visualization using Nano Banana / Text-to-Image
Step 1: Ideation & Sketching (Nano Banana / Text-to-Image) Purpose: To quickly visualize initial ideas for characters or environments. Action: Input a descriptive text prompt (e.g., "full-body concept art sketch of a desert fort defender character, traditional Emirati clothing" ) into an AI sketch tool like Nano Banana. Review the initial sketches generated by Nano Banana for suitable concepts. Refine your chosen concept further using a Text-to-Image AI by adding more detail (e.g., "practical vest, determined expression, carrying a futuristic wrench"). Preparation for 3D (Crucial): Before using your refined image in Meshy AI, edit the picture to have a clean white or transparent background. This significantly helps Meshy AI isolate the character and create a better 3D model.

Creating & Texturing 3D Models with Meshy AI
From 2D Concept to Textured 3D Asset
Step 2: Generating Base 3D Model (Meshy AI - Untextured) Purpose: Convert your refined 2D character concept into an initial, untextured 3D model. Action: Upload your chosen concept image (from Step 1) to Meshy AI. Provide a detailed text prompt (e.g., "Full body render of a male character, based on the provided image, dressed in traditional Emirati attire, holding a futuristic glowing blue wrench") to guide the AI in creating the 3D form. Observe the generated grey mesh and its basic geometric properties (triangles, faces) within Meshy.
Step 3: Adding Texture & Detail (Meshy AI - Textured) Purpose: To apply colors, materials, and surface details to your 3D model, bringing it to life within Meshy. Action: In the Meshy interface, locate and click the "Texture" button (or similar prompt) to initiate the texturing process. The AI will apply materials and colors based on your initial input image and text prompt. Ensure options like "Generate PBR Maps" are enabled if available, as these maps are crucial for realistic lighting and rendering in modern game engines.

Beyond AI: From Model to Game Engine Concept
1- Meshy AI (Textured Model)
This is the fully textured 3D character model you obtained directly from Meshy AI. It serves as a fantastic starting point for further game development.
2- Blender (Retopology & Rigging)
For a production-ready game, the model would typically be imported into a dedicated 3D software like Blender. Here, human artists perform crucial steps such as: Retopology: Creating a cleaner, lower-polygon mesh to optimize performance in the game engine. Rigging: Adding a skeletal structure (bones) to the character, which is essential for posing and animating it within the game.
3- Endstar(Game Engine)
Finally, the optimized and rigged 3D character is imported into a game engine like Endless Studios. Within the engine, it's configured with character controllers, collision properties, and animations to become an interactive and functional part of the game world.
Industry Connection In the games industry, a 3D artist creates characters and props using tools like Blender . These assets are then prepared and added to a game engine so they can move, interact, and be used in a game.

'My strongest concept was ___ because ___; building it taught me ___ about game design.'
Be specific about what made your chosen concept work. Was it the clarity of the mechanic, the connection to your brief, or something else entirely?

Extension / Adaptive Task – Endstar Warm-Up
If time allows — or if the class would benefit from a more hands-on, active task — this Endstar Warm-Up can reinforce prototyping skills through practical application.
Mechanic Remix Take an existing Endstar scene and add one new interaction or modify an existing mechanic.
Purpose: Apply iterative thinking and rapid prototyping skills developed during concept generation. Test whether ideas translate effectively into playable experiences.

What is the most accurate explanation?
A. The AI is copying a single reference image
B. The AI is optimising for realism
C. The AI is reflecting patterns dominant in its training data and human-labelled examples
D. The AI is responding to the strongest prompt keywords
Lesson 5 – Learning Check
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
MCQ 5 .1
When generating multiple AI concept images for the same character, students notice recurring visual traits across many outputs.
OECD Mapping Knowledge Area: Human Choices & Bias (K2) Code: K 2 . 1 AI reflects human choices in data selection, curation, and system design

What does this most clearly demonstrate?
A. The AI misunderstood the task
B. The AI lacks awareness of gameplay and performance constraints
C. The AI prioritised creativity over accuracy
D. The input image quality was too low
Lesson 5 – Learning Check
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
MCQ 5 . 2
After converting a 2D AI-generated character into a 3D model, the result looks detailed but performs poorly in a game engine.
OECD Mapping Knowledge Area: Capabilities & Limits (K4) Code: K 4 . 1 AI lacks contextual understanding, judgement, and real-world constraints

What is the most significant risk?
A. Reduced originality
B. Reinforcing dominant cultural or market assumptions
C. Slower asset production
D. Increased file size
Lesson 5 – Learning Check
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
MCQ 5 . 3
A team selects an AI-generated character design without questioning which perspectives or audiences are represented.
OECD Mapping Knowledge Area: Human Choices & Bias (K2) Code: K 4 . 1 Bias can be perpetuated or mitigated through human evaluation and selection

Term 2
Lesson 5

Lesson 5: Idea Generation & Brainstorming
From Concept to Micro-Prototype
Product Development Cycle – Term 2 | Ideate → Prototype Phase
In this stage, students transform creative ideas into tangible prototypes through structured experimentation and early testing using Endstar.
UAE Link: This lesson supports innovation in the UAE by encouraging creative exploration of multiple ideas before selecting the strongest concept. Reflection Question: How can exploring different ideas lead to more innovative digital outcomes?

Big Question / Big Picture
Ideate → Prototype Phase
How do we turn an idea into something we can actually play?

Lesson 5: Learning Objectives
By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:
Translate to Micro-Prototype Develop one concept into a micro-prototype in Endstar and use peer feedback to plan improvements.
Generate and Refine Concepts Create and refine three game concepts linked to your brief using sketches or storyboards.

Lesson 5: Success Criteria – From Concept to Micro-Prototype
We will know we are successful when we can …
Build micro-prototype Create a micro-prototype showing at least one core mechanic in Endstar and document peer feedback to guide your next iteration.
Generate concepts Produce three distinct concepts aligned with your brief and select the strongest one.
Aligned to Product Development Cycle – Ideate → Prototype Phase.

Starter: From Idea to Experience
Discussion Questions What makes an idea playable? How do we bridge the gap between imagination and interactive experience? Connection Task Think about your favorite game mechanic. What made it engaging the first time you experienced it? Share with a partner: describe one moment when a simple interaction felt surprisingly fun.
Note : This lesson connects your creative thinking to the Prototype stage.

Mini Recap: What Makes a Great Game?
A quick recap to guide your game-building this lesson.
🎮 Mechanics — What the player does Core actions, rules, goals, interactions
Term 1 Recap "In Term 1, you explored how mechanics and simple level design shape player experience. This recap prepares you to begin building your game." You also studied the importance of a strong narrative .
The 3 Essential Pillars
⚡ Design / Dynamics — How the game feels Flow, pacing, difficulty, choices, challenge.
📖 Narrative / Theme — Why the game matters Story, setting, motivation, mood.
🎥 Optional Videos (for teachers & students): How the inventor of Mario designs a game : https://youtu.be/K-NBcP0YUQI
"A game is a series of interesting decisions." — Sid Meier "Gameplay always comes first." — Shigeru Miyamoto

Explore: Brainstorm & Sketch
Generate three game concepts based on your brief. Use paper, Miro , or Figma to sketch storyboards and reward loops.
Concept 1: Core Mechanic What is the main player action? (collect, build, solve, explore)
Concept 2: Player goal What does success look like? How do players know they're progressing?
Concept 3: Reward Loop Action → Feedback → Motivation. What keeps players engaged?
AI Prompt: 'Suggest two mechanics that fit a game about heritage or discovery.'

🟧🟩
Concept Selection Choose the concept you want to develop further and list the Endstar tools you expect to use: Dialogue systems for NPCs Triggers and interactions Props and environment pieces Logic chains for mechanics Other tools as needed
Peer Feedback Share your three concepts with a partner. Ask: Which concept feels most engaging for players? What might be challenging to build in Endstar? How does this concept connect to your brief's purpose?

Deepen: Micro-Prototype Build
Build a tiny scene showing the main mechanic or interaction. Focus on one core element that players can test.
1- Set Up Scene Create basic environment with essential props
3- Test & Debug Play through your interaction and fix any issues
2- Add Interaction Implement one key mechanic (button, dialogue, trigger)
4- Document Progress Take screenshots and note ideas for improvement
Remember: This is a micro-prototype. Keep it simple but functional. Need a refresher from Term 1? Watch this quick recap: 🎥 👉 Video Link: “ How to Build a Basic Puzzle in Endstar ”

Stretch Activity - AI Workflow: 3D Iteration with Nano Banana & Meshy AI
Explore an advanced workflow for rapid 3D concept iteration, combining human blocking with AI enhancement. This process helps you understand and apply a professional workflow for evolving 3D concepts, leveraging AI as a powerful creative co-pilot without replacing foundational human creativity
Step-by-Step Workflow Initial 3D Blocking in Nano Banana: Create a basic prop or environment using simple geometric shapes. Focus on overall layout and form. Screenshot & Upload: Take a clear screenshot of your Nano Banana blockout and upload it to Meshy AI. AI Enhancement with Meshy AI: Use Meshy AI's "Image-to-3D" or "Text + Image" features to enhance your concept. Experiment with descriptive text prompts to guide the AI. Compare & Caption Results: Generate a new screenshot of the AI-refined concept. Compare it with your original and add a caption: "This shows my design evolving from a human 3D sketch to an AI-refined concept."
Evidence Submit two side-by-side screenshots: Your initial Nano Banana blockout. The AI-refined concept from Meshy AI. Include a brief reflection: "AI helped me refine my 3D idea and visualize a detailed prop or environment, bridging my initial concept with a polished visual prototype."
🟩🟪

Concept Ideation: Sketching with AI
Rapid Visualization using Nano Banana / Text-to-Image
Step 1: Ideation & Sketching (Nano Banana / Text-to-Image) Purpose: To quickly visualize initial ideas for characters or environments. Action: Input a descriptive text prompt (e.g., "full-body concept art sketch of a desert fort defender character, traditional Emirati clothing" ) into an AI sketch tool like Nano Banana. Review the initial sketches generated by Nano Banana for suitable concepts. Refine your chosen concept further using a Text-to-Image AI by adding more detail (e.g., "practical vest, determined expression, carrying a futuristic wrench"). Preparation for 3D (Crucial): Before using your refined image in Meshy AI, edit the picture to have a clean white or transparent background. This significantly helps Meshy AI isolate the character and create a better 3D model.

Creating & Texturing 3D Models with Meshy AI
From 2D Concept to Textured 3D Asset
Step 2: Generating Base 3D Model (Meshy AI - Untextured) Purpose: Convert your refined 2D character concept into an initial, untextured 3D model. Action: Upload your chosen concept image (from Step 1) to Meshy AI. Provide a detailed text prompt (e.g., "Full body render of a male character, based on the provided image, dressed in traditional Emirati attire, holding a futuristic glowing blue wrench") to guide the AI in creating the 3D form. Observe the generated grey mesh and its basic geometric properties (triangles, faces) within Meshy.
Step 3: Adding Texture & Detail (Meshy AI - Textured) Purpose: To apply colors, materials, and surface details to your 3D model, bringing it to life within Meshy. Action: In the Meshy interface, locate and click the "Texture" button (or similar prompt) to initiate the texturing process. The AI will apply materials and colors based on your initial input image and text prompt. Ensure options like "Generate PBR Maps" are enabled if available, as these maps are crucial for realistic lighting and rendering in modern game engines.

Beyond AI: From Model to Game Engine Concept
1- Meshy AI (Textured Model)
This is the fully textured 3D character model you obtained directly from Meshy AI. It serves as a fantastic starting point for further game development.
2- Blender (Retopology & Rigging)
For a production-ready game, the model would typically be imported into a dedicated 3D software like Blender. Here, human artists perform crucial steps such as: Retopology: Creating a cleaner, lower-polygon mesh to optimize performance in the game engine. Rigging: Adding a skeletal structure (bones) to the character, which is essential for posing and animating it within the game.
3- Endstar(Game Engine)
Finally, the optimized and rigged 3D character is imported into a game engine like Endless Studios. Within the engine, it's configured with character controllers, collision properties, and animations to become an interactive and functional part of the game world.
Industry Connection In the games industry, a 3D artist creates characters and props using tools like Blender . These assets are then prepared and added to a game engine so they can move, interact, and be used in a game.

2- Final Meshy Ai 3D Model

A. The AI is copying a single reference image
B. The AI is optimising for realism
C. The AI is reflecting patterns dominant in its training data and human-labelled examples
D. The AI is responding to the strongest prompt keywords

What does this most clearly demonstrate?
A. The AI misunderstood the task
B. The AI lacks awareness of gameplay and performance constraints
C. The AI prioritised creativity over accuracy
D. The input image quality was too low

MCQ 5 . 2
After converting a 2D AI-generated character into a 3D model, the result looks detailed but performs poorly in a game engine.
OECD Mapping Knowledge Area: Capabilities & Limits (K4) Code: K 4 . 1 AI lacks contextual understanding, judgement, and real-world constraints

What is the most significant risk?
A. Reduced originality
B. Reinforcing dominant cultural or market assumptions
C. Slower asset production
D. Increased file size

MCQ 5 . 3
A team selects an AI-generated character design without questioning which perspectives or audiences are represented.
OECD Mapping Knowledge Area: Human Choices & Bias (K2) Code: K 4 . 1 Bias can be perpetuated or mitigated through human evaluation and selection

Term 2
Lesson 5

Lesson 5: Idea Generation & Brainstorming
From Concept to Micro-Prototype
Product Development Cycle – Term 2 | Ideate → Prototype Phase
In this stage, students transform creative ideas into tangible prototypes through structured experimentation and early testing using Endstar.
UAE Link: This lesson supports innovation in the UAE by encouraging creative exploration of multiple ideas before selecting the strongest concept. Reflection Question: How can exploring different ideas lead to more innovative digital outcomes?
native_slides.speaker_notes: Structure of lesson:Students brainstorm three ideas (mechanic, goal, reward loop), share with peers, and select ONE idea to develop. The chosen concept becomes a micro-prototype in Endstar. Students upload a screenshot of the prototype and reflect on what worked and what they’ll improve next. The three initial concepts support ideation but are not part of the portfolio.

native_slides.content:
Big Question / Big Picture
Ideate → Prototype Phase
How do we turn an idea into something we can actually play?
native_slides.speaker_notes: Display beside short clips of simple prototypes evolving into finished levels. Guiding Computational Thinking Question: What if we don't have the right tools — how can we adapt our ideas to what's possible in Endstar?

native_slides.content:
Lesson 5: Learning Objectives
By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:
Translate to Micro-Prototype Develop one concept into a micro-prototype in Endstar and use peer feedback to plan improvements.
Generate and Refine Concepts Create and refine three game concepts linked to your brief using sketches or storyboards.

Lesson 5: Success Criteria – From Concept to Micro-Prototype
We will know we are successful when we can …
Build micro-prototype Create a micro-prototype showing at least one core mechanic in Endstar and document peer feedback to guide your next iteration.
Generate concepts Produce three distinct concepts aligned with your brief and select the strongest one.
Aligned to Product Development Cycle – Ideate → Prototype Phase.

Starter: From Idea to Experience
Discussion Questions What makes an idea playable? How do we bridge the gap between imagination and interactive experience? Connection Task Think about your favorite game mechanic. What made it engaging the first time you experienced it? Share with a partner: describe one moment when a simple interaction felt surprisingly fun.
Note : This lesson connects your creative thinking to the Prototype stage.

rows: Props
Treasure (x3 crystals), Door - Mechanical, Lever
Rule Blocks
Counter Rule, Trigger Volume, Dialogue Node
NPCs
Medium NPC (caretaker character)
Display
Anachronist Billboard (show crystal count)
Environment
Ambient Settings (skybox change for ending)
Logic
Level Transition Rule (win screen)

headers: Why it Worked / What I learned
rows: Player spawned facing away from the NPC caretaker, so they walked the wrong direction and missed the dialogue completely
Moved the Trigger Volume to cover the entire spawn area and rotated the spawn point to face the NPC
Now players trigger the dialogue automatically when they spawn - I learned spawn orientation matters a lot for first impressions
Only 2 of the 3 crystals were counting. Counter showed 2/3 even after collecting all crystals
Found that Crystal #3 wasn't wired to the Counter Rule - I forgot to click "Confirm" on the wire
Always check every wire individually and click Confirm! One missing connection breaks the whole system
Door opened but there was no feedback - player didn't know it happened because they were far away collecting the last crystal
Players need feedback at the moment something happens, not just the result. Sound or text helps a lot
Added wire from Counter Rule OnCountReached → Billboard SetRawText to display "Door Unlocked!" message

teacher_notes.notes: Guiding Computational Thinking Question: What if we don't have the right tools — how can we adapt and problem-solve in Endstar?
*Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1c | AQA 3.1.3 | IB Criterion C | CSTA 3A-CS-02 (Computing Systems)*

Curriculum Alignment: 🇬🇧 UK D&T DfE KS3 1a – Use research to understand user needs 🇬🇧 AQA 3.1.3 – Develop and model prototypes digitally and physically 🌍 IB MYP Criterion C – Creating the Solution (construct and test models) 🇺🇸 US CSTA 3A-CS-02 – Understand how components of computing systems interact

Teacher Note: Encourage students to provide clear, labelled images or sketches. Remind them to connect their concept board to the "Define" stage of the Product Development Cycle and explain how it shapes their game brief.

Teacher Note: Students need to understand that designers don’t follow the process in one straight line. Early steps were linear to help them learn the structure. Now they begin working like real developers — looping between Prototype ↔ Test ↔ Improve. Reinforce that iteration is normal, and mistakes or bugs are expected parts of the cycle.” Curriculum Alignment: 🇬🇧 UK D&T DfE KS3 1a – Use research to understand user needs 🇬🇧 AQA 3.1.3 – Develop and model prototypes digitally and physically 🌍 IB MYP Criterion C – Creating the Solution (construct and test models) 🇺🇸 US CSTA 3A-CS-02 – Understand how components of computing systems interact
Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1c | AQA 3.1.3 | IB Criterion C | CSTA 3A-AP-17 (Iteration)
Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1c-1d | AQA 3.1.3-3.1.4 | IB Criterion C Creating the Solution | CSTA 3A-AP-20 (Debugging)
*Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1d | AQA 3.1.4 | IB Criterion D Evaluating | CSTA 3A-AP-20 (Debugging)*

Teacher Notes: Phind AI is an AI assistant trained for technical reasoning and debugging. Students should use it after they test their prototype themselves — not before. Encourage them to document which fix worked and why it was effective. Emphasise computational thinking: identifying cause and effect, evaluating multiple solutions. 📘 Speaker Notes (Curriculum Alignment): CSTA Standards: CT.L1:6-05 – Test and debug to improve solutions. CT.L2:6-06 – Implement problem-solving strategies for real-world issues. UK D&T: KS3 Design 1d – Test and refine ideas. IB Design (2026): Criterion C – Creating the Solution → prototype testing and refinement.
Teacher Note : Students may upload screenshots, paste images, or type directly in the table. If no bugs were found, they must document one playtest observation and one planned improvement.
*Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1d | AQA 3.1.4 | IB Criterion D Evaluating*

slides.content:
Term 2
Lesson 6

Lesson 6: Prototype v1 - Building the Core Mechanic
Product Development Cycle – Term 2 | Ideate → Prototype Phase
In this stage, students translate their concepts into working builds and begin iterative testing of core mechanics using Endstar.
UAE Link: This lesson develops digital design skills by turning ideas into a working prototype through experimentation and testing, reflecting practices used in digital product development in the UAE. Reflection Question: Why is building an early prototype important in digital product development?

Big Question / Big Picture
Ideate → Prototype Phase
How can we make our first playable version simple but effective?

Lesson 6: Learning Objectives
By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:
Implement and Debug Implement and debug core mechanics through peer and AI-assisted testing to ensure functionality.
Develop Functional Prototype Develop your Lesson 5 micro-prototype into a functional Prototype v1 that meets brief requirements

Lesson 6: Success Criteria – Building the Core Mechanic
We will know we are successful when we can …
Aligned to Product Development Cycle – Ideate → Prototype → Test Phase.
Debug and test Identify and fix at least one bug or design issue through testing and AI support.
Functional prototype Demonstrate a functional Prototype v1 with a working feature and interaction.

Starter: Recap & Reward Loop
Lesson 5 Recap Review your micro-prototype from last lesson. What worked well? What needs improvement? Reward Loop Connection Think about successful games you've played. What keeps you coming back? How does the game make you feel accomplished?
⭐ Term 1 Reminder You explored reward loops in Term 1 — how games keep players engaged through challenge, feedback, and rewards. Apply that thinking as you look at what works in your prototype and what could be improved.

Inside a Game System
Input: player actions or triggers (e.g., pressing a button) Process: logic or rules (e.g., open door when pressed) Output: the result (door opens, sound plays, reward appears)
Every prototype works like a computing system — it takes inputs, processes them through logic, and produces outputs for the player. You used this pattern in Lesson 5 when building your micro-prototype. Now we’re applying it again as you expand to Prototype v1.
Let's map this out using Endstar's tools:
Task: "Choose one mechanic from your own prototype. Sketch a flow chart to show how it follows the Input → Process → Output sequence." Example: Player steps on plate → rule activates sound → door opens.
Output
Process
Input

Explore — Activity 6.1: Sketch a flowchart
You may sketch your flowchart on paper or use any digital tool such as Miro , Figma , Canva , Google Sketch, or a tablet drawing app. Upload your sketch or screenshot below, and add a link if needed. https://app.diagrams.net/
insert video clip or digital drawing link here.
🟧🟥

The Product Development Cycle – Visual Overview
The Product Development Cycle is used in technology, engineering, business and game design to turn ideas into real products. At the start of Term 2, you followed the stages in a linear way — Empathise → Define → Ideate → Prototype . From this lesson onward, the process becomes cyclical: you will build , test , improve, and repeat . This looping approach is called iteration , and it’s how real designers refine a product until it feels great to use.
Need a clearer explanation? Watch the short explainer video: 🎥 🔗 The Product Development Cycle

The 5 Stages of the Product Development Cycle
Everything you’ve done so far — analysing briefs, researching, and generating ideas — is part of the designer’s process. You have already completed Empathise → Define → Ideate. Today you enter the PROTOTYPE stage, and next you begin TEST & EVALUATE as you start improving your game through iteration .

Explore: Set-Up and Plan
Plan your Prototype v1 expansion. Review your micro-prototype and identify what needs to be added or improved.
1
Review Micro-Prototype
What worked? What broke? What felt incomplete?
2
Plan Implementation
Which Endstar tools will you need? What's the build order?
3
Identify Core Features
List 2-3 essential features for a complete experience
4
Predict Issues
What problems might players encounter?
AI Prompt: Use this AI prompt for Step 4, ‘Predict three issues a player might have with this prototype.'

Explore: Prototype V1 Plan
“A moment where the player might get confused is…” “An interaction that felt unfinished or missing was…”
Review Micro-Prototype What worked? What broke? What felt incomplete?
“To make the mechanic work, I need to use the ___ tool because…” “My build order will be: ” 1) ________ 2) ________ 3) ________
Plan Implementation Which Endstar tools will you need? What’s the build order?
“My game won’t make sense unless players can…” “Players should always be able to…”
Identify Core Features List 2-3 essential features for a complete experience.
Predict Issues What problems might players encounter?

Analyse: Build Prototype v1
Transform your micro-prototype into a more complete experience. Focus on functionality over polish.
Expand Core Mechanic Add the essential features you identified in planning
Link System Connect different elements with logic chains and triggers
Test As You Build Play through each addition to ensure it works
UAE Example – Digital Product Development in the UAE Careem, founded in the UAE, was developed through early prototypes, user testing, and continuous iteration before scaling into a global digital platform. Like your first prototype, early versions focused on core functionality before refinement.
Discussion: "What features do you think Careem tested first?"

Deepen: Playtest and Debug
Playtest Play through your Prototype v1 from beginning to end. Look for anything confusing, broken, or missing Check interactions, timings, triggers, visibility, and flow. Make notes as you go.
Debug (Fix) Fix one issue at a time. After each fix, playtest again to confirm it works. Keep track of what changed.
Document Screenshot any issues or improvements. Explain what changed and why. If you had no issues, document what worked well and one improvement you want to add in Prototype v2.

AI Support : CoT (chain of thought) with Phind AI
Example prompt: "My button trigger only works once — explain why and give three possible fixes."
Reflection prompt: "How did Phind's reasoning help you understand your game logic better?"
Having trouble fixing your prototype? Describe your problem in Phind AI using clear, simple language. What is Phind AI? Phind is an AI tool designed specifically for problem-solving and debugging. It gives practical, step-by-step reasoning to help identify issues in your logic, triggers, or interactions. https://www.phind.com/
Try one suggestion from Phind AI.
Test it inside Endstar.
Record what worked and what didn't in your Debug Log.
Steps:
Chat GPT-designed part of Sheikh Tahnoon's home hailed as AI becomes 'new lifestyle'

Activity 6.2: Playtest & Debug Log
Playtest your Prototype v1 Play through your prototype from beginning to end. Identify anything confusing, broken, or incomplete. If everything works, record what worked well and one improvement you plan for v2. Debug (Fix) Fix one issue at a time. After each fix, test again to confirm it works. Document only the key change and why it worked.
🟩🟥

Portfolio Entry 8 – Prototype V1 Evidence
🟧🟩
What worked well in my first build was ___; what I'll improve next is ___.
Upload Area - Upload your Prototype v1 screenshots, or gameplay video here. (Image, screenshot, video, or link from Drive/OneDrive/Endstar)
insert video clip or digital drawing link here.
Optional: Add brief on-image notes or labels to help explain what your screenshot or clip is showing.

Portfolio Entry 8 Additional Upload Space (Optional)
Use this slide if you need more room to upload extra Image, screenshot, video, or link from Drive/OneDrive/Endstar.
insert video clip or digital drawing link here.
🟧🟩
Optional: Add brief on-image notes or labels to help explain what your screenshot or clip is showing.

Reflect: My Development Process
Reflect on your journey from micro-prototype to Prototype v1. What did you learn about the iterative development process?
'Building Prototype v1 taught me ___ about game development; my biggest challenge was ___ and I solved it by ___.'
Consider both technical challenges (bugs, mechanics) and design challenges (player experience, clarity). How did testing and debugging change your understanding of your game?
My Reflection:
I used AI in this lesson OR chose not to use AI I checked AI output instead of trusting it straight away I used my own judgement to make decisions
✍️ AI Reflection (2–3 sentences) Complete the sentence starters:
Today AI helped me by…
I still needed to decide…
🤖 AI Literacy Reflection

My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment
Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson
Self-Consistency Prompting for Debugging AI can provide more reliable debugging help when you ask it to check its own reasoning. This technique reduces errors and gives you more trustworthy solutions.
Why This Works By asking AI to show its reasoning process, you can evaluate whether its suggestions make sense for your specific situation.
Comparison Activity General Prompt: 'Fix this bug in my game.' Self-Consistency Prompt: 'Analyze this bug, explain three possible causes, then recommend the most likely solution and explain why.'
Practice Task Try both approaches with a bug in your prototype and compare the quality of responses.

Extension / Adaptive Task – Endstar Warm-Up
Quick Fix Challenge Find a broken scene in Endstar (or break something intentionally) and practice systematic debugging.
If time allows — or if the class would benefit from a more hands-on, active task — this Endstar Warm-Up connects debugging skills to practical problem-solving.
Purpose: Apply logical debugging methods and systematic problem-solving skills developed during prototype testing. Reinforce the importance of testing each change individually.

End of Define → Prototype Phase Summary
Review Portfolio Entries 3–6 and ensure all evidence is uploaded.
1
Micro-Prototype Evidence
Showcases small-scale tests of specific mechanics to ensure they work as intended before full development.
2
Prototype V1
Captures your first complete playable iteration and identifies specific goals for the next stage of development.
How has your understanding of the design process changed from research to prototyping?
🟥🟩

Endless Studios Session
Let’s Build. Create. Experiment Make You Choose.
Jump into Endstar Build and experiment Test with your team Fix bugs together Try new ideas Explore AI tools Choose how you want to work
Schools with 2 lessons/week: this can be self-directed learning from home. Schools with 3 lessons/week: teachers may run this as an open studio build sessions.

Why is AI poorly suited to making final design decisions in a game project?
A. It lacks sufficient training data
B. It cannot follow instructions
C. It lacks lived experience, values, and judgement
D. It prioritises efficiency
Lesson 6 – Learning Check
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
MCQ 6 . 1
OECD Mapping Knowledge Area: Capabilities & Limits (K4) Code: K1. 1 AI lacks context, ethics, and human judgement

What should guide the final decision?
A. The AI’s confidence level
B. The most detailed output
C. Human evaluation grounded in real constraints
D. Re-prompting the AI
Lesson 6 – Learning Check
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
MCQ 6 . 2
An AI generates confident recommendations that conflict with real player feedback.
OECD Mapping Knowledge Area: Work & Human Roles (K3) Code: K 3 . 3 Humans remain responsible for judgement and decisions

Which situation BEST illustrates an AI limitation rather than a prompt failure?
A. Vague output from vague input
B. Incorrect facts delivered confidently
C. Short answers to short prompts
D. Repetitive phrasing
Lesson 6 – Learning Check
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
MCQ 6 . 3
OECD Mapping Knowledge Area: Capabilities & Limits (K4) Code: K 4 . 3 Generative AI may produce misinformation or fabricated content
