Starter: Recap & Reward Loop
Lesson 5 Recap Review your micro-prototype from last lesson. What worked well? What needs improvement? Reward Loop Connection Think about successful games you've played. What keeps you coming back? How does the game make you feel accomplished?
⭐ Term 1 Reminder You explored reward loops in Term 1 — how games keep players engaged through challenge, feedback, and rewards. Apply that thinking as you look at what works in your prototype and what could be improved.
native_slides.speaker_notes: *Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1c | AQA 3.1.3 | IB Criterion C | CSTA 3A-CS-02 (Computing Systems)*

native_slides.content:
Inside a Game System
Input: player actions or triggers (e.g., pressing a button) Process: logic or rules (e.g., open door when pressed) Output: the result (door opens, sound plays, reward appears)
Every prototype works like a computing system — it takes inputs, processes them through logic, and produces outputs for the player. You used this pattern in Lesson 5 when building your micro-prototype. Now we’re applying it again as you expand to Prototype v1.
Let's map this out using Endstar's tools:
Task: "Choose one mechanic from your own prototype. Sketch a flow chart to show how it follows the Input → Process → Output sequence." Example: Player steps on plate → rule activates sound → door opens.
Output
Process
Input
native_slides.speaker_notes: Curriculum Alignment: 🇬🇧 UK D&T DfE KS3 1a – Use research to understand user needs 🇬🇧 AQA 3.1.3 – Develop and model prototypes digitally and physically 🌍 IB MYP Criterion C – Creating the Solution (construct and test models) 🇺🇸 US CSTA 3A-CS-02 – Understand how components of computing systems interact

native_slides.content:
Explore — Activity 6.1: Sketch a flowchart
You may sketch your flowchart on paper or use any digital tool such as Miro , Figma , Canva , Google Sketch, or a tablet drawing app. Upload your sketch or screenshot below, and add a link if needed. https://app.diagrams.net/
insert video clip or digital drawing link here.
🟧🟥
native_slides.speaker_notes: Teacher Note: Encourage students to provide clear, labelled images or sketches. Remind them to connect their concept board to the "Define" stage of the Product Development Cycle and explain how it shapes their game brief.

native_slides.content:
The Product Development Cycle – Visual Overview
The Product Development Cycle is used in technology, engineering, business and game design to turn ideas into real products. At the start of Term 2, you followed the stages in a linear way — Empathise → Define → Ideate → Prototype . From this lesson onward, the process becomes cyclical: you will build , test , improve, and repeat . This looping approach is called iteration , and it’s how real designers refine a product until it feels great to use.
Need a clearer explanation? Watch the short explainer video: 🎥 🔗 The Product Development Cycle
native_slides.speaker_notes: Teacher Note: Students need to understand that designers don’t follow the process in one straight line. Early steps were linear to help them learn the structure. Now they begin working like real developers — looping between Prototype ↔ Test ↔ Improve. Reinforce that iteration is normal, and mistakes or bugs are expected parts of the cycle.” Curriculum Alignment: 🇬🇧 UK D&T DfE KS3 1a – Use research to understand user needs 🇬🇧 AQA 3.1.3 – Develop and model prototypes digitally and physically 🌍 IB MYP Criterion C – Creating the Solution (construct and test models) 🇺🇸 US CSTA 3A-CS-02 – Understand how components of computing systems interact

native_slides.content:
The 5 Stages of the Product Development Cycle
Everything you’ve done so far — analysing briefs, researching, and generating ideas — is part of the designer’s process. You have already completed Empathise → Define → Ideate. Today you enter the PROTOTYPE stage, and next you begin TEST & EVALUATE as you start improving your game through iteration .

Explore: Set-Up and Plan
Plan your Prototype v1 expansion. Review your micro-prototype and identify what needs to be added or improved.
1
Review Micro-Prototype
What worked? What broke? What felt incomplete?
2
Plan Implementation
Which Endstar tools will you need? What's the build order?
3
Identify Core Features
List 2-3 essential features for a complete experience
4
Predict Issues
What problems might players encounter?
AI Prompt: Use this AI prompt for Step 4, ‘Predict three issues a player might have with this prototype.'
native_slides.speaker_notes: Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1c | AQA 3.1.3 | IB Criterion C | CSTA 3A-AP-17 (Iteration)

native_slides.content:
Explore: Prototype V1 Plan
“A moment where the player might get confused is…” “An interaction that felt unfinished or missing was…”
Review Micro-Prototype What worked? What broke? What felt incomplete?
“To make the mechanic work, I need to use the ___ tool because…” “My build order will be: ” 1) ________ 2) ________ 3) ________
Plan Implementation Which Endstar tools will you need? What’s the build order?
“My game won’t make sense unless players can…” “Players should always be able to…”
Identify Core Features List 2-3 essential features for a complete experience.
Predict Issues What problems might players encounter?

Analyse: Build Prototype v1
Transform your micro-prototype into a more complete experience. Focus on functionality over polish.
Expand Core Mechanic Add the essential features you identified in planning
Link System Connect different elements with logic chains and triggers
Test As You Build Play through each addition to ensure it works
UAE Example – Digital Product Development in the UAE Careem, founded in the UAE, was developed through early prototypes, user testing, and continuous iteration before scaling into a global digital platform. Like your first prototype, early versions focused on core functionality before refinement.
Discussion: "What features do you think Careem tested first?"
native_slides.speaker_notes: Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1c-1d | AQA 3.1.3-3.1.4 | IB Criterion C Creating the Solution | CSTA 3A-AP-20 (Debugging)

native_slides.content:
Deepen: Playtest and Debug
Playtest Play through your Prototype v1 from beginning to end. Look for anything confusing, broken, or missing Check interactions, timings, triggers, visibility, and flow. Make notes as you go.
Debug (Fix) Fix one issue at a time. After each fix, playtest again to confirm it works. Keep track of what changed.
Document Screenshot any issues or improvements. Explain what changed and why. If you had no issues, document what worked well and one improvement you want to add in Prototype v2.
native_slides.speaker_notes: *Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1d | AQA 3.1.4 | IB Criterion D Evaluating | CSTA 3A-AP-20 (Debugging)*

native_slides.content:
AI Support : CoT (chain of thought) with Phind AI
Example prompt: "My button trigger only works once — explain why and give three possible fixes."
Reflection prompt: "How did Phind's reasoning help you understand your game logic better?"
Having trouble fixing your prototype? Describe your problem in Phind AI using clear, simple language. What is Phind AI? Phind is an AI tool designed specifically for problem-solving and debugging. It gives practical, step-by-step reasoning to help identify issues in your logic, triggers, or interactions. https://www.phind.com/
Try one suggestion from Phind AI.
Test it inside Endstar.
Record what worked and what didn't in your Debug Log.
Steps:
Chat GPT-designed part of Sheikh Tahnoon's home hailed as AI becomes 'new lifestyle'
native_slides.speaker_notes: Teacher Notes: Phind AI is an AI assistant trained for technical reasoning and debugging. Students should use it after they test their prototype themselves — not before. Encourage them to document which fix worked and why it was effective. Emphasise computational thinking: identifying cause and effect, evaluating multiple solutions. 📘 Speaker Notes (Curriculum Alignment): CSTA Standards: CT.L1:6-05 – Test and debug to improve solutions. CT.L2:6-06 – Implement problem-solving strategies for real-world issues. UK D&T: KS3 Design 1d – Test and refine ideas. IB Design (2026): Criterion C – Creating the Solution → prototype testing and refinement.

native_slides.content:
Activity 6.2: Playtest & Debug Log
Playtest your Prototype v1 Play through your prototype from beginning to end. Identify anything confusing, broken, or incomplete. If everything works, record what worked well and one improvement you plan for v2. Debug (Fix) Fix one issue at a time. After each fix, test again to confirm it works. Document only the key change and why it worked.
🟩🟥
native_slides.speaker_notes: Teacher Note : Students may upload screenshots, paste images, or type directly in the table. If no bugs were found, they must document one playtest observation and one planned improvement.

🟧🟩
What worked well in my first build was ___; what I'll improve next is ___.

Reflect: My Development Process
Reflect on your journey from micro-prototype to Prototype v1. What did you learn about the iterative development process?
'Building Prototype v1 taught me ___ about game development; my biggest challenge was ___ and I solved it by ___.'
Consider both technical challenges (bugs, mechanics) and design challenges (player experience, clarity). How did testing and debugging change your understanding of your game?
My Reflection:
I used AI in this lesson OR chose not to use AI I checked AI output instead of trusting it straight away I used my own judgement to make decisions
✍️ AI Reflection (2–3 sentences) Complete the sentence starters:
Today AI helped me by…
I still needed to decide…
🤖 AI Literacy Reflection
native_slides.speaker_notes: *Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1d | AQA 3.1.4 | IB Criterion D Evaluating*

native_slides.content:
My Success Criteria / Self-Assessment
Reflect on how well you met today's learning objectives.

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson
Self-Consistency Prompting for Debugging AI can provide more reliable debugging help when you ask it to check its own reasoning. This technique reduces errors and gives you more trustworthy solutions.
Why This Works By asking AI to show its reasoning process, you can evaluate whether its suggestions make sense for your specific situation.
Comparison Activity General Prompt: 'Fix this bug in my game.' Self-Consistency Prompt: 'Analyze this bug, explain three possible causes, then recommend the most likely solution and explain why.'
Practice Task Try both approaches with a bug in your prototype and compare the quality of responses.

Extension / Adaptive Task – Endstar Warm-Up
Quick Fix Challenge Find a broken scene in Endstar (or break something intentionally) and practice systematic debugging.
If time allows — or if the class would benefit from a more hands-on, active task — this Endstar Warm-Up connects debugging skills to practical problem-solving.
Purpose: Apply logical debugging methods and systematic problem-solving skills developed during prototype testing. Reinforce the importance of testing each change individually.

End of Define → Prototype Phase Summary
Review Portfolio Entries 3–6 and ensure all evidence is uploaded.
1
Micro-Prototype Evidence
Showcases small-scale tests of specific mechanics to ensure they work as intended before full development.
2
Prototype V1
Captures your first complete playable iteration and identifies specific goals for the next stage of development.
How has your understanding of the design process changed from research to prototyping?
🟥🟩

Endless Studios Session
Let’s Build. Create. Experiment Make You Choose.
Jump into Endstar Build and experiment Test with your team Fix bugs together Try new ideas Explore AI tools Choose how you want to work
Schools with 2 lessons/week: this can be self-directed learning from home. Schools with 3 lessons/week: teachers may run this as an open studio build sessions.

Why is AI poorly suited to making final design decisions in a game project?
A. It lacks sufficient training data
B. It cannot follow instructions
C. It lacks lived experience, values, and judgement
D. It prioritises efficiency

native_slides.content:
What should guide the final decision?
A. The AI’s confidence level
B. The most detailed output
C. Human evaluation grounded in real constraints
D. Re-prompting the AI

An AI generates confident recommendations that conflict with real player feedback.

native_slides.speaker_notes: Teaching note: The questions here are to help students prepare for the end of unit assessment. These questions are practice only to be completed in Classkick at the end of each lesson Answer KEY - C

Term 2

Lesson 7

Reflection Questions

Which enhancement had the biggest impact on player experience?
The NPC dialogue change had the biggest impact. Before it was just "collect stuff" but now players actually understand why they're exploring the mosque and what the crystals represent. Ahmed said it made him want to find all of them instead of just rushing through.
How did your Lesson 2 persona research influence your design choices?
My persona was a Grade 7 student who likes adventure games but gets bored with too much reading. So I kept the dialogue short but meaningful - just 2 sentences that give context without being a wall of text. I also made sure there's still action right away.
 What did you learn about the relationship between technical implementation and emotional impact?
I learned that small changes make big differences. Adding a Dialogue Node is technically easy - just place it, wire it, type text - but the right words can completely change how players feel about the game. Same with props - lanterns are just decorations but they make the space feel sacred and important. The technical part is simple but the design thinking behind it is what matters.

Activity 8.1 – Feedback Rewrite

‹#›

🟧 🟥

Use the poor feedback example from the Starter (“The game is boring and confusing.”). Rewrite it to make it more useful, then complete the comparison table below.

Feedback Comparison Table

Reflection Question
What are three key elements that make feedback truly useful for game developers?


| Original Feedback | Improved Feedback | What Made It Better | Why This Helps the Developer |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| "The game is boring and confusing." | "The first zone felt slow because I was just walking around collecting crystals with no challenge. I didn't know where to go next after getting the first 3 crystals." | It says exactly WHICH part was boring (Zone 1) and WHY (no challenge, just walking). It also explains what was confusing (not knowing where to go). | Now I know to add more interaction in Zone 1 and maybe add a hint system or NPC dialogue pointing to the next area |
| "The game is boring and confusing." | "The crystal collection got repetitive after a while. Also the door mechanic confused me - I didn't realize I needed to step on the pressure plate first." | It identifies the specific mechanic that was repetitive (crystals) and the exact confusion point (pressure plate + door connection) | I can add variety to collectibles or add a visual hint near the pressure plate showing it connects to the door |

Be specific – Say exactly which part of the game you're talking about, not just "the whole thing." Like instead of "it's confusing," say "the lever in Zone 2 confused me."
Explain why – Don't just say something is bad, explain what made it feel that way. "The timer was too short" is better than "the timer was annoying."
Connect to player experience – Describe what you were trying to do and what stopped you. Like "I wanted to find the last crystal but there was no hint where to look" tells the developer exactly what to fix.

AI Analysis Documentation
Use Gemini/Claude/other AI to summarise your tester comments. Paste the AI summary below.

Based on the feedback from 4 testers, three main themes emerge:

  1. Navigation/Guidance Issues (High Priority) - Multiple testers mentioned difficulty finding hidden crystals and wanting more hints or a help system. This was the most frequently mentioned concern.
  2. NPC Presence/Interaction (Medium Priority) - Testers wanted more NPC interaction throughout the game, not just at the beginning. The single dialogue made exploration feel lonely.
  3. Counter Clarity (Medium Priority) - The current counter system shows progress but doesn't help players locate remaining items. Consider adding directional hints when players are close to collectibles.

  Positive themes: Visual polish (skybox, decorations), educational content (NPC dialogue), and mechanic variety (timed door, pressure plate, levers) were consistently praised."


| Tester Name | What Went Well (WWW) | Even Better If (EBI) | Specific Issues Found |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Fatima | The golden skybox ending was beautiful and felt like a real reward. The NPC dialogue taught me about Sheikh Zayed. | There were more hints about where to find the hidden crystals. I got stuck for a while looking. | Couldn't find crystal #4 - no visual clue where to look |
| Omar | The timed door in Zone 2 was exciting and made me rush. Loved the decorated entrance with lanterns. | The counter showed how many crystals were left to find, not just how many I collected. | Counter shows "3/5" but doesn't tell me where to find the missing 2 |
| Khalid | Lever puzzles were fun and the wiring worked perfectly. The Zone 2 area looked really cool. | The NPC had more dialogue or appeared again later in the game to give encouragement. | NPC only talks once at the start, then disappears - felt lonely exploring alone |
| Sara | The welcome message when entering Zone 2 was a nice touch. Gameplay was smooth, no bugs. | The NPC gave me a hint if I was stuck for too long. Some kind of help system. | Got frustrated after 2 minutes of searching, almost quit |

Theme Identification (Top 3 Issues)
Based on the AI analysis and your own, what are the top 3 themes that need attention?

1. Player guidance / hint system – Players get lost finding crystals
2. NPC appears only once – Game feels lonely after the intro
3. Counter doesn't help locate items – Shows progress but not direction

Portfolio Entry 10b – Theme Mapping & Planning

‹#›

🟧 🟥

Rank your feedback themes by importance: “Planned actions should focus on improving player experience (e.g., flow, clarity, engagement, or functionality).”

Theme Priority Table

AI Suggestions Documentation
Change 1: Add proximity hint system using Trigger Volumes
  Rationale: AI suggested placing invisible Trigger Volumes in a radius around each hidden crystal. When player enters the zone, a Dialogue Node triggers with text like "The light grows stronger here..." This guides players without giving exact locations, keeping the exploration fun but less frustrating.
  Change 2: Add a second NPC encounter in Zone 2
  Rationale: AI suggested placing another Medium NPC (could be the same guide character) near the Zone 2 entrance with a Dialogue Node wired to a Trigger Volume. The NPC says something like "You're doing well, explorer. Two more Lights remain in this courtyard." This breaks up the lonely feeling and reinforces the educational narrative.

I'll implement Change 1 (hint system) first because it was mentioned by 3 out of 4 testers and has the highest impact on player experience. If players quit because they're frustrated, they won't even see the cool ending. The Trigger Volume + Dialogue Node combo is easy to build in Endstar and I already know how to wire it from my Zone 2 welcome message.

  Then I'll do Change 2 (second NPC) because it's quick to add and will make the game feel more alive. I can copy my existing NPC and just change the dialogue text.

  Change 3 (zone-specific counters) I'll skip for now because it needs multiple Billboards and more complex wiring - I'll save that for polish if I have time.

| Counter doesn't help locate items | 2 out of 4 testers | Medium | 3 | Add Billboard signs in each zone showing "Lights in this area: 2" so players know where to search |

Stretch Activity – Cutscene Challenge
Encourage students to deepen immersion by adding a short cutscene to their game. Cutscenes help guide players, introduce story moments, and create atmosphere. They can build tension, reveal goals, or add personality to characters. If you finish early, try adding a cutscene — a short, non-playable sequence triggered by player actions. Use Endstar tools such as camera changes, dialogue nodes, timed events, or animated props. A cutscene in game design creates a cinematic moment for players to engage with — like an intro scene, warning message, character reveal, or mission start.
Examples: Trigger-activated camera pan revealing a new area NPC dialogue scene that introduces a challenge or clue Fade-in/fade-out moment before a major event Timed lighting or sound cue that sets the mood A short intro sequence when the player enters a new zone

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson
AI "hallucination" can be reduced by combining AI generation with reliable information retrieval. RAG helps AI access accurate, up-to-date information before generating responses.
Retrieve
AI searches reliable sources for relevant information
RAG – Retrieval Augmented Generation
Augment
AI combines retrieved facts with its knowledge
Generate
AI creates responses based on verified information
How RAG Works
Gaming Application Educational games can use RAG to ensure historical facts, scientific concepts, or cultural information remain accurate and current.
Activity Create a simple diagram showing: Retrieve → Augment → Generate
Document this in Portfolio Entry #7 as an example of how AI can maintain accuracy in educational content.

B. Tree-of-Thought
C. Prompt stacking

Theme Identification (Top 3 Issues) Based on the AI analysis and your own, what are the top 3 themes that need attention?
1. Player guidance / hint system – Players get lost finding crystals 2. NPC appears only once – Game feels lonely after the intro 3. Counter doesn't help locate items – Shows progress but not direction

Portfolio Entry 10b – Theme Mapping & Planning
🟧 🟥
Rank your feedback themes by importance: “Planned actions should focus on improving player experience (e.g., flow, clarity, engagement, or functionality).”
Theme Priority Table
AI Suggestions Documentation Change 1: Add proximity hint system using Trigger Volumes Rationale: AI suggested placing invisible Trigger Volumes in a radius around each hidden crystal. When player enters the zone, a Dialogue Node triggers with text like "The light grows stronger here..." This guides players without giving exact locations, keeping the exploration fun but less frustrating. Change 2: Add a second NPC encounter in Zone 2 Rationale: AI suggested placing another Medium NPC (could be the same guide character) near the Zone 2 entrance with a Dialogue Node wired to a Trigger Volume. The NPC says something like "You're doing well, explorer. Two more Lights remain in this courtyard." This breaks up the lonely feeling and reinforces the educational narrative.
I'll implement Change 1 (hint system) first because it was mentioned by 3 out of 4 testers and has the highest impact on player experience. If players quit because they're frustrated, they won't even see the cool ending. The Trigger Volume + Dialogue Node combo is easy to build in Endstar and I already know how to wire it from my Zone 2 welcome message. Then I'll do Change 2 (second NPC) because it's quick to add and will make the game feel more alive. I can copy my existing NPC and just change the dialogue text. Change 3 (zone-specific counters) I'll skip for now because it needs multiple Billboards and more complex wiring - I'll save that for polish if I have time.
Next Steps Planning Which changes will you implement in Lesson 9 and why?

Stretch Activity – Cutscene Challenge
Encourage students to deepen immersion by adding a short cutscene to their game. Cutscenes help guide players, introduce story moments, and create atmosphere. They can build tension, reveal goals, or add personality to characters. If you finish early, try adding a cutscene — a short, non-playable sequence triggered by player actions. Use Endstar tools such as camera changes, dialogue nodes, timed events, or animated props. A cutscene in game design creates a cinematic moment for players to engage with — like an intro scene, warning message, character reveal, or mission start.
Examples: Trigger-activated camera pan revealing a new area NPC dialogue scene that introduces a challenge or clue Fade-in/fade-out moment before a major event Timed lighting or sound cue that sets the mood A short intro sequence when the player enters a new zone

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson
AI "hallucination" can be reduced by combining AI generation with reliable information retrieval. RAG helps AI access accurate, up-to-date information before generating responses.
Retrieve
AI searches reliable sources for relevant information
RAG – Retrieval Augmented Generation
Augment
AI combines retrieved facts with its knowledge
Generate
AI creates responses based on verified information
How RAG Works
Gaming Application Educational games can use RAG to ensure historical facts, scientific concepts, or cultural information remain accurate and current.

rows: Change skybox to golden sunset when player reaches Zone 2

Place decorative props (plants, lanterns, carpets) around the courtyard

Changed NPC text from "Collect 5 crystals" to "Marhaba, young explorer! Sheikh Zayed built this mosque for all people to find peace. Help me gather the 5 Lights of Knowledge hidden in the courtyards, and you will understand why." — Makes it feel welcoming and educational
Zone 2 entrance area
"Add environmental props that hint this is a special sacred space"
Added decorative lantern props and carpet props near the Zone 2 doorway, plus placed plants to frame the entrance — Creates a "wow" moment when the door opens
"The game is boring and confusing."
"The first zone felt slow because I was just walking around collecting crystals with no challenge. I didn't know where to go next after getting the first 3 crystals."
It says exactly WHICH part was boring (Zone 1) and WHY (no challenge, just walking). It also explains what was confusing (not knowing where to go).
Now I know to add more interaction in Zone 1 and maybe add a hint system or NPC dialogue pointing to the next area
"The game is boring and confusing."
"The crystal collection got repetitive after a while. Also the door mechanic confused me - I didn't realize I needed to step on the pressure plate first."
It identifies the specific mechanic that was repetitive (crystals) and the exact confusion point (pressure plate + door connection)
I can add variety to collectibles or add a visual hint near the pressure plate showing it connects to the door

There were more hints about where to find the hidden crystals. I got stuck for a while looking.

NPC only talks once at the start, then disappears - felt lonely exploring alone

The NPC gave me a hint if I was stuck for too long. Some kind of help system.
Got frustrated after 2 minutes of searching, almost quit

Optional Activity – Easter Egg Capture Sheet
ai_focus: I used AI in this lesson OR chose not to use AI I checked AI output instead of trusting it straight away I used my own judgement to make decisions
🤖 AI Literacy Reflection
Jump into Endstar Build and experiment Test with your team Fix bugs together Try new ideas Explore AI tools Choose how you want to work
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
learning_objectives: Reflection Prompts: What were the most common themes in your peer feedback? Which change do you plan to make first, and why? How confident are you that your feedback analysis reflects real player needs?
metadata.activity_description:
Activity 8.1 – Feedback Rewrite

Use the poor feedback example from the Starter (“The game is boring and confusing.”). Rewrite it to make it more useful, then complete the comparison table below.

Reflection Question What are three key elements that make feedback truly useful for game developers?

Explore: Peer Testing Circuits with AI Support

Conduct systematic peer testing using structured feedback collection and AI analysis tools.

Quick-Fix Sprint (Before Full Analysis) – 5 min Fix one small issue in Endstar before starting formal feedback analysis. Helps students apply early feedback immediately and keep momentum.

Testing Circuit Process: Form pairs and set a 5-7 minute testing timer Tester plays through the prototype completely Fill out WWW/EBI feedback forms 8.2  with specific observations Switch roles and repeat the process Collect feedback from at least 3 different testers

AI Feedback Analysis Upload your collected feedback to Claude or Gemini using this prompt:

“Summarise the main themes from this game testing feedback and rank them by importance. Here is the feedback: [paste all comments here].”

Tool Choice: Why Use Claude/Gemini Instead of ChatGPT? Claude and Gemini perform better at: grouping tester comments finding recurring issues ranking problems by importance ChatGPT is great for rewriting ideas, but Claude/Gemini are more accurate for feedback analysis .

You will paste your AI summary into the “AI Analysis Documentation” box in Activity 8.2.

Activity 8.2 – Peer Testing Results

Each tester completes one row. Aim for 3 testers minimum

AI Analysis Documentation Use Gemini/Claude/other AI to summarise your tester comments. Paste the AI summary below.

Theme Identification (Top 3 Issues) Based on the AI analysis and your own, what are the top 3 themes that need attention? _______________ _______________ _______________

Optional: insert a link to external documents if needed.

Analyse: Theme Mapping & Next Steps

Theme Mapping Activity Create sticky notes (physical or digital) for each piece of feedback Group similar feedback together Label each group with a theme name Prioritise themes by frequency and impact

Document your planned changes and rationale for the next lesson.

Use collaborative tools to map feedback themes and plan evidence-based improvements.

AI Planning Support Use this prompt to generate actionable next steps:

"Suggest 2 specific changes I could make to my game based on this feedback, keeping within the scope of Endstar's capabilities. The main issues are: [list your top 3 themes]"

Focus on changes that: Address the most common feedback Are technically feasible in Endstar Will have the biggest impact on player experience

Portfolio Entry 10a – Theme Mapping

Upload your theme map from Miro or a photo of your physical sticky note groupings.(Image, screenshot, or link from collaborative tools)

Add a link to your digital map if created online.

Portfolio Entry 10b – Theme Mapping & Planning

Rank your feedback themes by importance: “Planned actions should focus on improving player experience (e.g., flow, clarity, engagement, or functionality).”

AI Suggestions Documentation Record the AI's suggested changes: Change 1: _______________Rationale: _______________ Change 2: _______________Rationale: _______________

Next Steps Planning Which changes will you implement in Lesson 9 and why?

Optional Stretch Activity – Easter Egg Challenge

Purpose: Encourage students to use tester feedback to create a playful or surprising element in their game. Great designers don't just fix what's broken — they also surprise their players. If you finish early, try adding an Easter egg — a hidden feature or secret element inspired by your feedback. An Easter egg in game design means a fun secret for players to discover — like a hidden door, collectible, message, or sound.

Examples: Hidden trigger block opening a new path Reward prop appearing after a specific action Secret sound effect or dialogue revealed by exploration Colour or lighting change rewarding discovery

🎥 Secret Developer Easter Eggs in Video Games!

🎥 Why You Should Add Easter Eggs To Your Games?

Optional Activity – Easter Egg Capture Sheet

Upload a screenshot or short clip of your Easter egg. Include a brief caption describing your hidden easter egg.

insert video clip or digital drawing link here.

Extension / Adaptive Task – Endstar Warm-Up

Activity Create a scorecard rating your AI feedback analysis on these four criteria. Take a photo of your scorecard for your portfolio. This critical evaluation skill helps you become a more effective AI collaborator by recognizing when AI analysis needs human oversight or correction.

Accuracy Did the AI correctly identify the main themes?

Evaluating AI Outputs – Accuracy, Relevance, Bias, Clarity When AI analyses your feedback, it's important to evaluate the quality of its analysis. Not all AI summaries are equally useful or accurate.

Evaluation Criteria Rate AI outputs on a scale of 1-4 for each criterion:

Relevance Are the suggestions actually helpful for your project?

Bias Did the AI show preference for certain types of feedback?

Clarity Are the recommendations clear and actionable?

Portfolio Entry / Next Steps

Upload your artefact evidence and write one sentence: 'Today I demonstrated…'

Prototype V2 Expanded Mechanics

Evidence of how new or improved mechanics create a more functional and challenging game.

Prototype V2 Immersion Enhancement

Captures the aesthetic and atmospheric upgrades that keep players engaged in the story.

A visual breakdown of user feedback used to find recurring strengths and weaknesses.

A roadmap for your final changes based on prioritized data and AI-assisted insights.

Next Steps: Verify Evidence: Ensure every portfolio entry includes a clear, labeled screenshot or link that directly demonstrates your progress and design choices. Prioritize Refinements: Execute your "Priority 1" planned actions to improve gameplay mechanics and immersion based on your research and feedback. Align with Goals: Cross-check your final prototype against the success criteria in your Rewritten Brief to ensure you have met your project's objectives.

teacher_notes.notes: Teacher Note: Rewrite poor comment exercise. Share improved feedback with partner.

Encourage students to focus on fixing the most important 1–2 issues first — like the F1 '6% of data' idea. Don't try to fix everything at once. Students must use the themes identified from Activity 8.2 as the starting point for their clustering Teacher Note: Use Jamboard/Miro for theme mapping. AI prompt for actionable next steps. Focus on feasible changes in Endstar scope.

Teacher Note: Encourage students to provide clear, labelled images or sketches. Remind them to connect their concept board to the "Define" stage of the Product Development Cycle and explain how it shapes their game brief.
*Curriculum Alignment: UK D&T KS3 1d | AQA 3.1.4 | IB Criterion D Evaluating*

Teacher Note: Frame as studio play-test session (not graded). Model WWW/EBI feedback format. Require teams to set one test objective. Use AI tools to cluster themes.➡ These insights feed directly into Lesson 9 – Iteration & Refinement.

Teacher Note: Portfolio entries demonstrate completion of Define phase. Focus feedback on clarity of brief elements and quality of team planning. These documents will guide students through upcoming design phases.

slides.content:
Term 2
Lesson 8

Lesson 8: Peer Testing & Feedback Analysis
In this stage, students conduct structured peer tests, gather data, identify patterns, and use AI to summarise and visualise feedback for evidence-based design improvements. This stage mirrors real-world game development, where designers test their prototypes with players outside their core team to gather unbiased, customer-like insights.
Product Development Cycle – Term 2 | Prototype → Test & Evaluate Phase
“Unlike Lesson 7’s internal testing, this lesson focuses on feedback from peers outside your team to simulate real player testing.”
UAE Link: This lesson reflects a culture of continuous improvement and collaboration in the UAE by using peer feedback and testing to inform better design decisions. Reflection Question: How can peer feedback help improve the effectiveness of a digital solution?

Big Question / Big Picture
How do we know when our design is really working for players?

Lesson 8: Learning Objectives
By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

Lesson 8: Success Criteria & Self-Assessment
We will know we are successful when we can …
Aligned to Product Development Cycle – Test & Evaluate Phase.

Starter: What Makes Useful Feedback?
Discussion Questions What’s the difference between helpful and unhelpful feedback? What makes feedback useful for improving a game? Feedback Rewrite Challenge Look at this poor feedback comment: “The game is boring and confusing.” Remember: Useful feedback is specific , observable , and linked to player experience . Rewrite it to be more useful by being specific about: What exact part of the game felt boring or repetitive? What, specifically, was confusing (controls, goals, layout, story, etc.)? What change would make this section more fun or clearer for the player?

Activity 8.1 – Feedback Rewrite
🟧 🟥
Use the poor feedback example from the Starter (“The game is boring and confusing.”). Rewrite it to make it more useful, then complete the comparison table below.
Feedback Comparison Table

Accuracy Did the AI correctly identify the main themes?
Evaluating AI Outputs – Accuracy, Relevance, Bias, Clarity When AI analyses your feedback, it's important to evaluate the quality of its analysis. Not all AI summaries are equally useful or accurate.
Evaluation Criteria Rate AI outputs on a scale of 1-4 for each criterion:
Relevance Are the suggestions actually helpful for your project?
Bias Did the AI show preference for certain types of feedback?
Clarity Are the recommendations clear and actionable?

Term 2
Lesson 8

Lesson 8: Peer Testing & Feedback Analysis
In this stage, students conduct structured peer tests, gather data, identify patterns, and use AI to summarise and visualise feedback for evidence-based design improvements. This stage mirrors real-world game development, where designers test their prototypes with players outside their core team to gather unbiased, customer-like insights.
Product Development Cycle – Term 2 | Prototype → Test & Evaluate Phase
“Unlike Lesson 7’s internal testing, this lesson focuses on feedback from peers outside your team to simulate real player testing.”
UAE Link: This lesson reflects a culture of continuous improvement and collaboration in the UAE by using peer feedback and testing to inform better design decisions. Reflection Question: How can peer feedback help improve the effectiveness of a digital solution?

Big Question / Big Picture
How do we know when our design is really working for players?

Lesson 8: Learning Objectives
By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

Lesson 8: Success Criteria & Self-Assessment
We will know we are successful when we can …
Aligned to Product Development Cycle – Test & Evaluate Phase.

Starter: What Makes Useful Feedback?
Discussion Questions What’s the difference between helpful and unhelpful feedback? What makes feedback useful for improving a game? Feedback Rewrite Challenge Look at this poor feedback comment: “The game is boring and confusing.” Remember: Useful feedback is specific , observable , and linked to player experience . Rewrite it to be more useful by being specific about: What exact part of the game felt boring or repetitive? What, specifically, was confusing (controls, goals, layout, story, etc.)? What change would make this section more fun or clearer for the player?

Activity 8.1 – Feedback Rewrite
🟧 🟥
Use the poor feedback example from the Starter (“The game is boring and confusing.”). Rewrite it to make it more useful, then complete the comparison table below.
Feedback Comparison Table
Reflection Question What are three key elements that make feedback truly useful for game developers?

Explore: Peer Testing Circuits with AI Support
Conduct systematic peer testing using structured feedback collection and AI analysis tools.
Quick-Fix Sprint (Before Full Analysis) – 5 min Fix one small issue in Endstar before starting formal feedback analysis. Helps students apply early feedback immediately and keep momentum.
Testing Circuit Process: Form pairs and set a 5-7 minute testing timer Tester plays through the prototype completely Fill out WWW/EBI feedback forms 8.2  with specific observations Switch roles and repeat the process Collect feedback from at least 3 different testers
AI Feedback Analysis Upload your collected feedback to Claude or Gemini using this prompt:
“Summarise the main themes from this game testing feedback and rank them by importance. Here is the feedback: [paste all comments here].”
Tool Choice: Why Use Claude/Gemini Instead of ChatGPT? Claude and Gemini perform better at: grouping tester comments finding recurring issues ranking problems by importance ChatGPT is great for rewriting ideas, but Claude/Gemini are more accurate for feedback analysis .
You will paste your AI summary into the “AI Analysis Documentation” box in Activity 8.2.

Activity 8.2 – Peer Testing Results
🟩🟧
Each tester completes one row. Aim for 3 testers minimum

Optional: insert a link to external documents if needed.

Analyse: Theme Mapping & Next Steps
Theme Mapping Activity Create sticky notes (physical or digital) for each piece of feedback Group similar feedback together Label each group with a theme name Prioritise themes by frequency and impact
Document your planned changes and rationale for the next lesson.
Use collaborative tools to map feedback themes and plan evidence-based improvements.
AI Planning Support Use this prompt to generate actionable next steps:
"Suggest 2 specific changes I could make to my game based on this feedback, keeping within the scope of Endstar's capabilities. The main issues are: [list your top 3 themes]"
Focus on changes that: Address the most common feedback Are technically feasible in Endstar Will have the biggest impact on player experience

Optional Stretch Activity – Easter Egg Challenge
Purpose: Encourage students to use tester feedback to create a playful or surprising element in their game. Great designers don't just fix what's broken — they also surprise their players. If you finish early, try adding an Easter egg — a hidden feature or secret element inspired by your feedback. An Easter egg in game design means a fun secret for players to discover — like a hidden door, collectible, message, or sound.
Examples: Hidden trigger block opening a new path Reward prop appearing after a specific action Secret sound effect or dialogue revealed by exploration Colour or lighting change rewarding discovery

metadata.activity_description:
Activity 9.1 – Impact vs Effort Action Planning

Choose the top 2 changes for today:

Change 1: Add Trigger Volumes with hint dialogue near hidden crystals.

Why:  This is High Impact + Low Effort. 3 out of 4 testers got lost finding crystals, so this fixes the biggest problem. I already know how to wire Trigger Volumes and Dialogue Nodes from my Zone 2 welcome message, so I can build it fast.

Change 2:  Add second NPC in Zone 2 with encouragement dialogue

Why:  This is Medium Impact + Low Effort. It only takes a few minutes to copy my existing NPC and write new dialogue text. It makes the game feel less lonely and keeps the educational story going throughout the experience.

Use your three improvements from Portfolio Task 10 (Lesson 8). Place each idea in the matrix according to:

Delete the example boxes when completing your own work.

|  | Low Effort | Medium Effort | High Effort |

| Medium Impact | Add second NPC in Zone 2 with encouragement dialogue |  |  |

| High Impact | Add Trigger Volumes with hint dialogue near hidden crystals |  | Create automatic timed hint system - if player hasn't found a crystal in 60 seconds, Timer Rule triggers NPC to walk over and give a clue |

Place Idea 1, Idea 2 and Idea 3 into the correct boxes.

Use the examples shown to guide you.

Portfolio Entry 11a– Prototype V3 Refinement Implementation

Screenshot A – BEFORE: Hidden Crystal Area (No Hints)

This is Zone 1 before I added the hint system. You can see the crystal is hidden behind the pillar but there's nothing to help players find it. Most testers walked right past it and got frustrated.

Screenshot B – AFTER: Trigger Volume + Hint Dialogue Added

Now I placed a Trigger Volume near the pillar entrance and wired it to a Dialogue Node. When players walk close, Wise Elder NPC says "The light often hides in the shadows of great pillars..." This gives them a hint without telling them exactly where it is.

Wiring: Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node (OnTriggered → GiveInstruction)

Screenshot C – BEFORE: Zone 2 (Empty, No NPCs)

Zone 2 was just tiles and crystals with no characters. Players said it felt lonely and they didn't know if they were going the right way.

Screenshot D – AFTER: Second NPC Added with Encouragement

I added a Medium NPC (Mosque Guide character) in Zone 2 with a Trigger Volume. When you enter Zone 2, he says "You're doing well, young seeker. The mosque holds three more lights in this sacred space." This tells players how many crystals are in this zone and keeps them motivated.

Wiring: Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node (OnTriggered → GiveInstruction)

Dialogue Node Settings: NPC Reference = Mosque Guide NPC, Text = encouragement message

Upload your before-and-after screenshots showing the improvements you made today. Label your screenshots (e.g., Screenshot A, Screenshot B) — you will reference them in the log on the next slide.

Portfolio Entry 11b – Prototype V3 Implementation Log

Document each change you made: Refer to your uploaded screenshots from Slide 11a (e.g., “Screenshot A” / “Screenshot B”). Do not upload images in the table.

1. Which change had the most noticeable impact on player experience?

The hint system near the hidden crystal. Before, players just walked past and got frustrated. Now they get the Wise Elder's hint and actually look around the pillar area.

2. How did AI suggestions help or hinder your improvement process?

AI helped me figure out the right wiring (Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node with OnTriggered → GiveInstruction). It also suggested using contextual hints instead of obvious signs which keeps the game feeling like a real mosque.

3. What surprised you about the implementation process?

How fast it was. I thought adding NPCs and dialogue would take forever but once I understood the wiring pattern it only took like 25 minutes total for both changes.

| Change Made | Before (reference or short note) | After (reference or short note) | AI Suggestion Used | Impact Assessment | Next Steps |

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| Added Trigger Volume + hint dialogue near hidden crystal in Zone 1 | Screenshot A: Empty area near pillar, no guidance | Screenshot B: Trigger Volume placed, Dialogue Node wired to Wise Elder NPC | "Use Trigger Volume near difficult areas to give contextual hints without breaking immersion" | High Impact – Players now get a hint before passing the crystal | Add hints to Zone 2 and Zone 3 hidden crystals |

| Added second NPC (Mosque Guide) in Zone 2 with encouragement dialogue | Screenshot C: Zone 2 empty, no characters | Screenshot D: Mosque Guide NPC at zone entrance with Trigger Volume + Dialogue Node | "Add NPCs at zone transitions to guide players and show progress" | Medium Impact – Zone feels more alive, but NPC placement could be better | Reposition NPC so players see him immediately when entering |

High Impact – Clearly improved player experience (flow, clarity, engagement, pacing, or functionality).

Medium Impact – Some improvement, but still needs refinement.

Low Impact – Minimal improvement or needs major changes.

Activity 9.2 – Impact Evaluation

Evaluate the effectiveness of your changes:

| Original Feedback Theme | Change Made | Addresses Feedback? (Y/N) | New Issues Created | Overall Impact Rating (1-5) |

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| Players can't find crystals / need hints (3/4 testers) | Added Trigger Volume + Dialogue Node with Wise Elder hint near hidden crystal in Zone 1 | Yes | Hint might be too obvious for some players | 4 |

| NPC only appears once / Zone 2 feels empty (1/4 testers) | Added Mosque Guide NPC at Zone 2 entrance with encouragement dialogue | Yes | NPC placement blocks path slightly | 3 |

Paste your AI evaluation response here:

"Comparing your before and after versions: The hint system is a clear improvement for player guidance - contextual dialogue feels natural in a mosque setting and doesn't break immersion like a floating arrow would. Players now receive subtle guidance without feeling hand-held. The second NPC addition improves engagement by making Zone 2 feel inhabited and giving players confirmation they're progressing. However, both changes focus on early-game areas - Zone 3 still lacks guidance. Consider extending these patterns to later zones for consistent player support throughout."

Ready for next iteration cycle

| Yousef Al Zaabi | Story/Marketing Lead | Write NPC dialogue, create game description, plan trailer, handle publishing | Good writer, understands the Sheikh Zayed Mosque theme | Write dialogue for new Zone 2 NPC, draft game description for publishing |

Use this table to ensure balanced workload distribution and clear accountability for upcoming milestones.

| Risk | Impact Level (High/Medium/Low) | Likelihood (High/Medium/Low) | Response Plan |

| Team member absence | High | Medium | Cross-train team members so anyone can do basic tasks. Keep shared notes on what each person is working on. If someone is absent, PM redistributes their tasks. |

| Technical difficulties | Medium | Medium | Test wiring in small sections. Ask teacher or use AI for debugging help if stuck. |

| Scope creep | High | High | Stick to our 3-zone plan. No new zones or major features. Use a "future ideas" list for anything we want to add later but won't do now. |

| Time constraints | High | Medium | Follow the timeline strictly. If we fall behind, cut polish tasks first and focus on core mechanics working. Weekly check-ins to track progress. |

This self-reflection skill helps you become a more effective AI collaborator by improving the quality of your requests and getting better results from AI tools.

Context Did you provide enough background information?

Output Did you specify the format or type of response you wanted?

Iteration Did you refine your prompt based on the results?

Challenge Description Revisit one unresolved issue from Lesson 7 and fix it collaboratively or with AI support. This could be a mechanic that still doesn't work properly, a visual element that needs improvement, or a flow issue that affects player experience. Purpose Apply systematic debugging methods and iterative problem-solving skills developed during the refinement process. Test whether your improved debugging approach from this lesson can solve previously challenging issues. Collaborative Approach Work in pairs to tackle the problem from different angles - one person focuses on technical implementation while the other considers player experience impact.

lessons.description_of_activities:
Activity 9.1 – Impact vs Effort Action Planning

Choose the top 2 changes for today:

Change 1: Add Trigger Volumes with hint dialogue near hidden crystals.

Why:  This is High Impact + Low Effort. 3 out of 4 testers got lost finding crystals, so this fixes the biggest problem. I already know how to wire Trigger Volumes and Dialogue Nodes from my Zone 2 welcome message, so I can build it fast.

Change 2:  Add second NPC in Zone 2 with encouragement dialogue

Why:  This is Medium Impact + Low Effort. It only takes a few minutes to copy my existing NPC and write new dialogue text. It makes the game feel less lonely and keeps the educational story going throughout the experience.

Use your three improvements from Portfolio Task 10 (Lesson 8). Place each idea in the matrix according to:

Delete the example boxes when completing your own work.

|  | Low Effort | Medium Effort | High Effort |

| Medium Impact | Add second NPC in Zone 2 with encouragement dialogue |  |  |

| High Impact | Add Trigger Volumes with hint dialogue near hidden crystals |  | Create automatic timed hint system - if player hasn't found a crystal in 60 seconds, Timer Rule triggers NPC to walk over and give a clue |

Place Idea 1, Idea 2 and Idea 3 into the correct boxes.

Use the examples shown to guide you.

Portfolio Entry 11a– Prototype V3 Refinement Implementation

Screenshot A – BEFORE: Hidden Crystal Area (No Hints)

This is Zone 1 before I added the hint system. You can see the crystal is hidden behind the pillar but there's nothing to help players find it. Most testers walked right past it and got frustrated.

Screenshot B – AFTER: Trigger Volume + Hint Dialogue Added

Now I placed a Trigger Volume near the pillar entrance and wired it to a Dialogue Node. When players walk close, Wise Elder NPC says "The light often hides in the shadows of great pillars..." This gives them a hint without telling them exactly where it is.

Wiring: Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node (OnTriggered → GiveInstruction)

Screenshot C – BEFORE: Zone 2 (Empty, No NPCs)

Zone 2 was just tiles and crystals with no characters. Players said it felt lonely and they didn't know if they were going the right way.

Screenshot D – AFTER: Second NPC Added with Encouragement

I added a Medium NPC (Mosque Guide character) in Zone 2 with a Trigger Volume. When you enter Zone 2, he says "You're doing well, young seeker. The mosque holds three more lights in this sacred space." This tells players how many crystals are in this zone and keeps them motivated.

Wiring: Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node (OnTriggered → GiveInstruction)

Dialogue Node Settings: NPC Reference = Mosque Guide NPC, Text = encouragement message

Upload your before-and-after screenshots showing the improvements you made today. Label your screenshots (e.g., Screenshot A, Screenshot B) — you will reference them in the log on the next slide.

Portfolio Entry 11b – Prototype V3 Implementation Log

Document each change you made: Refer to your uploaded screenshots from Slide 11a (e.g., “Screenshot A” / “Screenshot B”). Do not upload images in the table.

1. Which change had the most noticeable impact on player experience?

The hint system near the hidden crystal. Before, players just walked past and got frustrated. Now they get the Wise Elder's hint and actually look around the pillar area.

2. How did AI suggestions help or hinder your improvement process?

AI helped me figure out the right wiring (Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node with OnTriggered → GiveInstruction). It also suggested using contextual hints instead of obvious signs which keeps the game feeling like a real mosque.

3. What surprised you about the implementation process?

How fast it was. I thought adding NPCs and dialogue would take forever but once I understood the wiring pattern it only took like 25 minutes total for both changes.

| Added second NPC (Mosque Guide) in Zone 2 with encouragement dialogue | Screenshot C: Zone 2 empty, no characters | Screenshot D: Mosque Guide NPC at zone entrance with Trigger Volume + Dialogue Node | "Add NPCs at zone transitions to guide players and show progress" | Medium Impact – Zone feels more alive, but NPC placement could be better | Reposition NPC so players see him immediately when entering |

High Impact – Clearly improved player experience (flow, clarity, engagement, pacing, or functionality).

Medium Impact – Some improvement, but still needs refinement.

Low Impact – Minimal improvement or needs major changes.

Evaluate the effectiveness of your changes:

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| NPC only appears once / Zone 2 feels empty (1/4 testers) | Added Mosque Guide NPC at Zone 2 entrance with encouragement dialogue | Yes | NPC placement blocks path slightly | 3 |

Paste your AI evaluation response here:

Players now receive subtle guidance without feeling hand-held. The second NPC addition improves engagement by making Zone 2 feel inhabited and giving players confirmation they're progressing. However, both changes focus on early-game areas - Zone 3 still lacks guidance. Consider extending these patterns to later zones for consistent player support throughout."

Ready for next iteration cycle

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson

Clarity Was your request clear and specific?

Prompt Scorecard – Evaluating Your Own AI Interactions

Activity Choose one AI prompt you used today and rate it using these criteria. Then rewrite it as a version 2.0 that addresses any weaknesses you identified. This self-reflection skill helps you become a more effective AI collaborator by improving the quality of your requests and getting better results from AI tools.

Effective AI collaboration requires evaluating not just AI outputs, but also the quality of your own prompts. Better prompts lead to better results.

Prompt Quality Criteria Rate your own prompts on a scale of 1-4 for each criterion:

Context Did you provide enough background information?

Output Did you specify the format or type of response you wanted?

Iteration Did you refine your prompt based on the results?

Extension / Adaptive Task – Endstar Warm-Up

Bug Hunt Reloaded If time allows — or if the class would benefit from a more hands-on, active task — this Endstar Warm-Up connects iteration skills to collaborative problem-solving. Challenge Description Revisit one unresolved issue from Lesson 7 and fix it collaboratively or with AI support. This could be a mechanic that still doesn't work properly, a visual element that needs improvement, or a flow issue that affects player experience. Purpose Apply systematic debugging methods and iterative problem-solving skills developed during the refinement process. Test whether your improved debugging approach from this lesson can solve previously challenging issues. Collaborative Approach Work in pairs to tackle the problem from different angles - one person focuses on technical implementation while the other considers player experience impact.

‹#›

Term 2

Lesson 9

Activity 9.1 – Impact vs Effort Action Planning

‹#›

Selected Actions
Choose the top 2 changes for today:
Change 1: Add Trigger Volumes with hint dialogue near hidden crystals.
Why:  This is High Impact + Low Effort. 3 out of 4 testers got lost finding crystals, so this fixes the biggest problem. I already know how to wire Trigger Volumes and Dialogue Nodes from my Zone 2 welcome message, so I can build it fast.
Change 2:  Add second NPC in Zone 2 with encouragement dialogue
Why:  This is Medium Impact + Low Effort. It only takes a few minutes to copy my existing NPC and write new dialogue text. It makes the game feel less lonely and keeps the educational story going throughout the experience.

🟧🟥

Instructions
Use your three improvements from Portfolio Task 10 (Lesson 8). Place each idea in the matrix according to:
Impact: High/Medium/Low
Effort: Low/Medium/High
Delete the example boxes when completing your own work.


|  | Low Effort | Medium Effort | High Effort |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Low Impact |  |  |  |
| Medium Impact | Add second NPC in Zone 2 with encouragement dialogue |  |  |
| High Impact | Add Trigger Volumes with hint dialogue near hidden crystals |  | Create automatic timed hint system - if player hasn't found a crystal in 60 seconds, Timer Rule triggers NPC to walk over and give a clue |

Place Idea 1, Idea 2 and Idea 3 into the correct boxes.
Use the examples shown to guide you.

Portfolio Entry 11a– Prototype V3 Refinement Implementation

‹#›

Screenshot A – BEFORE: Hidden Crystal Area (No Hints)

  This is Zone 1 before I added the hint system. You can see the crystal is hidden behind the pillar but there's nothing to help players find it. Most testers walked right past it and got frustrated.

Screenshot B – AFTER: Trigger Volume + Hint Dialogue Added

  Now I placed a Trigger Volume near the pillar entrance and wired it to a Dialogue Node. When players walk close, Wise Elder NPC says "The light often hides in the shadows of great pillars..." This gives them a hint without telling them exactly where it is.

  Wiring: Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node (OnTriggered → GiveInstruction)

Screenshot C – BEFORE: Zone 2 (Empty, No NPCs)

  Zone 2 was just tiles and crystals with no characters. Players said it felt lonely and they didn't know if they were going the right way.

Screenshot D – AFTER: Second NPC Added with Encouragement

  I added a Medium NPC (Mosque Guide character) in Zone 2 with a Trigger Volume. When you enter Zone 2, he says "You're doing well, young seeker. The mosque holds three more lights in this sacred space." This tells players how many crystals are in this zone and keeps them motivated.

  Wiring: Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node (OnTriggered → GiveInstruction)
  Dialogue Node Settings: NPC Reference = Mosque Guide NPC, Text = encouragement message

Upload your before-and-after screenshots showing the improvements you made today. Label your screenshots (e.g., Screenshot A, Screenshot B) — you will reference them in the log on the next slide.

🟧 🟥

Portfolio Entry 11b – Prototype V3 Implementation Log

‹#›

🟩🟧

Document each change you made: Refer to your uploaded screenshots from Slide 11a (e.g., “Screenshot A” / “Screenshot B”). Do not upload images in the table.

Implementation Log

Reflection Questions
 1. Which change had the most noticeable impact on player experience?
  The hint system near the hidden crystal. Before, players just walked past and got frustrated. Now they get the Wise Elder's hint and actually look around the pillar area.
2. How did AI suggestions help or hinder your improvement process?
  AI helped me figure out the right wiring (Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node with OnTriggered → GiveInstruction). It also suggested using contextual hints instead of obvious signs which keeps the game feeling like a real mosque.
3. What surprised you about the implementation process?
  How fast it was. I thought adding NPCs and dialogue would take forever but once I understood the wiring pattern it only took like 25 minutes total for both changes.

| Added Trigger Volume + hint dialogue near hidden crystal in Zone 1 | Screenshot A: Empty area near pillar, no guidance | Screenshot B: Trigger Volume placed, Dialogue Node wired to Wise Elder NPC | "Use Trigger Volume near difficult areas to give contextual hints without breaking immersion" | High Impact – Players now get a hint before passing the crystal | Add hints to Zone 2 and Zone 3 hidden crystals |
| Added second NPC (Mosque Guide) in Zone 2 with encouragement dialogue | Screenshot C: Zone 2 empty, no characters | Screenshot D: Mosque Guide NPC at zone entrance with Trigger Volume + Dialogue Node | "Add NPCs at zone transitions to guide players and show progress" | Medium Impact – Zone feels more alive, but NPC placement could be better | Reposition NPC so players see him immediately when entering |

Impact Assessment Guide:
High Impact – Clearly improved player experience (flow, clarity, engagement, pacing, or functionality).
Medium Impact – Some improvement, but still needs refinement.
Low Impact – Minimal improvement or needs major changes.

Activity 9.2 – Impact Evaluation

‹#›

🟩🟧

Evaluate the effectiveness of your changes:

Impact Assessment Table


| Original Feedback Theme | Change Made | Addresses Feedback? (Y/N) | New Issues Created | Overall Impact Rating (1-5) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Players can't find crystals / need hints (3/4 testers) | Added Trigger Volume + Dialogue Node with Wise Elder hint near hidden crystal in Zone 1 | Yes | Hint might be too obvious for some players | 4 |
| NPC only appears once / Zone 2 feels empty (1/4 testers) | Added Mosque Guide NPC at Zone 2 entrance with encouragement dialogue | Yes | NPC placement blocks path slightly | 3 |

AI Analysis Documentation
Paste your AI evaluation response here:

"Comparing your before and after versions: The hint system is a clear improvement for player guidance - contextual dialogue feels natural in a mosque setting and doesn't break immersion like a floating arrow would. Players now receive subtle guidance without feeling hand-held. The second NPC addition improves engagement by making Zone 2 feel inhabited and giving players confirmation they're progressing. However, both changes focus on early-game areas - Zone 3 still lacks guidance. Consider extending these patterns to later zones for consistent player support throughout."

Validation Checklist

Change addresses original feedback
No major unintended consequences
Player flow improved
Ready for next iteration cycle

1 = No improvement
3 = Moderate improvement
5 = Major improvement in flow, clarity, or engagement

🟧 🟥

Portfolio Entry 11b – Prototype V3 Implementation Log
🟩🟧
Document each change you made: Refer to your uploaded screenshots from Slide 11a (e.g., “Screenshot A” / “Screenshot B”). Do not upload images in the table.
Implementation Log
Reflection Questions 1. Which change had the most noticeable impact on player experience? The hint system near the hidden crystal. Before, players just walked past and got frustrated. Now they get the Wise Elder's hint and actually look around the pillar area. 2. How did AI suggestions help or hinder your improvement process? AI helped me figure out the right wiring (Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node with OnTriggered → GiveInstruction). It also suggested using contextual hints instead of obvious signs which keeps the game feeling like a real mosque. 3. What surprised you about the implementation process? How fast it was. I thought adding NPCs and dialogue would take forever but once I understood the wiring pattern it only took like 25 minutes total for both changes.
Impact Assessment Guide: High Impact – Clearly improved player experience (flow, clarity, engagement, pacing, or functionality). Medium Impact – Some improvement, but still needs refinement. Low Impact – Minimal improvement or needs major changes.

Players now receive subtle guidance without feeling hand-held. The second NPC addition improves engagement by making Zone 2 feel inhabited and giving players confirmation they're progressing. However, both changes focus on early-game areas - Zone 3 still lacks guidance. Consider extending these patterns to later zones for consistent player support throughout."

Lesson 9

Lesson 9: Iteration & Refinement - Turning Feedback into Action
Product Development Cycle – Term 2 | Test & Evaluate (Iteration & Improvement)
In this stage, students apply feedback to improve flow and clarity, refine mechanics and visuals, use AI to evaluate design consistency, and document improvements with measurable impact. By the end of this lesson, you will produce Prototype v3 — an improved version of your game based on prioritised feedback.
UAE Link: This lesson reinforces evidence-based improvement by turning feedback into measurable refinements in performance and user experience, reflecting practices used in digital product development in the UAE. Reflection Question: Why is iteration essential when developing high-quality digital products?

Big Question / Big Picture
How do small changes lead to big improvements?

Lesson 9: Learning Objectives
By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:
Apply Peer Feedback Apply peer feedback to refine your prototype by making meaningful improvements to mechanics, visuals, or flow.
Evaluate Design Refinements Evaluate the impact of your refinements using clear before-and-after comparisons of player experience.

Lesson 9: Success Criteria & Self-Assessment
We will know we are successful when we can …
Implement Meaningful Changes Apply at least two meaningful feedback-driven updates to your prototype.
Evaluate Improvements Use before-and-after evidence to explain how your refinements improved player experience (such as flow, clarity, or engagement).
Aligned to Product Development Cycle – Test & Evaluate (Iteration & Improvement).

Starter: From Feedback to Focus
Action Planning From your Lesson 8 feedback analysis, list 3 specific actions you could take to improve your prototype: Rank these by impact vs effort - which will give you the biggest improvement for the least work?
Review Your Lesson 8 Analysis Look back at your feedback themes and AI analysis from the previous lesson. Which feedback themes from Lesson 8 should guide your improvements today, and why?
Note: Review Lesson 8 feedback first. List 3 actions, rank by impact vs effort. Choose biggest improvement for least work.
Quick Starter – Spot the AI: Students choose which tasks AI should do (✓) and which they should not (✘): Identify broken rule chain (✓) Choose colour scheme (✘) Suggest environment improvements (✓) Decide storyline direction (✘)

Activity 9.1 – Impact vs Effort Action Planning
Selected Actions Choose the top 2 changes for today: Change 1: _______________ Why: _______________ Change 2: _______________ Why: _______________
🟧🟥
Instructions Use your three improvements from Portfolio Task 10 (Lesson 8). Place each idea in the matrix according to: Impact : High/Medium/Low Effort: Low/Medium/High Delete the example boxes when completing your own work.
Place Idea 1, Idea 2 and Idea 3 into the correct boxes. Use the examples shown to guide you.

Data Gather all player or peer feedback.
AI Analysis Use Gemini / Claude to find recurring themes.
6 % Key Insights Focus only on what truly matters
Iteration Apply small, meaningful changes in Endstar .
Improvement Test, document, and measure results.
Inspiration: How F1 Uses AI to Win Races
Formula 1 teams collect massive amounts of race data — tire temperature, fuel use, aerodynamics — but they only act on the small fraction that truly changes results. Like them, you analyse feedback to decide which 6 % of insights will make the biggest impact on your game.
In Formula 1, cars collect 100 GB of data per lap — but teams can only send 6 % of it to the pit wall. Their AIs decide which data matters most in real time. Designers do the same: focus only on the feedback that truly improves the player experience.
If you could only act on 6 % of your feedback today, which changes would you prioritise — and why?
Explore more: How Formula 1 Does AI — Ruben Harris (2025)
AI Consideration: Designers choose what data matters. Sending too much data confuses AI — the right 6% matters most.

Explore: Refinement Sprint
Implement your chosen improvements systematically. Focus on one change at a time to measure impact effectively. These improvements will form your Prototype v3.
AI Consideration: Designers choose what data matters. Sending too much data confuses AI — the right 6% matters most.
Implementation Process Take "before" screenshots of areas you plan to change Implement your first priority change Test the change immediately Document what improved and what didn't Repeat for your second priority change
AI Enhancement Prompts Use an AI assistant to help guide improvements. If you wish you can use these prompts to help.
For Difficulty/Pacing Issues: "Suggest a small tweak to make this challenge more rewarding without making it too easy. The current issue is: [describe problem]" For Flow/Navigation Problems: "How can I improve the pacing of this section to keep players engaged? Current feedback says: [paste relevant feedback]" For Visual/Audio Enhancement: "Suggest ways to make this interaction feel more satisfying using Endstar's available tools."
Documentation Requirements: Before/after screenshots for each change Notes on what you modified and why Initial assessment of improvement impact

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson
Clarity Was your request clear and specific?
Prompt Scorecard – Evaluating Your Own AI Interactions
Activity Choose one AI prompt you used today and rate it using these criteria. Then rewrite it as a version 2.0 that addresses any weaknesses you identified. This self-reflection skill helps you become a more effective AI collaborator by improving the quality of your requests and getting better results from AI tools.
Effective AI collaboration requires evaluating not just AI outputs, but also the quality of your own prompts. Better prompts lead to better results.
Prompt Quality Criteria Rate your own prompts on a scale of 1-4 for each criterion:
Context Did you provide enough background information?
Output Did you specify the format or type of response you wanted?
Iteration Did you refine your prompt based on the results?

Extension / Adaptive Task – Endstar Warm-Up
Bug Hunt Reloaded If time allows — or if the class would benefit from a more hands-on, active task — this Endstar Warm-Up connects iteration skills to collaborative problem-solving. Challenge Description Revisit one unresolved issue from Lesson 7 and fix it collaboratively or with AI support. This could be a mechanic that still doesn't work properly, a visual element that needs improvement, or a flow issue that affects player experience. Purpose Apply systematic debugging methods and iterative problem-solving skills developed during the refinement process. Test whether your improved debugging approach from this lesson can solve previously challenging issues. Collaborative Approach Work in pairs to tackle the problem from different angles - one person focuses on technical implementation while the other considers player experience impact.

Why should AI-generated feedback not replace real player testing?
A. AI lacks access to playtest tools
B. AI cannot simulate lived player experience and values
C. AI feedback is always generic
D. AI cannot process emotions

B. Data bias

A team adjusts its design solely to match AI recommendations, ignoring user feedback.

Which practice BEST ensures AI supports rather than overrides human judgement?
A. Increasing output variety
B. Trusting the most detailed response
C. Using AI suggestions as inputs for human evaluation
D. Re-prompting until answers agree
