metadata.activity_description:
Starter: Think Like a Product Team

Andrew Ng is one of the world’s leading AI experts and product innovators. His point is simple: building a great product isn’t mainly about coding — it’s about making the right decisions at the right time In real game studios, teams spend most of their time: choosing which features matter most defining clear team roles and responsibilities planning timelines, risks, and owners deciding what to improve or fix first Today, your team is doing the same thing — thinking like real product managers as you define roles, strengthen accountability, and plan the next development stage of your game.

👉 This lesson connects modern product-management thinking with game development — helping your team work more clearly, efficiently, and collaboratively.

🎥 Optional: Want to see how a real game studios plan features?

Quick Starter Task (1–2 minutes): Turn to a partner and discuss: Which of these product-team skills does your group already do well? Which one do you need to strengthen today?

Explore: Introducing the Project Manager

From Lesson 10 onward, one team member will take on the Project Manager role to guide the team through production in Term 2 and Term 3.

The Project Manager helps the team: organise tasks and milestones manage the project timeline coordinate responsibilities track progress and risks support communication ensure the game stays on schedule

This role is added on top of each student's main creative role (Designer, Programmer, Art Lead, or Story/Marketing Lead). 👉 Choose the teammate who is best suited to guide the project forward. 👉 Next, you will update your Team Roles Table to include the Project Manager role and clarify everyone’s responsibilities for the upcoming milestones.

Activity 10.1 – Team Roles Table

Include your new Project Manager and update each teammate’s responsibilities for the next milestone.

Use this table to ensure balanced workload distribution and clear accountability for upcoming milestones.

Analyse: Creating the Project Timeline

Your timeline must cover Week 5 and Week 6 of Term 2, plus the first 2 weeks of Term 3 (Refine stage). • List the key milestones for your game (builds, playtests, polish, hand-in, etc.). • Choose realistic dates for each milestone. • Assign an owner for every milestone. • Note any major risks or dependencies between tasks. You’ll capture this in Activity 10.2 (Project Timeline Table) and Activity 10.3 (Risk & Response Table) .

🎥 Production Management and Kanban Guide

Activity 10.2 – Project Timeline Table

Refine your project timeline for Week 5–6 (Term 2) and Weeks 1–2 (Term 3) with clear milestones, task ownership, and contingency plans.

Focus on realistic timeframes and identify potential bottlenecks or dependencies between tasks.

Activity 10.3 – Risk & Response Table

Document potential project risks and your team's response strategies. Include at least one extra risk specific to your own project, not just the examples in the table.

Example: “We kept adding new ideas, so our game was getting bigger and harder to finish. → Response plan: focus only on essential features so we can complete the core game on time.”

Scope creep = when the project grows because new ideas keep being added, making it harder to finish on time.

Game developers often  say “games are never finished — just stopped”. It’s a reminder to avoid adding too many new ideas, which leads to scope creep.

Upload a screenshot (or attach a link) for each of your three outputs from today's lesson — Activity 10.1 (Team Roles), Activity 10.2 (Project Timeline), and Activity 10.3 (Risk & Response Table) — to complete your planning portfolio entry.

Portfolio Entry 12 – Team Roles & Planning

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson

Each role writes a useful AI prompt to support the project—Designer, Programmer, Art Lead, Producer.

"Suggest ways to make level goals clearer."

Collaborative Prompt Design

"Generate desert-themed colour palette ideas."

"Explain why my NPC dialogue chain might not trigger."

"Create a two-week timeline with three milestones."

Student Task: Swap prompts and give 1 feedback point. Evidence: Add to Team Charter.

Extension / Adaptive Task – Endstar Warm-Up

Testing the Pipeline One student builds a small scene → another tests and logs bugs. Discussion: how communication affects iteration. Purpose: Apply team coordination skills developed during role planning. Test whether clear communication improves collaborative building and debugging processes.

Portfolio Entry / Next Steps

Upload your artefact evidence and write one sentence: 'Today I demonstrated…'

Prototype V3 Refinement Implementation

Provides visual "before-and-after" evidence of the specific improvements made to your final build.

Prototype V3 Implementation Log

Tracks technical changes and evaluates how AI suggestions impacted the final player experience.

Outlines team roles, project timelines, and risk management plans for a professional delivery.

Next Steps: Cross-Reference Your Work: Ensure your labels in Entry 11a (e.g., "Screenshot A") match the descriptions in your 11b Implementation Log for clear documentation. Evaluate Change Impact: Use the Impact Assessment Guide to critically judge whether your final refinements truly solved the player experience issues you identified earlier. Finalize Team Logistics: Confirm that your team roles, timelines, and risk responses are realistic and agreed upon by all members to ensure a smooth final production.

Which practice BEST reflects responsible use of Chain-of-Thought prompting in design tasks?

A. Accepting explanations as proof

B. Using explanations to replace testing

C. Treating explanations as hypotheses to be evaluated by humans

D. Increasing reasoning length

Lesson 10 – Learning Check

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

OECD Mapping Knowledge Area: Work & Human Roles (K3) Code: K1. 1 Humans remain accountable for interpretation and decisions.

Term 2
Lesson 10

Lesson 10: Team Roles & Milestones — Collaborative Project Planning
Product Development Cycle – Term 2 | Test & Evaluate Phase
Students strengthen professional team collaboration skills
Refine structured project timelines with clear milestones
Implement risk management strategies
UAE Link: This lesson aligns with the UAE’s emphasis on collaboration and leadership by developing teamwork, planning, and project organisation skills. Reflection Question: How do teamwork and planning contribute to successful technology projects?
UAE Example – Smart City Systems In the UAE, smart city platforms such as Abu Dhabi’s TAMM integrate multiple digital and AI systems to deliver government services through a single coordinated experience. This highlights the importance of clear roles, collaboration, and planning when managing complex digital projects. 🎥 Want to learn more on this.

Big Question / Big Picture
How do professional design teams work together to deliver creative projects on time?

Lesson 10: Learning Objectives
By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

Lesson 10: Success Criteria – Team Roles & Milestones
We will know we are successful when we can …

Reflect: My Development Process
Reflect on how your updated team role, responsibilities, and planning for the next milestone will support your project’s success.
“The most important responsibility I clarified for myself today was ___ because ___.” “Strengthening this responsibility will help my team by ___.” “Updating our project timeline showed me that ___.” “The most important risk my team must manage is ___ because ___, and our response plan is to ___.”
Focus on how today’s planning work (roles, timeline, and risks) will help your team stay organised and meet upcoming milestones.

I used AI in this lesson OR chose not to use AI I checked AI output instead of trusting it straight away I used my own judgement to make decisions

What is the primary benefit of this request?
A. It guarantees the answer is correct
B. It reveals intermediate reasoning steps for human evaluation
C. It reduces randomness
D. It improves creativity
Lesson 10 – Learning Check
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
MCQ 10 .1
A team asks an AI to explain how it arrived at a design recommendation.
OECD Mapping Knowledge Area: Nature of AI (K1) Code: K1. 1 AI systems can be configured to respond differently depending on purpose (e.g. explain vs generate)

Why should teams remain cautious even when AI provides a clear step-by-step explanation?
A. Explanations are always incomplete
B. Intermediate reasoning may still be flawed or misleading
C. Step-by-step outputs reduce accuracy
D. AI cannot explain its outputs
Lesson 10 – Learning Check
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
MCQ 10 . 2
OECD Mapping Knowledge Area: Capabilities & Limits (K4) Code: K1. 1 AI lacks judgement and may produce convincing but incorrect reasoning

Which practice BEST reflects responsible use of Chain-of-Thought prompting in design tasks?
A. Accepting explanations as proof
B. Using explanations to replace testing
C. Treating explanations as hypotheses to be evaluated by humans
D. Increasing reasoning length
Lesson 10 – Learning Check
Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy
MCQ 10 . 3
OECD Mapping Knowledge Area: Work & Human Roles (K3) Code: K1. 1 Humans remain accountable for interpretation and decisions

Term 2
Lesson 10

Lesson 10: Team Roles & Milestones — Collaborative Project Planning
Product Development Cycle – Term 2 | Test & Evaluate Phase
Students strengthen professional team collaboration skills
Refine structured project timelines with clear milestones
Implement risk management strategies
UAE Link: This lesson aligns with the UAE’s emphasis on collaboration and leadership by developing teamwork, planning, and project organisation skills. Reflection Question: How do teamwork and planning contribute to successful technology projects?
UAE Example – Smart City Systems In the UAE, smart city platforms such as Abu Dhabi’s TAMM integrate multiple digital and AI systems to deliver government services through a single coordinated experience. This highlights the importance of clear roles, collaboration, and planning when managing complex digital projects. 🎥 Want to learn more on this.

Big Question / Big Picture
How do professional design teams work together to deliver creative projects on time?

Lesson 10: Learning Objectives
By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

Lesson 10: Success Criteria – Team Roles & Milestones
We will know we are successful when we can …

Starter: Think Like a Product Team
Andrew Ng is one of the world’s leading AI experts and product innovators. His point is simple: building a great product isn’t mainly about coding — it’s about making the right decisions at the right time In real game studios, teams spend most of their time: choosing which features matter most defining clear team roles and responsibilities planning timelines, risks, and owners deciding what to improve or fix first Today, your team is doing the same thing — thinking like real product managers as you define roles, strengthen accountability, and plan the next development stage of your game.
👉 This lesson connects modern product-management thinking with game development — helping your team work more clearly, efficiently, and collaboratively.
🎥 Optional: Want to see how a real game studios plan features?
Quick Starter Task (1–2 minutes): Turn to a partner and discuss: Which of these product-team skills does your group already do well? Which one do you need to strengthen today?

Explore: Introducing the Project Manager
From Lesson 10 onward, one team member will take on the Project Manager role to guide the team through production in Term 2 and Term 3.
The Project Manager helps the team: organise tasks and milestones manage the project timeline coordinate responsibilities track progress and risks support communication ensure the game stays on schedule
This role is added on top of each student's main creative role (Designer, Programmer, Art Lead, or Story/Marketing Lead). 👉 Choose the teammate who is best suited to guide the project forward. 👉 Next, you will update your Team Roles Table to include the Project Manager role and clarify everyone’s responsibilities for the upcoming milestones.

Activity 10.1 – Team Roles Table
🟥🟧
Include your new Project Manager and update each teammate’s responsibilities for the next milestone.
Use this table to ensure balanced workload distribution and clear accountability for upcoming milestones.

Analyse: Creating the Project Timeline
Your timeline must cover Week 5 and Week 6 of Term 2, plus the first 2 weeks of Term 3 (Refine stage). • List the key milestones for your game (builds, playtests, polish, hand-in, etc.). • Choose realistic dates for each milestone. • Assign an owner for every milestone. • Note any major risks or dependencies between tasks. You’ll capture this in Activity 10.2 (Project Timeline Table) and Activity 10.3 (Risk & Response Table) .
🎥 Production Management and Kanban Guide

Activity 10.2 – Project Timeline Table
Refine your project timeline for Week 5–6 (Term 2) and Weeks 1–2 (Term 3) with clear milestones, task ownership, and contingency plans.
Focus on realistic timeframes and identify potential bottlenecks or dependencies between tasks.
🟦🟥

Activity 10.3 – Risk & Response Table
Document potential project risks and your team's response strategies. Include at least one extra risk specific to your own project, not just the examples in the table.
Example: “We kept adding new ideas, so our game was getting bigger and harder to finish. → Response plan: focus only on essential features so we can complete the core game on time.”
Scope creep = when the project grows because new ideas keep being added, making it harder to finish on time.
Game developers often  say “games are never finished — just stopped”. It’s a reminder to avoid adding too many new ideas, which leads to scope creep.
🟦🟥

Upload a screenshot (or attach a link) for each of your three outputs from today's lesson — Activity 10.1 (Team Roles), Activity 10.2 (Project Timeline), and Activity 10.3 (Risk & Response Table) — to complete your planning portfolio entry.
Attach link if needed
Portfolio Entry 12 – Team Roles & Planning
Attach link if needed
Attach link if needed
🟦🟥

Reflect: My Development Process
Reflect on how your updated team role, responsibilities, and planning for the next milestone will support your project’s success.
“The most important responsibility I clarified for myself today was ___ because ___.” “Strengthening this responsibility will help my team by ___.” “Updating our project timeline showed me that ___.” “The most important risk my team must manage is ___ because ___, and our response plan is to ___.”
Focus on how today’s planning work (roles, timeline, and risks) will help your team stay organised and meet upcoming milestones.

I used AI in this lesson OR chose not to use AI I checked AI output instead of trusting it straight away I used my own judgement to make decisions

Today AI helped me by…
I still needed to decide…

metadata.activity_description:
Portfolio Entry 13 – Curated Evidence & Captions (Required)

Upload your selected 6-8 artefacts (screenshots/images) from Term 2. Write one clear caption explaining each artefact.

This hand-drawn sketch shows my initial Zone 1 layout with crystal locations and player path, which helped me plan prop placement before building in Endstar.

My first playable prototype with basic crystal collection and a Counter Rule tracking progress - this proved the core mechanic worked before adding complexity.

Added a pressure plate + timed door puzzle using Timer Rule and Mechanical Door, showing how I expanded gameplay beyond simple collection.

Feedback from 4 testers revealed that 3/4 players struggled to find hidden crystals, which became my top priority for V3 improvements.

Early Level Sketch (Zone 1 Layout)

Prototype V1 Screenshot (Basic Mechanics)

Prototype V2 Screenshot (Expanded Mechanics)

Peer Testing Feedback Table (WWW/EBI)

I used this matrix to prioritise changes, choosing high-impact/low-effort fixes like hint dialogues over complex features like automatic timed hints.

Added a Trigger Volume near the hidden crystal that activates Wise Elder dialogue, directly addressing the "can't find crystals" feedback.

Before/After - Hint System Implementation

Optional Stretch Activity – AI Battle Reflection & Documentation

| Question | Your Response |

| Which AI tool did you use for the challenge? (e.g., ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, etc.) | ChatGPT, Claude |

| What prompt did you give the AI to complete the challenge? | "Give me one suggestion to improve my game or portfolio this week. My game is about collecting light crystals in Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque and players said they can't find the hidden crystals." |

| How well did the AI perform? Rate 1-5 and explain. | ChatGPT: 4/5 - gave a good idea but was a bit generic. Claude: 4/5 - more specific to Endstar but longer answer than I needed. |

| What surprised you about the AI's response? | ChatGPT suggested adding a "glowing particle effect" which isn't possible in Endstar. Claude knew about Trigger Volumes and Dialogue Nodes which actually exist in the engine. |

| Compare: Which suggestion was clearer or more helpful? | Claude's was more helpful because it suggested using Trigger Volumes with hint dialogue, which I can actually build. ChatGPT's particle effect idea sounded cool but isn't realistic for Endstar. |

| Compare: Which critique spotted real weaknesses? | Claude spotted that my hint might be "too obvious" and suggested making it poetic instead of direct. ChatGPT just said "make sure it fits the theme" which is vague. |

| Compare: Which AI gave the most useful improvement? | Claude gave more useful improvement because it suggested specific wiring (Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node with OnTriggered → GiveInstruction) that I could copy straight into my game. |

| Declare your winner: AI System 1 or AI System 2 - and explain why | Winner: Claude - It understood what Endstar can actually do and gave me a suggestion I could implement right away. ChatGPT's ideas were creative but not realistic for my platform |

| What weakness did both AIs miss? | Neither AI mentioned that I should test the Trigger Volume size - if it's too small players might walk past it, if it's too big the hint triggers too early. I had to figure that out myself. |

| How might you use AI tools in your game development project? | I'll use AI to help write NPC dialogue that sounds natural, debug wiring problems when props don't connect properly, and get feedback on my portfolio captions. But I'll always check if the suggestions actually work in Endstar before implementing them. |

This activity is optional and not part of the portfolio or assessment.

Document your findings from the AI Battle challenge. Reflect on the AI tools' performance and what you learned.

Portfolio Entry 14A — Evaluation Table

Complete a full evaluation of your progress across each stage of the design cycle.

Rate each stage 1–5 and list which existing portfolio artifacts best support your score (no need to upload again).

| Stage | Rate 1-5 | What I Did Well | What I Need to Improve | Evidence |

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| Empathise | 4 | I really thought about what players my age would want - learning about UAE culture but in a fun way, not boring. I imagined players exploring Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque and discovering hidden gems. | Could have asked more classmates what they actually want in a mosque game before starting. Next time I'll do actual interviews. | Early brainstorm notes, player persona sketches |

| Define | 4 | Created clear game concept - collect 5 hidden crystals while exploring mosque zones. Focused on one main mechanic so it wouldn't get too complicated. | My problem statement was a bit vague at first. Took me time to narrow down exactly what makes it educational AND fun. | Design document, problem statement draft |

| Ideate | 5 | Generated lots of ideas for puzzles - lever + counter, pressure plate + timed door, NPC hints. Drew multiple sketches before deciding. Used paper prototyping to test ideas. | Sometimes I got too excited and wanted to add everything. Need to be better at picking only the best 2-3 ideas. | Paper prototypes, brainstorm sketches, idea maps |

| Prototype | 4 | Built 3 versions! V1 was basic, V2 added puzzles, V3 added hint system with Wise Elder NPC after feedback. Each version got better. Used proper Endstar wiring. | V1 took too long because I was learning Endstar. Should have done simpler test first. Some wiring got messy. | Screenshot of V1, V2, V3 builds in Endstar |

| Test & Evaluate | 4 | Got feedback from Khalid, Fatima, and Layla. Actually listened and changed things - added hint NPC because of Khalid's feedback about confusing crystal locations. Used impact vs effort to prioritize. | Could have tested more often instead of waiting until the end. Should test after every small change. | WWW/EBI feedback forms, Impact vs Effort matrix |

Portfolio Entry 14B — SMART Goals for Term 3

Choose 2–3 areas from the table below, or replace one with your own area, and write a SMART goal for each selected area. Each goal must be one sentence and follow the Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant and Time-bound criteria.

|  | Goal | Steps to Achieve It | Deadline |

| Visual / Atmosphere | I will improve the mosque atmosphere by adding 3 new decorative props and adjusting Ambient Settings lighting in each zone by Week 2 of Term 3. | 1. Browse Endstar extended library for Islamic-style props  2. Place decorations in each zone  3. Use Inspector to adjust Ambient Settings for warmer lighting | Week 2, Term 3 |

| Testing / Feedback | I will test my game with 4 different players and collect WWW/EBI feedback after each major change before the final pitch. | 1. Create simple feedback form  2. Schedule one test per week with classmates  3. Record feedback and pick one change per session | Week 4, Term 3 |

| Technical / Mechanics | I will add one new puzzle using a Counter Rule and 3 Levers where players must pull all levers to open a locked door in Zone 3 by Week 3 of Term 3. | 1. Paper prototype the lever puzzle layout first  2. Place 3 Levers and wire each OnFlipped→ Counter Rule ChangeCount  3. Wire Counter OnCountReached → Door Open 4. Test with 2 players to check if it's clear | Week 3, Term 3 |

Footer note: These goals will form your starting point for Term 3's Refine, Market & Pitch phase.

Portfolio Entry 15 – Term 2 Final Prototype

Describe in one sentence: What improved the most in this version, and why does it matter?

Upload Area — Add your final prototype screenshots or gameplay clip.

(This is your final Term 2 build. It will be used as evidence for your end-of-term assessment and as your starting point for refinement in Term 3.)

[Screenshot 1] | Zone 1 - Entrance Courtyard: Starting area with first crystal visible, Wise Elder NPC near archway gives hint dialogue when player approaches via Trigger Volume

[Screenshot 2] | Zone 2 - Prayer Hall: Pressure plate puzzle - step on plate to start Timer Rule, reach door before it closes. Mosque Guide NPC explains the mechanic

[Screenshot 3]  Zone 3 - Minaret Area: Lever + Counter puzzle - pull 2 levers (OnPulled → ModifyCount) to unlock mechanical door hiding final crystal

[Screenshot 4] Win Condition: All 5 crystals collected (Counter Rule OnCountReached) triggers victory message on Anachronist Billboard

The hint system with the Wise Elder NPC improved the most because playtesters were getting stuck on crystal locations, and now the Trigger Volume + Dialogue Node gives guidance without giving away the answer, which keeps the exploration fun but not frustrating.

Analyse: Curation & Captioning

Teacher Model: “This test showed me players needed clearer instructions.” “This sketch helped me refine the final environment layout.” AI Helper:

"Summarise this design decision in one clear sentence (max 20 words)."

Task: Select your best 6–8 artefacts from your game development work so far (sketches, prototypes, AI outputs, test results, gameplay screenshots). Write a 1-sentence caption explaining what each artefact shows.

Portfolio Entry 13 – Curated Evidence & Captions (Required)

Upload your selected 6-8 artefacts (screenshots/images) from Term 2. Write one clear caption explaining each artefact.

Write one clear caption here…

Write one clear caption here…

Write one clear caption here…

Write one clear caption here…

Portfolio Entry 13 – Curated Evidence & Captions ( Continued)

Write one clear caption here…

Write one clear caption here…

Write one clear caption here…

Write one clear caption here…

Stretch Activity - AI Battle

Put two AI systems head-to-head and see which one gives better ideas.

Round 1 — Ask both AIs the same question Ask AI System 1: "Give me one suggestion to improve my game or portfolio this week." Ask AI System 2 the same question. Now you have two competing suggestions.

Round 2 — Make them evaluate each other Copy AI System 1's answer into AI System 2 and ask: "Evaluate this suggestion. What is strong, weak, or missing? What would you change?" Copy AI System 2's answer into AI System 1 and ask: "Evaluate this suggestion. What is strong, weak, or missing? What would you change?" Now you have two critiques.

Round 3 — Decide the winner of the AI Battle Compare: Which suggestion was clearer or more helpful? Which critique spotted real weaknesses? Which AI gave the most useful improvement? Declare your winner: AI System 1 or AI System 2.

You may write: The suggestion you preferred A weakness each AI missed Which critique was stronger Your overall winner

Stretch Activity – AI Battle Reflection & Documentation

Document your findings from the AI Battle challenge. Reflect on the AI tools' performance and what you learned.

Extension / Adaptive Task – AI Mini-Lesson

"Write a 60-word reflection explaining how I used AI this term (research, ideas, debugging, testing). Mention one AI tool and one limitation."

Portfolio Evidence: Short paragraph + one screenshot of an AI output.

One screenshot of an AI output here

Extension / Adaptive Task – Endstar Warm-Up

Extra practice time for students who want to refine mechanics, test small changes, or explore additional tools in Endstar. This warm-up is optional and not part of the assessed core content.

Why is Tree-of-Thought often more effective than Chain-of-Thought for open-ended design problems?

A. It produces shorter outputs

B. It explores and evaluates multiple solution paths

C. It removes bias automatically

D. It increases creativity without constraints

Lesson 11 – Learning Check

Answer these questions to check your understanding of AI Literacy

OECD Mapping Knowledge Area: Nature of AI (K1) Code: K1. 4 AI systems can be used differently depending on task objectives

What human skill is being most directly exercised?

C. Design judgement and evaluation

A team compares several AI-generated solutions before choosing one.

OECD Mapping Knowledge Area: Work & Human Roles (K3) Code: K 3 . 2 Humans determine which tasks require human expertise versus automation

Used proper Endstar wiring. | V1 took too long because I was learning Endstar. Should have done simpler test first. Some wiring got messy. | Screenshot of V1, V2, V3 builds in Endstar |

| Test & Evaluate | 4 | Got feedback from Khalid, Fatima, and Layla. Actually listened and changed things - added hint NPC because of Khalid's feedback about confusing crystal locations. Used impact vs effort to prioritize. | Could have tested more often instead of waiting until the end. Should test after every small change. | WWW/EBI feedback forms, Impact vs Effort matrix |

Portfolio Entry 14B — SMART Goals for Term 3

Choose 2–3 areas from the table below, or replace one with your own area, and write a SMART goal for each selected area. Each goal must be one sentence and follow the Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant and Time-bound criteria.

|  | Goal | Steps to Achieve It | Deadline |

| Visual / Atmosphere | I will improve the mosque atmosphere by adding 3 new decorative props and adjusting Ambient Settings lighting in each zone by Week 2 of Term 3. | 1. Browse Endstar extended library for Islamic-style props  2. Place decorations in each zone  3. Use Inspector to adjust Ambient Settings for warmer lighting | Week 2, Term 3 |

| Testing / Feedback | I will test my game with 4 different players and collect WWW/EBI feedback after each major change before the final pitch. | 1. Create simple feedback form  2. Schedule one test per week with classmates  3. Record feedback and pick one change per session | Week 4, Term 3 |

| Technical / Mechanics | I will add one new puzzle using a Counter Rule and 3 Levers where players must pull all levers to open a locked door in Zone 3 by Week 3 of Term 3. | 1. Paper prototype the lever puzzle layout first  2. Place 3 Levers and wire each OnFlipped→ Counter Rule ChangeCount  3. Wire Counter OnCountReached → Door Open 4. Test with 2 players to check if it's clear | Week 3, Term 3 |

Footer note: These goals will form your starting point for Term 3's Refine, Market & Pitch phase.

[Screenshot 1] | Zone 1 - Entrance Courtyard: Starting area with first crystal visible, Wise Elder NPC near archway gives hint dialogue when player approaches via Trigger Volume

[Screenshot 2] | Zone 2 - Prayer Hall: Pressure plate puzzle - step on plate to start Timer Rule, reach door before it closes. Mosque Guide NPC explains the mechanic

[Screenshot 3]  Zone 3 - Minaret Area: Lever + Counter puzzle - pull 2 levers (OnPulled → ModifyCount) to unlock mechanical door hiding final crystal

[Screenshot 4] Win Condition: All 5 crystals collected (Counter Rule OnCountReached) triggers victory message on Anachronist Billboard

The hint system with the Wise Elder NPC improved the most because playtesters were getting stuck on crystal locations, and now the Trigger Volume + Dialogue Node gives guidance without giving away the answer, which keeps the exploration fun but not frustrating.

curriculum_alignment.text:
CSTA Computer Science Standards (US)
3A-AP-17 – Decompose problems and define requirements prior to developing solutions.
Students organise evidence that shows how design requirements evolved across the development cycle.

UK Computer Science
Evaluation and communication of digital development processes.
Students document and explain how their digital solution was developed, tested, and refined.

UK Design & Technology
Evaluating and communicating design processes and outcomes.
Students curate and communicate evidence demonstrating design decisions and iteration.

IB Design
Criterion D – Evaluating.
Students evaluate and communicate their design journey using structured evidence.

lessons.detailed_activities:
Portfolio Entry 13 – Curated Evidence & Captions (Required)

Upload your selected 6-8 artefacts (screenshots/images) from Term 2. Write one clear caption explaining each artefact.

This hand-drawn sketch shows my initial Zone 1 layout with crystal locations and player path, which helped me plan prop placement before building in Endstar.

My first playable prototype with basic crystal collection and a Counter Rule tracking progress - this proved the core mechanic worked before adding complexity.

Added a pressure plate + timed door puzzle using Timer Rule and Mechanical Door, showing how I expanded gameplay beyond simple collection.

Feedback from 4 testers revealed that 3/4 players struggled to find hidden crystals, which became my top priority for V3 improvements.

Early Level Sketch (Zone 1 Layout)

Prototype V1 Screenshot (Basic Mechanics)

Prototype V2 Screenshot (Expanded Mechanics)

Peer Testing Feedback Table (WWW/EBI)

I used this matrix to prioritise changes, choosing high-impact/low-effort fixes like hint dialogues over complex features like automatic timed hints.

Added a Trigger Volume near the hidden crystal that activates Wise Elder dialogue, directly addressing the "can't find crystals" feedback.

Before/After - Hint System Implementation

Optional Stretch Activity – AI Battle Reflection & Documentation

| Question | Your Response |

| Which AI tool did you use for the challenge? (e.g., ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, etc.) | ChatGPT, Claude |

| What prompt did you give the AI to complete the challenge? | "Give me one suggestion to improve my game or portfolio this week. My game is about collecting light crystals in Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque and players said they can't find the hidden crystals." |

| What surprised you about the AI's response? | ChatGPT suggested adding a "glowing particle effect" which isn't possible in Endstar. Claude knew about Trigger Volumes and Dialogue Nodes which actually exist in the engine. |

| Compare: Which suggestion was clearer or more helpful? | Claude's was more helpful because it suggested using Trigger Volumes with hint dialogue, which I can actually build. ChatGPT's particle effect idea sounded cool but isn't realistic for Endstar. |

| Compare: Which critique spotted real weaknesses? | Claude spotted that my hint might be "too obvious" and suggested making it poetic instead of direct. ChatGPT just said "make sure it fits the theme" which is vague. |

| Compare: Which AI gave the most useful improvement? | Claude gave more useful improvement because it suggested specific wiring (Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node with OnTriggered → GiveInstruction) that I could copy straight into my game. |

| Declare your winner: AI System 1 or AI System 2 - and explain why | Winner: Claude - It understood what Endstar can actually do and gave me a suggestion I could implement right away. ChatGPT's ideas were creative but not realistic for my platform |

| What weakness did both AIs miss? | Neither AI mentioned that I should test the Trigger Volume size - if it's too small players might walk past it, if it's too big the hint triggers too early. I had to figure that out myself. |

| How might you use AI tools in your game development project? | I'll use AI to help write NPC dialogue that sounds natural, debug wiring problems when props don't connect properly, and get feedback on my portfolio captions. But I'll always check if the suggestions actually work in Endstar before implementing them. |

rows: Which AI tool did you use for the challenge? (e.g., ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, etc.)
ChatGPT, Claude
What prompt did you give the AI to complete the challenge?
"Give me one suggestion to improve my game or portfolio this week. My game is about collecting light crystals in Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque and players said they can't find the hidden crystals."
How well did the AI perform? Rate 1-5 and explain.
ChatGPT: 4/5 - gave a good idea but was a bit generic. Claude: 4/5 - more specific to Endstar but longer answer than I needed.
What surprised you about the AI's response?
ChatGPT suggested adding a "glowing particle effect" which isn't possible in Endstar. Claude knew about Trigger Volumes and Dialogue Nodes which actually exist in the engine.
Compare: Which suggestion was clearer or more helpful?
Claude's was more helpful because it suggested using Trigger Volumes with hint dialogue, which I can actually build. ChatGPT's particle effect idea sounded cool but isn't realistic for Endstar.
Compare: Which critique spotted real weaknesses?
Claude spotted that my hint might be "too obvious" and suggested making it poetic instead of direct. ChatGPT just said "make sure it fits the theme" which is vague.
Compare: Which AI gave the most useful improvement?
Claude gave more useful improvement because it suggested specific wiring (Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node with OnTriggered → GiveInstruction) that I could copy straight into my game.
Declare your winner: AI System 1 or AI System 2 - and explain why
Winner: Claude - It understood what Endstar can actually do and gave me a suggestion I could implement right away. ChatGPT's ideas were creative but not realistic for my platform
What weakness did both AIs miss?
Neither AI mentioned that I should test the Trigger Volume size - if it's too small players might walk past it, if it's too big the hint triggers too early. I had to figure that out myself.
How might you use AI tools in your game development project?

I'll use AI to help write NPC dialogue that sounds natural, debug wiring problems when props don't connect properly, and get feedback on my portfolio captions. But I'll always check if the suggestions actually work in Endstar before implementing them.
Empathise
I really thought about what players my age would want - learning about UAE culture but in a fun way, not boring. I imagined players exploring Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque and discovering hidden gems.
Could have asked more classmates what they actually want in a mosque game before starting. Next time I'll do actual interviews.
Early brainstorm notes, player persona sketches

Created clear game concept - collect 5 hidden crystals while exploring mosque zones. Focused on one main mechanic so it wouldn't get too complicated.
My problem statement was a bit vague at first. Took me time to narrow down exactly what makes it educational AND fun.
Design document, problem statement draft
Ideate
Generated lots of ideas for puzzles - lever + counter, pressure plate + timed door, NPC hints. Drew multiple sketches before deciding. Used paper prototyping to test ideas.
Sometimes I got too excited and wanted to add everything. Need to be better at picking only the best 2-3 ideas.
Paper prototypes, brainstorm sketches, idea maps
Prototype
Built 3 versions! V1 was basic, V2 added puzzles, V3 added hint system with Wise Elder NPC after feedback. Each version got better. Used proper Endstar wiring.
V1 took too long because I was learning Endstar. Should have done simpler test first. Some wiring got messy.
Screenshot of V1, V2, V3 builds in Endstar
Test & Evaluate
Got feedback from Khalid, Fatima, and Layla. Actually listened and changed things - added hint NPC because of Khalid's feedback about confusing crystal locations. Used impact vs effort to prioritize.
Could have tested more often instead of waiting until the end. Should test after every small change.

Optional Stretch Activity – AI Battle Reflection & Documentation

10 minutes

‹#›


| Question | Your Response |
| --- | --- |
| Which AI tool did you use for the challenge? (e.g., ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, etc.) | ChatGPT, Claude |
| What prompt did you give the AI to complete the challenge? | "Give me one suggestion to improve my game or portfolio this week. My game is about collecting light crystals in Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque and players said they can't find the hidden crystals." |
| How well did the AI perform? Rate 1-5 and explain. | ChatGPT: 4/5 - gave a good idea but was a bit generic. Claude: 4/5 - more specific to Endstar but longer answer than I needed. |
| What surprised you about the AI's response? | ChatGPT suggested adding a "glowing particle effect" which isn't possible in Endstar. Claude knew about Trigger Volumes and Dialogue Nodes which actually exist in the engine. |
| Compare: Which suggestion was clearer or more helpful? | Claude's was more helpful because it suggested using Trigger Volumes with hint dialogue, which I can actually build. ChatGPT's particle effect idea sounded cool but isn't realistic for Endstar. |
| Compare: Which critique spotted real weaknesses? | Claude spotted that my hint might be "too obvious" and suggested making it poetic instead of direct. ChatGPT just said "make sure it fits the theme" which is vague. |
| Compare: Which AI gave the most useful improvement? | Claude gave more useful improvement because it suggested specific wiring (Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node with OnTriggered → GiveInstruction) that I could copy straight into my game. |
| Declare your winner: AI System 1 or AI System 2 - and explain why | Winner: Claude - It understood what Endstar can actually do and gave me a suggestion I could implement right away. ChatGPT's ideas were creative but not realistic for my platform |
| What weakness did both AIs miss? | Neither AI mentioned that I should test the Trigger Volume size - if it's too small players might walk past it, if it's too big the hint triggers too early. I had to figure that out myself. |
| How might you use AI tools in your game development project? | I'll use AI to help write NPC dialogue that sounds natural, debug wiring problems when props don't connect properly, and get feedback on my portfolio captions. But I'll always check if the suggestions actually work in Endstar before implementing them. |

This activity is optional and not part of the portfolio or assessment.

| Ideate | 5 | Generated lots of ideas for puzzles - lever + counter, pressure plate + timed door, NPC hints. Drew multiple sketches before deciding. Used paper prototyping to test ideas. | Sometimes I got too excited and wanted to add everything. Need to be better at picking only the best 2-3 ideas. | Paper prototypes, brainstorm sketches, idea maps |
| Prototype | 4 | Built 3 versions! V1 was basic, V2 added puzzles, V3 added hint system with Wise Elder NPC after feedback. Each version got better. Used proper Endstar wiring. | V1 took too long because I was learning Endstar. Should have done simpler test first. Some wiring got messy. | Screenshot of V1, V2, V3 builds in Endstar |
| Test & Evaluate | 4 | Got feedback from Khalid, Fatima, and Layla. Actually listened and changed things - added hint NPC because of Khalid's feedback about confusing crystal locations. "Give me one suggestion to improve my game or portfolio this week. My game is about collecting light crystals in Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque and players said they can't find the hidden crystals."
How well did the AI perform? Rate 1-5 and explain.
ChatGPT: 4/5 - gave a good idea but was a bit generic. Claude: 4/5 - more specific to Endstar but longer answer than I needed.
What surprised you about the AI's response?
ChatGPT suggested adding a "glowing particle effect" which isn't possible in Endstar. Claude knew about Trigger Volumes and Dialogue Nodes which actually exist in the engine.
Compare: Which suggestion was clearer or more helpful?
Claude's was more helpful because it suggested using Trigger Volumes with hint dialogue, which I can actually build. ChatGPT's particle effect idea sounded cool but isn't realistic for Endstar.
Compare: Which critique spotted real weaknesses?
Claude spotted that my hint might be "too obvious" and suggested making it poetic instead of direct. ChatGPT just said "make sure it fits the theme" which is vague.
Compare: Which AI gave the most useful improvement?
Claude gave more useful improvement because it suggested specific wiring (Trigger Volume → Dialogue Node with OnTriggered → GiveInstruction) that I could copy straight into my game.
Declare your winner: AI System 1 or AI System 2 - and explain why
Winner: Claude - It understood what Endstar can actually do and gave me a suggestion I could implement right away. ChatGPT's ideas were creative but not realistic for my platform
What weakness did both AIs miss?
Neither AI mentioned that I should test the Trigger Volume size - if it's too small players might walk past it, if it's too big the hint triggers too early. I had to figure that out myself.
How might you use AI tools in your game development project?

I'll use AI to help write NPC dialogue that sounds natural, debug wiring problems when props don't connect properly, and get feedback on my portfolio captions. But I'll always check if the suggestions actually work in Endstar before implementing them.
Empathise
I really thought about what players my age would want - learning about UAE culture but in a fun way, not boring. I imagined players exploring Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque and discovering hidden gems.
Could have asked more classmates what they actually want in a mosque game before starting. Next time I'll do actual interviews.
Early brainstorm notes, player persona sketches
Define
Created clear game concept - collect 5 hidden crystals while exploring mosque zones. Focused on one main mechanic so it wouldn't get too complicated.
My problem statement was a bit vague at first. Took me time to narrow down exactly what makes it educational AND fun.
Design document, problem statement draft
Ideate
Generated lots of ideas for puzzles - lever + counter, pressure plate + timed door, NPC hints. Drew multiple sketches before deciding. Used paper prototyping to test ideas.
Sometimes I got too excited and wanted to add everything. Need to be better at picking only the best 2-3 ideas.
Paper prototypes, brainstorm sketches, idea maps
Prototype
Built 3 versions! V1 was basic, V2 added puzzles, V3 added hint system with Wise Elder NPC after feedback. Each version got better. Used proper Endstar wiring.
V1 took too long because I was learning Endstar. Should have done simpler test first. Some wiring got messy.
Screenshot of V1, V2, V3 builds in Endstar
Test & Evaluate
Got feedback from Khalid, Fatima, and Layla. Actually listened and changed things - added hint NPC because of Khalid's feedback about confusing crystal locations. Used impact vs effort to prioritize.
Could have tested more often instead of waiting until the end. Should test after every small change.
WWW/EBI feedback forms, Impact vs Effort matrix
Visual / Atmosphere
I will improve the mosque atmosphere by adding 3 new decorative props and adjusting Ambient Settings lighting in each zone by Week 2 of Term 3.

1. Browse Endstar extended library for Islamic-style props 
2. Place decorations in each zone 
3. Use Inspector to adjust Ambient Settings for warmer lighting
Week 2, Term 3
Testing / Feedback
I will test my game with 4 different players and collect WWW/EBI feedback after each major change before the final pitch.

1. Create simple feedback form 
2. Schedule one test per week with classmates 
3. Record feedback and pick one change per session
Week 4, Term 3
Technical / Mechanics
I will add one new puzzle using a Counter Rule and 3 Levers where players must pull all levers to open a locked door in Zone 3 by Week 3 of Term 3.

1. Paper prototype the lever puzzle layout first 
2. Place 3 Levers and wire each OnFlipped→ Counter Rule ChangeCount 
3. Wire Counter OnCountReached → Door Open 4. Test with 2 players to check if it's clear
Week 3, Term 3

Code: K1.2 AI operates without intent or understanding and requires human oversights

Stretch Activity: Introduction to NotebookLM

NotebookLM — Your AI Learning Assistant

Today you will try NotebookLM for the first time.

NotebookLM is a tool that turns your notes, screenshots, and text into:

Later in Term 3, you will explore ALL of these features.

But today, you will use NotebookLM to auto-generate a narrated video of your Term 2 learning.

⭐ Potential Beyond Game Design: You can use NotebookLM for any school subject or course. Upload your Chemistry, English, or Maths notes and it can create study guides, cue cards, podcasts, or audio lessons to help you revise for exams. Where do you think this tool could help you the most?

Stretch Task: Create Your NotebookLM Narrated Video

Step-by-Step: Make Your AI-Generated Narrated Video

Go to notebooklm.google.com → click New Notebook.

Open NotebookLM Studio → select Create Video.

"Create a 45–60 second narrated video explaining my Term 2 game design journey: what I built, what changed, and what I will refine in Term 3."

Keep it simple — NotebookLM will handle most of the heavy lifting.”

NotebookLM will automatically:

Click Generate → Export Video or Copy Share Link.

Stretch Task: Upload Your NotebookLM Video

Portfolio Evidence — NotebookLM Narrated Video

Paste your NotebookLM video link here:[ Link Box Placeholder ]

If you want to include it, add your narrated video link to your Term 2 portfolio.

Next term, you will explore NotebookLM much more deeply.You will use it to create:

Feel free to explore and experiment — NotebookLM has many possibilities.

Students evaluate progress against defined requirements and establish new goals for future development.

UK Computer Science
Evaluation and planning for improvement of digital artefacts.
Students reflect on their work and plan structured next steps based on evidence.

UK Design & Technology
Evaluating design ideas and outcomes.
Students assess strengths and weaknesses of their design work and plan future improvements.

IB Design
Criterion D – Evaluating.
Students evaluate their design journey and set informed goals for future development.

lessons.detailed_activities:
Show an early version of your prototype beside your final Term 2 version.

What changed? What improved? What stayed the same?

What does this show about how your design skills grew this term?

What improvement are you most proud of?

Which design decision made the biggest impact on gameplay?

Turn to a partner and share one improvement you made from your early prototype to your final build. Why was this improvement important?

Explore: Evaluating Our Design Journey

Students assess their progress across each stage of the Product Development Cycle. Rate each stage 1–5 and justify your rating with specific examples.

How well did I understand my users?

How clearly did I frame the problem?

How creative and diverse were my solutions?

How effectively did I build and test?

How well did I gather and use feedback?

“I will share my ratings and short notes. Tell me which pieces of my portfolio (screenshots, prototypes, test notes) best match each stage. Do not write the evaluation — only suggest which evidence fits where.”

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| Test & Evaluate |  |  |  |  |

slides.content:
‹#›

Term 2

Lesson 12

Lesson 12: Reflection & Next Steps — From Evaluation to Vision

‹#›

End of Term 2 Checkpoint (Not Final Project Reflection) This lesson focuses on evaluating your Term 2 progress and preparing clear, achievable goals for Term 3.

Product Development Cycle: Term 2 → Test & Evaluate (Iteration & Improvement)

In this stage, students reflect on their growth, review their current prototype, and set realistic improvement goals for Term 3 (Refine & Pitch phase).

UAE Link: This lesson supports reflective practice by evaluating quality, impact, and next steps—habits linked to improvement and future readiness in the UAE.
Reflection Question: What is your strongest outcome from this project, and what would you improve next?

Big Question / Big Picture

‹#›

How does reflection help us grow as designers and prepare for our next challenge?

Lesson 12: Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:

‹#›

Design Evaluation
Evaluate your Term 2 progress across the design cycle.

SMART Goal Setting
Set clear, achievable SMART goals for Term 3 (Refine & Pitch).

Lesson 12: Success Criteria – Reflection & Next Steps

We will know we are successful when we can …

‹#›

Aligned to Product Development Cycle – Test & Evaluate → Transition to Refine & Pitch.

Evaluation of Progress
I evaluated my strengths and challenges from Term 2.

SMART Goal Setting
I created clear, achievable SMART targets for Term 3.

Starter: Before & After

‹#›

Show an early version of your prototype beside your final Term 2 version.
What changed? What improved? What stayed the same?
What does this show about how your design skills grew this term?

Discussion Questions
What improvement are you most proud of?
Which design decision made the biggest impact on gameplay?

Connection Task
Turn to a partner and share one improvement you made from your early prototype to your final build. Why was this improvement important?

Explore: Evaluating Our Design Journey

‹#›

Students assess their progress across each stage of the Product Development Cycle. Rate each stage 1–5 and justify your rating with specific examples.

Empathise
How well did I understand my users?

1

Define
How clearly did I frame the problem?

2

Ideate
How creative and diverse were my solutions?

3

Prototype
How effectively did I build and test?

4

Test & Evaluate
How well did I gather and use feedback?

5

AI Prompt (Manage AI):
“I will share my ratings and short notes. Tell me which pieces of my portfolio (screenshots, prototypes, test notes) best match each stage. Do not write the evaluation — only suggest which evidence fits where.”

Portfolio Entry 14A — Evaluation Table

‹#›

🟧🟥

Complete a full evaluation of your progress across each stage of the design cycle.
Rate each stage 1–5 and list which existing portfolio artifacts best support your score (no need to upload again).

Rate each area 1–5.


| Stage | Rate 1-5 | What I Did Well | What I Need to Improve | Evidence |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Empathise |  |  |  |  |
| Define |  |  |  |  |
| Ideate |  |  |  |  |
| Prototype |  |  |  |  |
| Test & Evaluate |  |  |  |  |

Analyse: Goal Setting for Term 3

‹#›

These goals will guide the first two weeks of Term 3 (Refine & Pitch).
Set SMART goals: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.

Model Examples:
Vague goal: "I will improve lighting"
Strong goal: "I will improve lighting by creating a small practice level by Week 2 of Term 3."

Focus Areas for Term 3:
Refining visual polish and atmosphere
Enhancing user testing and feedback integration
Developing presentation and communication skills
Strengthening technical implementation

You will write your final SMART goals in your Portfolio Entry on the next slide.

Portfolio Entry 14B — SMART Goals for Term 3

‹#›

Choose 2–3 areas from the table below, or replace one with your own area, and write a SMART goal for each selected area. Each goal must be one sentence and follow the Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant and Time-bound criteria.


|  | Goal | Steps to Achieve It | Deadline |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Visual / Atmosphere |  |  |  |
| Testing / Feedback |  |  |  |
| Technical / Mechanics |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |

Footer note: These goals will form your starting point for Term 3's Refine, Market & Pitch phase.

🟦🟥

Preparing for Term 3 – Refine, Market & Pitch

‹#›

Next term you will refine your game, create a marketing poster, produce a short video trailer, and prepare your final pitch presentation.
In Term 3, you will move from a prototype to a fully presented and published game.This includes five major steps:

Refine Your Game
Improve mechanics, visuals, and user experience
Use peer and AI feedback to polish your prototype
Create a Marketing Campaign
Design posters, banners, and social media content
Use design and AI tools to help communicate your concept clearly
Make a Video Trailer
Create a short promotional trailer
Highlight your gameplay, story, and main hook
Pitch Your Game
Build a professional pitch deck
Present your complete design journey to an audience
Explain how AI supported your development
Publish to the Endstar Hub
Upload your game for real players
Review feedback and analytics from the community

Portfolio Entry 15 – Term 2 Final Prototype

‹#›

🟧🟩

Describe in one sentence: What improved the most in this version, and why does it matter?

(This is your final Term 2 build. It will be used as evidence for your end-of-term assessment and as your starting point for refinement in Term 3.)

Term 2
Lesson 12

Lesson 12: Reflection & Next Steps — From Evaluation to Vision
End of Term 2 Checkpoint (Not Final Project Reflection) This lesson focuses on evaluating your Term 2 progress and preparing clear, achievable goals for Term 3. Product Development Cycle: Term 2 → Test & Evaluate (Iteration & Improvement) In this stage, students reflect on their growth, review their current prototype, and set realistic improvement goals for Term 3 (Refine & Pitch phase) .
UAE Link: This lesson supports reflective practice by evaluating quality, impact, and next steps—habits linked to improvement and future readiness in the UAE. Reflection Question: What is your strongest outcome from this project, and what would you improve next?

Big Question / Big Picture
How does reflection help us grow as designers and prepare for our next challenge?

Lesson 12: Learning Objectives
By the end of this lesson, you will be able to:
Design Evaluation Evaluate your Term 2 progress across the design cycle.
SMART Goal Setting Set clear, achievable SMART goals for Term 3 (Refine & Pitch).

Lesson 12: Success Criteria – Reflection & Next Steps
We will know we are successful when we can …
Aligned to Product Development Cycle – Test & Evaluate → Transition to Refine & Pitch.
Evaluation of Progress I evaluated my strengths and challenges from Term 2.
SMART Goal Setting I created clear, achievable SMART targets for Term 3.

Starter: Before & After
Show an early version of your prototype beside your final Term 2 version. What changed? What improved? What stayed the same? What does this show about how your design skills grew this term?
Discussion Questions What improvement are you most proud of? Which design decision made the biggest impact on gameplay? Connection Task Turn to a partner and share one improvement you made from your early prototype to your final build. Why was this improvement important?

Explore: Evaluating Our Design Journey
Students assess their progress across each stage of the Product Development Cycle. Rate each stage 1–5 and justify your rating with specific examples.
Empathise How well did I understand my users?
1
Define How clearly did I frame the problem?
2
Ideate How creative and diverse were my solutions?
3
Prototype How effectively did I build and test?
4
Test & Evaluate How well did I gather and use feedback?
5
AI Prompt (Manage AI): “I will share my ratings and short notes. Tell me which pieces of my portfolio (screenshots, prototypes, test notes) best match each stage. Do not write the evaluation — only suggest which evidence fits where.”

Portfolio Entry 14A — Evaluation Table
🟧🟥
Complete a full evaluation of your progress across each stage of the design cycle. Rate each stage 1–5 and list which existing portfolio artifacts best support your score (no need to upload again).
Rate each area 1–5.
native_slides.speaker_notes: “Use bullet points or short notes.”

native_slides.content:
Analyse: Goal Setting for Term 3
These goals will guide the first two weeks of Term 3 (Refine & Pitch). Set SMART goals: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
Model Examples: Vague goal: "I will improve lighting" Strong goal: "I will improve lighting by creating a small practice level by Week 2 of Term 3."
Focus Areas for Term 3: Refining visual polish and atmosphere Enhancing user testing and feedback integration Developing presentation and communication skills Strengthening technical implementation
You will write your final SMART goals in your Portfolio Entry on the next slide.

Portfolio Entry 14B — SMART Goals for Term 3
Choose 2–3 areas from the table below, or replace one with your own area, and write a SMART goal for each selected area. Each goal must be one sentence and follow the Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant and Time-bound criteria.
Footer note: These goals will form your starting point for Term 3's Refine, Market & Pitch phase.
🟦🟥

Preparing for Term 3 – Refine, Market & Pitch
Next term you will refine your game, create a marketing poster, produce a short video trailer, and prepare your final pitch presentation. In Term 3, you will move from a prototype to a fully presented and published game.This includes five major steps:
Refine Your Game Improve mechanics, visuals, and user experience Use peer and AI feedback to polish your prototype Create a Marketing Campaign Design posters, banners, and social media content Use design and AI tools to help communicate your concept clearly Make a Video Trailer Create a short promotional trailer Highlight your gameplay, story, and main hook Pitch Your Game Build a professional pitch deck Present your complete design journey to an audience Explain how AI supported your development Publish to the Endstar Hub Upload your game for real players Review feedback and analytics from the community

Keep this checklist open while preparing your portfolio. Tick each item once the corresponding entry is complete and uploaded.

Stretch Activity: Introduction to NotebookLM
NotebookLM — Your AI Learning Assistant Today you will try NotebookLM for the first time.
NotebookLM is a tool that turns your notes, screenshots, and text into: narrated videos audio explanations summaries cue cards revision guides pitch scripts
Later in Term 3, you will explore ALL of these features. But today, you will use NotebookLM to auto-generate a narrated video of your Term 2 learning.
⭐ Potential Beyond Game Design: You can use NotebookLM for any school subject or course. Upload your Chemistry, English, or Maths notes and it can create study guides, cue cards, podcasts, or audio lessons to help you revise for exams. Where do you think this tool could help you the most?
🟦🟥

Stretch Task: Create Your NotebookLM Narrated Video
Step-by-Step: Make Your AI-Generated Narrated Video
Open NotebookLM Go to notebooklm.google.com → click New Notebook.
1
Upload 2–3 pieces of work Examples: prototype screenshot reflection paragraph persona/empathy map captioned evidence
2
Generate the Video Open NotebookLM Studio → select Create Video. Prompt to use: "Create a 45–60 second narrated video explaining my Term 2 game design journey: what I built, what changed, and what I will refine in Term 3." Keep it simple — NotebookLM will handle most of the heavy lifting.” NotebookLM will automatically: analyse your work write the script illustrate the visuals narrate the video
3
Export Click Generate → Export Video or Copy Share Link.
4
🟦🟥

Reflect: Design Journey Summary
Take a moment to reflect on your learning and growth during Term 2. Use the questions below to guide a short written reflection.
1. What was your strongest skill this term? (Example: problem-solving, testing, creativity, teamwork, UI design, etc.) 2. What will you improve in Term 3? (Example: adding polish, improving mechanics, testing more often, clearer planning.)
My Reflection:
Write 2–3 sentences focusing only on your personal growth this term. This reflection helps you summarise your learning before completing your final portfolio entries.
I used AI in this lesson OR chose not to use AI I checked AI output instead of trusting it straight away I used my own judgement to make decisions

I still needed to decide…
